From 7 to 9 p.m., Sustain Penn State will be hosting a free virtual screening of the award-winning documentary “Sally” as part of its Intersections Film Series.

The film dives into topics like sustainability, equality and social change through the life of American astronaut Sally Ride.

Registration for this event is required.

West Coast Swing Dance Lesson and Social Dance

At The Press: Cider & Craft Beverage Taphouse from 7 to 9 p.m., community members can stop by to learn the west coast swing dance, hosted by the Swing Dance Club. There will be two 30-minute lessons for both intermediate dancers and beginners.

The intermediate lessons will start at 7 p.m., and the beginner lessons will start at 7:30 p.m. The last hour of the event will be social dancing.

Anyone is invited to come, as no experience is needed, and can arrive solo or with a partner. Registration for this event is required.

Thursday, April 9

Build Your Career Skills

At 11 a.m. to noon at the Bank of America Career Services Building, the Career Services Team will be leading a workshop to help students learn how to market themselves.

During the workshop, the team will go over what employers are looking for, how to stand out and how to match one’s academic, professional and leadership experiences to the current job market.

Registration for this event is not required.

Friday, April 10

Penn State vs. Northeastern Softball

At 5 p.m., the Penn State softball team will take on Northeastern at the Nittany Lion Softball Field & Beard Field.

All home women’s softball games are free to both students and the public.

Happy Valley Improv Comedy Show

From 7 to 8:30 p.m., Happy Valley Improv will be hosting its weekly Friday night improv comedy show at the Blue Brick Theatre. The show will feature 3 long-form improv sets with a break in between each set.

The price to attend this event is $13.70.

Saturday, April 11

Stone Valley Lake Days

From 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., Nature and Art Rx and Campus Recreation will be spending the day at Stone Valley Recreation Area.

Participants will have the chance to go hiking at Lake Perez, go boating, have a picnic, register for a program and just be outdoors. Boats are available on a first come-first serve basis and start at $10 per hour.

There will also be some lawn games and self-guided hikes available. It’s recommended to bring a water bottle, sunglasses, sunscreen, a small backpack and whatever items you may need for the activities you would like to do.

Students can sign up for free shuttles leaving from the Intramural Building.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Column | Long live jazz
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
During his monologue during the 98th Academy Awards last month, host Conan O’Brien addressed Timothée Chalamet’s controversial comments about how “no one cares” about ballet and opera. “They’re [the opera and ballet communities] just mad you left out jazz,” O’Brien said jokingly.

If it’s not the butt of the joke, jazz generally tends to fly under the radar. In comparison to other genres, few engage with it. There are modern day jazz artists like Samara Joy and artists that have introduced a jazz- infused style of pop music like Laufey. Regardless, jazz is still a fairly niche genre.

Created by the Smithsonian’s National Museum of American History to celebrate jazz and honor the genre’s history, Jazz Appreciation Month (JAM) is recognized every April.

The genre’s origins can be traced back to the beginning of the twentieth century in New Orleans. It’s primarily a product of the African American community, where African musical traditions, spirituals, blues, European harmonic structures and ragtime were fused into the genre we know today. It eventually spread to major cities like Chicago, New York and St. Louis during the Great Migration and further gained popularity

One of the more defining characteristics of jazz is the use of improvisation, which is when a musician creates their own melodic solo on the spot in accordance with the rest of the band members. With this in mind, it makes jazz one of the freer musical genres. Improvisation allows the musician in that moment to express themselves by being in creative control. The listener is at their mercy.

It’s unpredictable and spontaneous. It’s a nice change of pace from the popular music of today that’s often repetitive and predictable.

Jazz is a collective effort, placing an importance on group collaboration. Jazz lets everyone in the ensemble shine by having many opportunities for multiple players to solo in a song. It’s unlike other genres where normally leading instruments like guitar are the ones to solo and therefore get the most recognition. It’s a more democratic art form.

One of the things that attracted me to jazz is how it can capture emotions within the song. There seems to be a song that can accurately capture any mood.

A driving drum beat with a fast rhythmic pattern on the ride cymbal creates frantic energy. The slow and brooding horn section brings melancholy. The warmth of the alto singer’s voice is like an aural sunshine.

It’s storytelling, regardless of whether the song is lyrical or not. The musicians are telling a story through their instruments and are also communicating with the other instrumentalists of the ensemble during the song.

I feel like jazz turns people off because it gives the impression that you need to have a music background to enjoy it. It’s been labeled “too intellectual,” whatever that means. You don’t need to have a deep understanding of music theory to enjoy it. Like I mentioned above, jazz is able to convey emotion. Chances are if the song is creating or reminding you of an emotion, it’s probably doing the same for someone else.

Therefore, it allows us to connect with others, similar to other genres. It’s only deemed intimidating and inaccessible because someone put that label on it. It requires a higher level of attention than other styles of music, but to label it inaccessible because of an unwillingness to be present isn’t fair.

Similar to anything, becoming familiar with jazz is about finding what you like. There are several subgenres to jazz including swing, bebop, cool, and avant-garde to name a few. They vary in tempo, themes, musical characteristics and ensemble size. But at the end of the day, they all create the diverse tapestry that is jazz.

While I understand that not everyone is going to like jazz, I think it’s important to at least try it before you decide it’s not for you. If you don’t know where to start, allow me to offer some of my favorites to begin with: Ella Fitzgerald, John Coltrane and Charles Mingus.

It’s no secret that popular music has an edge over jazz, but the genre has continued to persist regardless. May that vitality continue on. Long live jazz.

MORE COLUMNS

title: How Trump’s executive order on NIL, transfer portal previously could’ve affected Penn State football
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
On Friday, President Donald Trump officially signed an executive order aimed at “saving college sports.”

Among other provisions, the order affords student-athletes five years to play five seasons, one transfer within those five seasons and a risk of losing federal funding for universities that aren’t compliant.

The order isn’t set to take effect until Aug. 1, and is expected to face legal challenges, so there’s no guarantee it’ll ever go through. But how could this provision have affected Penn State football in the past? Specifically, from the start of the NIL and transfer portal era of college football, which were both established in 2021.

Let’s take a look at some potential scenarios.

Eligibility rules

Perhaps the biggest effect to come from the rule of getting five years to play five seasons is the opportunity for freshmen to play without sacrificing a year of eligibility.

In recent memory, some notable Nittany Lions to earn James Franklin’s “yellow light,” meaning they’d only burn their redshirt if needed to, were wide receivers Koby Howard and Tyseer Denmark, defensive end Yvan Kemajou, defensive end Max Granville and offensive tackle Anthony Donkoh.

Howard and Kemajou ended up burning their eligibility anyway, but there’s a chance they could’ve become immediate starters or impact players in the rotation had Franklin believed he could use them for five seasons.

Donkoh, on the other hand, redshirted in 2023, despite then-starter Olu Fashanu stating he was “nowhere near” as talented as Donkoh, along with fellow redshirt J’ven Williams, as a freshman.

Granville played in seven games in 2024, but the only ones that count toward his eligibility were the three during the regular season. Granville slotted in behind Abdul Carter in the Nittany Lions’ passing rushing package, called “Prowler,” so he could’ve contributed more in this hypothetical.

But this rule being in place could’ve affected some of Penn State’s veterans as well. The Covid year granted current student-athletes an additional year of eligibility, if they chose to use it, but players from the 2021 class could’ve stayed for 2025 had they not run out of eligibility.

The ones that burned their redshirt are particularly of interest, like safety Jaylen Reed.

Looking ahead to 2026, defensive tackle Zane Durant and transfer Devonte Ross are notables who could’ve redeemed one more season if they wanted.

Additionally, there’s a world where Drew Allar, who said he considered a medical redshirt after his 2025 season-ending broken ankle against Northwestern, plays one more year of college football, whether that’s under Campbell at Penn State, under Franklin with the Hokies or elsewhere, creating a butterfly effect for the fates of Rocco Becht and now-Hokie Ethan Grunkemeyer.

Transfer portal limit

Trump’s rule gives players one chance to transfer without penalty throughout their five years. Graduate transfers would get an additional opportunity to enter the portal.

That means safety King Mack, who left Penn State for Alabama after his freshman year and returned to Happy Valley a season later, would’ve been stuck with the Crimson Tide, unless he wanted to sit out for a season. Mack’s now at NC State, which also wouldn’t have been permissible under the pending rules.

2025 defensive tackle Owen Wafle would likely also be barred from transferring without penalty, as he’s now a redshirt sophomore at Duke after first transferring into Happy Valley from Michigan.

But, except for graduate transfers, the Nittany Lions haven’t housed many multi-time transfers in recent years, so they would’ve been largely unaffected by this rule in the past.

There is an effect this rule would’ve had on players who made multiple transfers after leaving Penn State. Notably, that’s quarterback Ta’Quan Roberson, who transferred to UConn, Kansas State and Buffalo, respectively, throughout his six years in college football.

Now-Florida running back London Montgomery would likely also be stuck at his previous program in this scenario. The redshirt junior and former 4-star recruit spent one season in Happy Valley before departing for East Carolina. He led the Pirates with 1,101 rushing yards and eight touchdowns before dipping back into the market.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s basketball forward Mason Blackwood to enter transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A promising Penn State freshman is set to leave the team.

Forward Mason Blackwood is entering the transfer portal, according to Sam Kayser of League Ready. He has three seasons of eligibility remaining.

NEWS: Penn State freshman Mason Blackwood is entering the transfer portal, he told @LeagueRDY.The 6-foot-7 wing is a former four-star recruit out of Rochester, New York who played just one season for the Nittany Lions.Averaged 2.6PPG and 1.8RPG in his first collegiate season. pic.twitter.com/AhK6vx2aJu— Sam Kayser (@KayserHoops) April 5, 2026

Blackwood was a depth piece in the rotation, seeing 11.9 minutes per game across 26 appearances. He flashed his potential when given an extended look, tallying 14 points and six rebounds with 57.1% shooting in 22 minutes against Wisconsin on Jan. 22.

When Blackwood arrived on campus, he became known as a player who gives full effort in practice. He made his first career start in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament, and Mike Rhoades explained the decision postgame.

“The last three weeks, he's been playing harder than others, his physicality and just the way he competes,” Rhoades said. “He's going to get better and better. He cares so much. He works really hard. He's very competitive, and he's got a big body, big, strong body. I thought his defense was good today. We need a little more offense out of him, but thought he did some good things, proud of him.”

The 247Sports 4-star prospect averaged 2.6 points and 1.8 rebounds per game this season. He ranked as the No. 114 player nationally, No. 43 small forward and No. 7 player in New Hampshire out of New Hampton High School.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls short of comeback, suffers sweep to Nebraska in series finale
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Back and looking for redemption, Penn State took on Nebraska one last time to close out their series against the current conference leader.

With a series sweep, the blue and white fell in a comeback game against Nebraska, trailing by just two runs in the bottom of the ninth.

Michael Anderson has been a consistent hitter for the Nittany Lions all year, and started them off with a two-run homer in the first inning. The Cornhuskers remained off the board, as the blue and white held a strong defensive position.

Nebraska's Will Jesske scored the team's first run in the bottom of the second inning before Drew Grego, a key player for the Cornhuskers this year and in this series, launched a two-run home run to centerfield and Nebraska scored two more runs, taking the lead 5-2.

Anderson, once again for Penn State, launched a homer into center field in the top of the third frame, before the Cornhusker's Rhett Stokes blasted a homerun down the left field line to bring in another pair. Nebraska took a huge lead at 8-3.

The blue and white struggled to come back in the fourth inning, but held up defensively with pitcher Mason Butash striking out first baseman Case Sanderson. Nittany Lion shortstop Preston Yaucher took out Cornhusker Dylan Carey's stolen base attempt with a throw to catcher Avery Smith.

The score remained through the fifth and sixth innings, with neither team letting up any runs.

After another empty top of the inning for Nebraska, relief pitcher Kyle Mannell came in for Cooper Katskee ahead of a fresh lineup start for the Nittany Lions. The team was unable to score any runs, still trailing by five to go into the final inning.

Nebraska saw a final pitching switch to maintain their lead with Chase Olson coming onto the mound, as third baseman Bryce Molinaro was eager to get it going for Penn State. Olson gave up two walks for the Nittany Lions before being switched out by Pryce Bender. With another walk, Molinaro was able to come home and put Penn State up 8-4.

Trying to close the gap, Penn State had its best run of the game in the bottom of the ninth with Jesse Jaconski starting them out with a ground double play where Barnett was able to score another run, quickly followed by Jack Porter scoring on a wild pitch.

Coming up just short, Penn State fell to Nebraska as the Cornhuskers remain undefeated at home, taking the win 8-6 in the final game of the series.

Up Next

Penn State returns home to face FDU in a one-game, Dollar Dog Night home stand. The clash is set for Tuesday, with first pitch at 5:30 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's lacrosse gets back in win column, defeats Ohio State
author: Annie Alberts | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State found itself in a tough stretch of the season, facing highly ranked opponents and not coming out victorious. However, that changed in Sunday afternoon's game against Ohio State.

The Nittany Lions defeated the Buckeyes 18-9, getting back into the win column in dominating fashion.

Penn State started scoring goals almost instantly. Graduate student Anna Salerno scored the first goal of the game at 13:50. Seconds later, Ohio State tied the scoreboard 1-1.

From there, Penn State’s offense began to lead the game. The game’s top four scorers, Payton Wainman, Kelly MacKinney, Rocquette Allen and Erika Ho scored four goals in three minutes.

Wainman started it off by scoring her 32nd goal of the season. It was assisted by Delaney Radin, Penn State’s leader in assists.

Two minutes later, the freshman star Allen scored her 31st goal of the season. It was unassisted and led the Nittany Lions to a 4-1 lead.

Less than a minute after that, Ho scored a man-up goal, causing Ohio State to call a timeout.

MacKinney contributed the most to the scoreboard, scoring five goals before the end of the third period. Her first goal was made by a free position shot in the first period.

After a goal scored by Ohio State’s Camryn Callaghan, both teams began going back and forth for scoring.

Although Penn State’s offense played with grit, goalkeeper Sydney Manning was a huge asset to Sunday's victory.

Manning made 12 saves against Ohio State’s offense. In total, Ohio State had 39 shots and 21 shots on goal. In comparison, Penn State had 26 shots and 23 shots on goal.

In the previous game, Manning was taken out of the game for receiving a yellow card. Freshman Makenna Gagliardi took her place and had a very impressive first collegiate appearance.

At the very end of the first period, Radin scored her 17th goal of the season. It was assisted by Allen, who was assisting more than scoring in Sunday's game.

In the second period, Penn State scored goals on rapid fire.

Radin scored once again at 13:23, followed by Ohio State scoring a free-position shot goal minutes later.

Ho led another man-up goal for the Lions at 6:42.

Then, Wainman scored two goals back-to-back, giving Penn State a five-point lead. Her first goal was assisted by Radin, while her second was assisted by MacKinney.

With 39 seconds left in the second period, MacKinney scored a man-up goal, assisted by Allen.

MacKinney and Allen were a goal-scoring duo, with MacKinney making the shots and Allen making the assists.

By the third and fourth quarters, Ohio State’s goal scoring became less frequent.

Allen scored her 32nd goal of the season in the first minute of the third, which came unassisted.

Then, MacKinney and Radin scored twice in two minutes together. The next goal Penn State scored, a man-up, was also by MacKinney, assisted by Allen.

The fourth and final quarter was a little quieter for Penn State.

Ho led her third and final man-up goal of the day, unassisted. Instantly afterwards, Emma Kelly got a draw control and MacKinney scored her 31st goal of the season. And, her final seventh goal of the day.

In addition to scoring, Penn State was very successful with their ground balls and turnovers. In total, there were 18 ground balls and 10 turnovers. Defenseman Lily Spilker led the team in ground balls with five.

The Nittany Lions took the victory 18-9, defeating Ohio State on the road.

Up Next

Penn State will return home to Panzer Stadium for a matchup with Oregon at 8 p.m. Thursday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Injuries in Penn State QB room allow others to see expanded role
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
With Rocco Becht working his way back from a torn labrum in his non-throwing shoulder and an AC joint sprain in his throwing shoulder, the expectation was that Alex Manske would take over the first-team reps.

That plan has changed, as after it was reported last week that Manske would be out for spring ball, Campbell confirmed on Tuesday the news.

“We feel for Alex,” Campbell said. “It's kind of something he's been dealing with, but we felt it was best to get it taken care of now so we can get him back ready for fall camp.”

This is the second consecutive spring ball that he has dealt with an injury. Still, quarterbacks coach Jake Waters said the Iowa State transfer is doing everything he can to stay up to date with what’s going on for Penn State.

While Manske isn’t currently in State College, having gone back home to Iowa to rehab, he’s been “doing awesome,” according to Waters.

“Talk to him every day. He's in great spirits. He's itching to get back here,” Waters said. “We Zoom him in on every single meeting. And with the time change, if it's early, we record it and send it to him, and then me and him meet. We’ll meet and go over practice together. He's all about watching every single rep. He's got a iPad at home that he can continue to to watch the film and stay in touch with him.”

So with Manske being sidelined and Becht limited, it’s been Connor Barry, the Division III transfer from Christopher Newport, who has been working with the first team. Campbell referred to him as the "surprise" of spring ball after four practices.

Other than Becht, Barry is the only gunslinger in the room who has played significant football. He’s thrown for 5,561 passing yards and 55 touchdowns in his career, adding 596 yards and 18 scores on the ground. The lefty is a two-time All-American, and in 2025, was a semifinalist for the Gagliardi Trophy, which is the Division III equivalent of the Heisman Trophy.

“He's doing really well — a guy that's played a lot of football coming in,” Waters said. “You can tell that the moment, out there in practice, isn't too big for him. He's playing fast and free, and the game for him has slowed down compared to some of those other guys who still should be in high school because he's played so much football.”

The injuries to Becht and Manske have also given an increased workload to freshmen Peyton Falzone and Kase Evans, as well as redshirt junior walk-on Jack Lambert.

While Becht is currently in the recovery process, limited in what he can do in practice, he’s still been around the unit, acting as another coach on the field.

Come fall camp, Becht will be on track to retain his role as the starting quarterback, but in the meantime he’s sharpening his skillset as a leader with the injury and being an asset for the other quarterbacks to capitalize on their expanded roles.

“I think it's great to have me on the sideline, and it's a big step for me, opportunity for me to lead those guys and help them with the plays that they're running in team reps in 7-on-7,” Becht said. “To give them feedback on what I'm seeing, and they take it. They love to be coached. They love to ask questions, and so I think it's been going great the past four weeks.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball sees Ivan Jurić return, adds 2 overseas players
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Important roster news for Penn State's 2026-27 squad came in on Sunday.

Starting center Ivan Jurić is returning to the team while the Nittany Lions are adding overseas players Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut, according to DraftExpress.

NEWS: Big day for Penn State, locking up 3 signings from Miško Ražnatović and Beobasket.— Ivan Jurić (re-signing): 10.2 points, 5.3 rebounds as a freshman starter— Roko Prkačin: 6'9, 23-year old Croatian national team player, 17 mpg for Nanterre (2nd in Pro A France)—… pic.twitter.com/YMrpqENH6G— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) April 5, 2026

Jurić can play inside and out, making plays in the paint while also showcasing the ability to knock down some 3-pointers. He had a 2-of-5 outing from deep against Michigan and Michigan State this season, and he finished the year with 15-of-53 shooting from 3-point range.

After coming in off the bench for the first four games of the season, Jurić became the Nittany Lions’ starter at the five. He averaged 10.2 points and 5.3 rebounds per game with 57.7% shooting from the field across 30 appearances.

Prkačin has played for the Croatian national team before, and he was with the professional team Nanterre in France this season. He's listed at 6-foot-9, averaging 6.2 points and 4.7 rebounds in 17.3 minutes per game across 23 appearances.

Prkačin was the MVP of the U16 European Championship in 2018 and was once a projected NBA lottery pick in the 2021 NBA draft. He withdrew his name from consideration and has been playing professionally overseas since the 2020-21 season.

Wibaut was a starter for Pac Orthez in Pro B in France, averaging 8.6 points, 3.6 rebounds and 1.6 assists per game. He's been playing professionally in France for the past three seasons, and he's also spent time on the French U21 team from 2021-24.

Prkačin and Wibaut are the first two additions for Penn State this offseason. The only departures so far are guards Freddie Dilione V and Melih Tunca, who both intend to enter the transfer portal when it opens on Tuesday.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: SPA, Opulence presents electrifying drag show featuring Bianca Del Rio and Alyssa Edwards
author: Lily Dorf | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s Student Programming Association (SPA) partnered with Opulence to bring an electrifying drag show Saturday night. The event was co-hosted by “RuPaul’s Drag Race” season six winner Bianca Del Rio and Drag Performer Alyssa Edwards as a part of Campus Pride Month

Opulence is Penn State’s Premiere Drag Ambassador club, hosting many drag shows and events throughout the school year. All students are welcome and encouraged to attend the upbeat shows and games.

With the bright lights and the buzz of excitement from audience members, Alumni Hall was ready for the queens and kings to perform. As the lights lowered and the crowd hushed, Alyssa Edwards made her way onto the stage, and the show began.

Each drag queen and king was given two slots of time to give it their all, showing off their dancing and lip-syncing skills. The energy throughout the show was electric, with students screaming and cheering for every performer who graced the stage.

For many, this was a new experience, but for Natalie Cappetta, this wasn’t her first rodeo, or rather, her first drag show. She said she readily grabbed tickets for this event after she saw the post on Instagram.

“My roommate, Grace, and I have been going to drag shows since our freshman year,” Cappetta, a fourth-year studying secondary education, said.

She and her roommate decided to attend the show tonight to fulfill their tradition and see some famous, familiar faces like Edwards and Del Rio.

“It's always good to support the community and for them to come to our school in the middle of Pennsylvania, it's just really great to see such big names and people that I watched both of their original seasons… we had to show up,” Cappetta said.

Juliana Gray came out to support her love for drag and friend, Tom Foolery, one of the drag performers tonight, who hit the stage in clown makeup and danced the night away.

“I've been going to drag shows since I was 16,” Gray, a fourth-year studying systems neuroscience, said. “I love them, and I love being a part of them and I love seeing smaller drag queens coming out and working with what they have to put on a performance for hundreds of people, so I love to go out and for any small drag queens.”

At the halfway point in the show, Bianca Del Rio pulled six contestants out of the crowd to compete in a lip-syncing competition. Each contestant was given a song to lip-sync to, and the crowd voted on who was the best. Contestant six, Timia Smith, was ready and willing to get up on stage and show the crowd what she had.

“I wasn't actually nervous, I don't really have stage fright,” Smith, a second-year studying advertising/public relations, said. “In high school, I participated in a lot of my high school musicals so I wasn't scared to be up there. It was more so I was only a little bit scared of what song it was going to be, but even though I didn't know the song, it would be okay.”

Smith said she loves drag performances and feels it’s important to show up and support the performers.

“There's no such thing as an awkward moment, in drag, you know?” Smith said. “It’s such a welcoming place for everyone…It doesn't matter about your race, your gender, your sexuality, it doesn't matter.”

Even though the energy could have kept the night going for hours, the show came to an end as all of the drag performers came to take the stage one last time to accept all of the love and support pouring out of the room.

“You're welcome there no matter what, and everyone supports each other,” Smith said. “We're all cheering for each other, and it’s an environment that we could really use nowadays. At PSU, we care, we're nice, we're kind, we're compassionate, and I think that's something that drag shows really speak to the world right now.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: What defines a successful spring for Penn State? | The 1-0 Podcast
author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
The “1-0 Podcast” co-hosts Avery Hill and Lexie Linderman rolled the tape as Penn State continues to chop away at its first spring ball under Matt Campbell.

They started the conversation with a look at a blueprint for success during the spring session, discussing multiple position groups, and an even closer peek at defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn, who drew praise from Campbell during the week.

The duo also touched on the recently announced Blue-White Practice, tossing ideas on what the state of the spring festivities will be in the future.

MORE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's gymnastics participates in Big Ten Championships
author: Jimmy Blair | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following the first night of the Big Ten Championships in Lincoln, Nebraska, the Nittany Lions competed in the event finals of the championship. Penn State came up short on the first night, with Michigan securing its seventh consecutive conference championship.

The blue and white delivered solid performances on all events they participated in, but failed to place first in any rotation.

Floor

Penn State didn't feature any athletes on the floor in the event finals, with Ohio State’s Chase Davenport-Mills securing the event title with a 13.825.

Pommel horse

The Nittany Lions featured Blacktown, Australia native Ritam Malik on the pommel horse, putting up a respectable 12.850. A perfect score was almost achieved by Illinois’ Brandon Dang, who scored an event-winning 14.850. Ohio State’s Parker Thackston came up just behind Dang with a 14.625.

Rings

Matt Underhill and Kellen Ryan took to the still rings for the blue and white. Ryan scored a 12.825, while Underhill scored an impressive 14.075, tying him in second for the event. Asher Cohen secured the event win for Nebraska with a 14.125.

Vault

The blue and white rolled out Big Ten freshman of the year Shaun Smith and Ryan on the Vault. Ryan managed a 13.600, and Smith continued his strong freshman campaign with a 13.975, putting him in fifth. Chase Pappas took the event for Michigan with a 14.150.

Bars

Akseli Karsikas represented the blue and white in the final two rotations of the evening, securing a 12.825 on the parallel bars and a 13.400 on the high bar, slotting him into fourth in the latter event. Nebraska’s Nathan York secured first in the parallel bars for the Cornhuskers, while Carson Eshleman took the final event for Michigan with a 14.200.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions look ahead to the NCAA Championships on April 17 and 18.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls in mercy rule defeat to Nebraska, closing out doubleheader
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Hawks Field at Haymarket Park for the final game in their doubleheader against Nebraska.

The Nittany Lions fell in a mercy-rule loss in the seventh to the Cornhuskers, 13-1.

Nebraska dominated the first inning, scoring a run with the bases loaded from Mac Moyer before Jett Buck slammed a homer into right field, letting in four more runs, putting the Cornhuskers at a quick 5-0.

Penn State came up short in the second, failing to respond as Nebraska’s Trey Fikes racked up another run, just before Moyer scored his second run of the game on a passed ball.

The blue and white were able to respond in the third with a double to right field by Spencer Barnett, letting in Preston Yaucher and earning their first run of the game. Fikes, once again for the Cornhuskers, came swinging with a hit that doubled into left field, earning another run from Drew Grego, putting the score at 8-1 in the third.

Showing no mercy, Nebraska came into the fourth strong on the offense, scoring another three runs as the Nittany Lions hitters struggled to get onto the bases, and pushed them further away from the Cornhuskers at a 10-run lead going into the fifth.

The blue and white offense couldn’t seem to get it together, continuing to strike out in the top of the fifth, and struggled on the mound with pitcher Frankie Sanchez letting in another run by Moyer and Fikes off a double RBI hit on the left field line.

A quiet sixth inning left the Nittany Lions in a scramble to make a comeback in the seventh, but they came up short as the Cornhuskers came into this second game of the doubleheader blazing, winning 13-1 after it seemed to be a competitive series after the score of the first game. Penn State now stands 8-20.

Up Next

Penn State will return to Hawks Field at Haymarket Park tomorrow at 1 p.m. in search of redemption against Nebraska in the last game of their series this weekend.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State picks up 2nd commitment of the day from WR Landon Blum
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
A 4-star is on the board for Penn State's 2027 class.

Landon Blum, a wide receiver from Woodbine, Iowa, has committed to the Nittany Lions.

Blum is ranked by 247Sports composite as the No. 300 player nationally and No. 1 player in Iowa.

He chose Penn State over Ole Miss, Iowa and Kansas.

Blum currently sits at 6-foot-4, 200 lbs. He plays eight-man football for his local high school, Woodbine.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball's bats show up, lose close matchup with No. 19 Nebraska
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
One week after taking on Illinois at home, the Nittany Lions traveled to Lincoln, Nebraska to face off against the number one team in the Big Ten, the Nebraska Cornhuskers.

While they came up just short, 8-7, the Nittany Lions put up a strong fight against Nebraska, setting a good tone for the weekend series.

After a quiet first inning, the Nittany Lions made the first play in the second inning, starting with a run from Jack Porter off a wild pitch. The Cornhuskers responded with a home run for left fielder Jett Buck.

Penn State took back the lead with a strong third inning, starting with an RBI double for Michael Anderson, letting in Cohl Mercado and Preston Yaucher. Soon followed by another two runs, this time a home run by Bryce Molinaro, putting up runs for him and Anderson.

The Cornhuskers met the blue and white with a two-run homer of their own, showing a strong offensive response from Nebraska.

A game seemingly filled with home runs, Justin Turcovski launched a homer into center field in the top of the fourth for two runs, putting the team up 7-4 after Nebraska’s Drew Grego ran one in from second base off a hit into center field.

The Nittany Lions came out of the fifth without any runs, while Nebraska had a strong offensive run, with the bases loaded. Grego singled through right field, allowing Case Sanderson to make the play and hit in another run for the Cornhuskers, to catch up to the blue and white.

Nebraska continued their catch-up to Penn State, as the Nittany Lions were stuck at seven runs, while the Cornhuskers dominated the sixth, putting up two runs, all on wild pitches by Dimond Loosli. They continued their runs into the seventh, with Grego making a statement for Nebraska on the day, scoring another run for the team, putting Nebraska up 8-7.

The Nittany Lions couldn’t seem to get their momentum going into the eighth and ninth innings, sitting at seven runs. Relying on a quick offensive win in the ninth , Penn State came up just short with three outs, leaving the Cornhuskers to take the win in the first game of the series.

As a team that's struggled this year, the momentum coming out of this game should set the team up well for the rest of their weekend in Nebraska.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions will take on the Cornhuskers once again on Saturday night in Hawks Field at Haymarket Park, in their doubleheader for the series this weekend.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State earns 2027 commitment from DL Carter Blattner
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Matt Campbell is continuing to build Penn State's 2027 class.

Carter Blattner has committed to the Nittany Lions, he announced on Saturday.

Blattner is a 3-star defensive lineman from New Jersey. He's ranked by 247Sports composite as the No. 48 defensive lineman nationally and the No. 8 player from his home state.

Penn State hosted Blattner on Saturday for an unofficial visit, and he has an official visit scheduled for June 5.

Blattner picked the Nittany Lions over USC, Kentucky and Virginia Tech.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball defeats Charleston 3-0 to complete weekend series sweep
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
After picking up a 3-0 victory over Charleston on Friday, Penn State returned to the court to attempt the sweep and leave West Virginia with a series victory.

The Nittany Lions were rewarded with another 3-0 victory for their efforts against the Golden Eagles as they extended their winning streak to five-straight.

Penn State kill leader Sean Harvey opened up the scoring for the blue and white, recording the first kill of the afternoon to put the Nittany Lions on the board early on.

A service error went to Owen Rose as the mistake shrunk Penn State’s lead to 6-4 just before Braxton Francis picked up a service ace to get the Golden Eagles’ deficit to just one point still in the early running of the opening set.

The action came to a 9-9 standstill after a few strong Charleston points, including a kill from Joonatan Salpakari, who previously had 101 total on the season.

Rose tallied an ace on his serve to put the blue and white up 16-14 as the end of the first set crept closer.

The 6-foot-7 middle blocker from Long Island entered Saturday’s match with 24 service aces on the season.

Getting his name back in the mix, Rose notched a quick kill right up at the net just low enough so nobody was able to get to it as he moved his squad up to the 22-20 lead with a first set victory in sight.

Penn State picked up the match point thanks to a Golden Eagle touch before the ball went out of play, sealing a 25-20 first set victory as the blue and white moved one set closer to a sweep.

Rose picked up another kill in the early running of the second set as he tied the action at 3-3. The redshirt senior notches 1.16 kills per set and has the highest hitting percentage of the Nittany Lions, hitting .375 this season.

Charleston was having trouble holding a lead, but jumped up to a 10-8 advantage after a Rose scoring effort went too long. The Golden Eagles added another one on with an ace immediately after to take a three-point lead before a timeout.

Harvey got back in on the scoring when he recorded an ace that brought the blue and white within one of Charleston’s lead, just before Matthew Luoma’s kill went flying into the stands, which tied the second set at 13-13

Luoma trails just Harvey atop Penn State’s leaderboard, as the redshirt junior entered Saturday’s match with 116 fewer kills than the latter.

Despite the Golden Eagles holding a lead for a while, the blue and white fought back and turned it into a 21-20 advantage in its favor before a Charleston kill tied the set up once again.

With the score stuck at 24-24, some extra play was needed, and through some last-minute theatrics that saw a Conrad Spidsø and Francis block among other high-flying play, Penn State captured the second set 28-26 and moved even closer to a weekend sweep.

Penn State again got on the board first in the third set due to a Charleston service error that marked the Golden Eagles’ 12th service error of the afternoon.

Rose recorded another service ace, adding to an already busy night as he put the blue and white up 5-2 quickly in the third set.

It was all Penn State in the third frame, with a Golden Eagle attack error giving the Nittany Lions an 11-6 lead after Charleston protested about a potential blue and white touch.

The Golden Eagles eventually got their way, going on a 3-1 scoring run to cut their deficit to 12-9 before Luoma tallied another kill to expand on the blue and white’s momentum.

No drama was involved with how the remainder of the third set was going, as Penn State increased its lead to 20-14 after another Rose kill, with the series sweep looking more and more likely by the second.

Charleston did its best to make things look interesting in the final moments of the contest, drawing within a few of Penn State’s lead, but back-to-back Luoma kills sealed the deal as the Nittany Lions picked up a 25-20 third-set victory to close out the 3-0 sweep in the match and the series as well.

Up Next

Penn State returns to action next weekend when it heads to Fairfield, Connecticut, to take on Sacred Heart in a two-match series.

The opening match will begin at 7 p.m. on Friday, with the second of the two at 3 p.m. Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball closes out series with walkoff victory over Maryland
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Following Penn State's large wins against Maryland, outscoring the Terrapins 29-9 in the course of two games, the Nittany Lions ended their Big Ten series against Maryland on Saturday afternoon, the second half of a doubleheader.

The blue and white secured the series sweep with a walkoff win late in the bottom of the seventh for a 2-0 win.

In the circle, righty pitcher Abigail Britton started things off for Penn State. An error from the infield got junior outfielder Bailey Murphy on base for an early rally for the Terrapins. Britton allowed a walk and a single that got the bases loaded.

Freshman catcher Zayda Rocke attempted a lead, but a lineout ended the top of the first.

Junior second baseman Michela Barbanente started things for the Nittany Lions in the bottom of the first with a single down the middle, but that was all the action the blue and white could get in the first inning.

Britton continued dominating, only allowing a single hit and striking out a Maryland batter to close out the top of the second.

Junior infielder Jordan George won the battle against Aubrey Wurst’s full count, getting a walk to start things off for Penn State in the bottom of the second. After a sacrifice bunt and fly out, freshman Gabby Gradishar stepped into the batter's box and also got walked. Freshman Kali Karwowski joined the game as a pinch runner and flew past the Terrapin defense to steal third base.

Sophomore catcher Macy Chamberlin got a strong hit with hopes to create momentum for the blue and white with a runner in scoring position, but a flyout ended the inning.

Britton continued her strong start, going 1-2-3 at the top of the third, securing her third strikeout of the day. Still nothing on the board.

The bottom of the third called for the top of the order for the Nittany Lions. Hanik and freshman shortstop Allison Oneacre were walked, but after a sacrifice bunt, foul out and ground out, the inning ended without a score.

For the top of the fourth, Britton surrendered her first walk of the game. Senior infielder Caroline Fox hit a ball to right field, and junior outfielder Jaylee Williams grounded out, but it gave Maryland two runners in scoring position.

The Terrapins were threatening to take a lead, but the Penn State defense closed the top of the fourth before any damage to the scoreboard could happen.

Sophomore outfielder Natalie Lieto got hit by a pitch to get a runner on base for the blue and white and attempted to sneak past the Maryland offense, but the overrule gave the Nittany Lions their first out of the inning.

Nothing came from Penn State's offense in the fourth. Coach Clarisa Crowell made a change in the circle and brought out junior right-hander McKenna Young.

Maryland returned to the top of its order, where Young gave up her first walk of the game to Murphy to start the top of the fifth. A wild pitch from Young gave Murphy the ability to steal third.

Fortunately for Penn State, Young secured her first strikeout to close out the inning, still scoreless, to head into the bottom of the fifth.

Another slow start in the bottom of the fifth, Wurst walked three Penn State batters, bringing Lieto up to bat with bases loaded and two outs. She attempted for a grand slam but hit a pop-up as a result to close out the fifth.

The top of the sixth was quick with Young on the mound. Despite allowing a walk, she kept the scoreboard empty.

Junior outfielder Frankee Flesher drew momentum for the Nittany Lion offense after stealing two bases and attempting to score the first run of the game, but an appeal from Maryland ended the inning.

In the top of the seventh, Oneacre caught a line drive that she turned into a double play to get the defense moving. Young followed by allowing another walk, but a lineout catch ended the top of the seventh, giving the blue and white the momentum to close out the weekend.

The top of the order was due up in the bottom of the seventh, while Wurst hit 120 pitches. By walking Hanik, and a sacrifice bunt by Barbenete. Klosowicz stepped into the batter's box and smashed the ball over the fence to give the Nittany Lions the walk-off win.

Up Next

Penn State will host another Big Ten series at Nittany Lion Softball Park at Beard Field, facing off with Northwestern. The series will begin at 5 p.m. Friday, with the two teams returning to the diamond at 2 p.m. Saturday. They’ll close out the series at 11:30 a.m. Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s lacrosse falls away from home to No. 16 Johns Hopkins
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
No. 6 Penn State put its perfect Big Ten record on the line when it traveled to Baltimore, Maryland, for a matchup with No. 16 Johns Hopkins.

The blue and white couldn’t withstand the comeback effort from the Blue Jays as it fell 11-10, dropping its first Big Ten matchup of the season.

After a silent first two minutes, the Blue Jays struck first on a goal from Jimmy Ayers. The Nittany Lions responded about two minutes later as Liam Matthews found Kyle Lehman, who found the back of the net to knot things up.

Scoring came at a premium in the opening part of the first quarter. Johns Hopkins’ Jameson Smith broke the tie at the 8:37 mark, scoring his second goal of the season.

Penn State went on a run from there. Michael Faraone found the back of the net off an assist from Jack Iannantuano. Just a minute later, Matthews scored on a feed from Patrick Carragher, giving Penn State a 3-2 lead.

The blue and white built on its lead with a goal from Chase Robertson, doubling the lead. As time expired, Matt Collison scored for the Blue Jays on an assist from Brooks English, leaving the score at 4-3 after the opening quarter.

Penn State came out firing in the second quarter. Iannantuano scored his first of the game as he cut into the front of the net, dove out and snuck it past Oran Gelinas. Scoring came at a premium in the second quarter as the two defenses ramped up, preventing either team from scoring.

At 8:43, Mac Hobbs scored his third goal of the season as he broke free from his man to score. Over three minutes later, John Jude Considine utilized a swim move to evade defenders and break open the score.

Johns Hopkins would get on the board in the second quarter. Charlie Iler beat Preston Hawkins, scoring his 12th goal of the season. Penn State entered halftime with a 7-4 lead.

The Nittany Lions opened the second half with the momentum still on their side. Chase Robertson scored his second goal of the game, becoming the first multi-goal scorer of the game.

Luke Walstrom extended the lead as the blue and white had all the momentum. His 10th goal of the season gave it a 9-4 lead.

However, Johns Hopkins flipped momentum on its head. Hunter Chauvette broke the scoring drought with a goal, and a little over a minute later, Ayers found the back of the net once again, pulling the game to 9-6.

The defenses once again ramped up as a 7:01 scoreless run began for both squads. Neither team scored during that entire time. That ended with a goal from Liam Burke, pulling Johns Hopkins to within two goals.

Smith fired his third goal of the season past Hawkins, as the Blue Jays went on a 4-0 run to pull within a goal entering the fourth quarter.

The goalies flipped from the first to the second half. In the first half, Gelinas struggled to make saves on the versatile Penn State offense. In the second half, it was Hawkins who seemingly couldn’t make a stop. Chauvette opened the fourth quarter to tie things up at 9-9 with all the momentum on the home team’s side.

Just 30 seconds later, Collison gave the Blue Jays their first lead since 2-1 with a goal, beating Hawkins. While Penn State couldn’t get anything going on offense, Johns Hopkins found success again. Chauvette scored his third goal of the game, giving the Blue Jays an 11-9 lead and a 7-0 run.

The almost 25-minute scoring drought for the blue and white came to an end as Michael Faraone received a contested feed in front of the net and immediately sent it into the net.

The Nittany Lions turned to a zone defense, and it slowed the game down immediately. Johns Hopkins worked the ball around with no movement and gave up possession, seemingly chewing the clock with still significant time left.

The Blue Jays forced a turnover in their offensive territory, and Penn State needed a turnover of its own to try to tie things up. It did so as an errant pass went out of bounds.

With 28 seconds left, the Nittany Lions had an opportunity to tie things up. Both shot attempts hit a defender in front, as Johns Hopkins’ defense held up to hold onto the upset, 11-10 victory.

Up Next

Penn State returns to Panzer Stadium for its Senior Day game against Michigan at 1 p.m. Saturday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: What does President Donald Trump’s executive order mean for Penn State Athletics?
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
President Donald Trump made headlines in the collegiate athletics world on Friday as he signed an executive order titled “Urgent National Action to Save College Sports.”

Trump’s order outlines new regulations for the NCAA to have more control over eligibility and the transfer portal, in addition to name, image and likeness (NIL). The order also calls for the protection of women’s and Olympic sports under these new rules.

The rules in the order are slated to become effective on Aug. 1, encouraging updates to policies to make the new regulations happen and invalidating any state laws that conflict with the order. For enforcement, an institution would risk a reduction of federal funding if it were to break the rules in the order.

So what does this all mean for Penn State Athletics? Here’s what to know.

Eligibility

Current NCAA eligibility rules feature a five-year window to play in four seasons. While Trump suggests keeping a five-year window, his order states that athletes could play in five seasons.

The order says that this promotes “fairness, consistency, safety and opportunities for student-athletes.” It also states that professional athletes can’t return to collegiate athletics.

Mike Rhoades commented on players in the NBA Summer League and G League returning to college basketball on Dec. 31. While he said he wasn’t mad at college coaches who signed a former professional player as the NCAA allowed it, he wanted the NCAA to have standards and ways to enforce those standards.

“I just wish there were people who are in charge that said we're going to stand for this and we're going to fight for this because this is who we are,” Rhoades said. “We just don't have that right now.”

Trump’s order provides limited exceptions to the five-year eligibility window rule, including military service, missionary service and other periods of absence from participation that are in the public interest.

Transfer portal

The order outlines new rules regarding the transfer portal, featuring the fact that players could only transfer once in their five-year window without having to sit out a year. A player could transfer an additional time without penalty if they were to earn a four-year degree.

If a player were to transfer a second time without having already earned a four-year degree, they would have to sit out a year. The order doesn’t immediately change the transfer rule, which is noteworthy as the men’s basketball transfer portal officially opens on Tuesday, in addition to the women’s basketball transfer portal opening on Monday.

In other words, this order would revert the NCAA back to the one-time transfer rule, preventing situations where a player would transfer to a new school every year. For example, former Penn State men’s basketball player Kachi Nzeh, who’s also played for Xavier and Little Rock, will be in search of his fourth team in four years as he recently announced plans to enter the transfer portal.

Meanwhile, a transfer player like men’s basketball forward Josh Reed would abide by the rules in Trump’s order. Reed played his first three seasons at Cincinnati before transferring to Penn State for his final year of eligibility in 2025-26.

NIL

The order also explains rules for NIL, banning the use of improper financial activities. Institutions can’t use federal funds for NIL or revenue-sharing payments, and institutions can’t be a part of a fraudulent NIL scheme.

Trump’s order states that a fraudulent NIL scheme means a school can’t pay for NIL services that are above the actual fair market value. This includes the use of collectives or similar entities. The order also says that a school can’t tortiously interfere with a contract between a student-athlete and another institution.

Women’s and Olympic sports

Trump’s order says that the current state of collegiate athletics is chaotic, and it jeopardizes women’s and Olympic sports as focus and resources shift more toward football and men’s basketball.

As such, the order calls for adequate protection of women’s and Olympic sports by preserving or expanding scholarships and collegiate athletic opportunities.

In revenue sharing, Penn State Athletics reported that it spent $110,000 on women’s basketball, $95,000 on men’s hockey, $50,000 on men’s lacrosse, $10,000 on men’s tennis and $10,000 on women’s volleyball during the 2024-25 fiscal year.

The Nittany Lions have seen notable results in women’s athletics in recent years, such as women’s volleyball winning the 2024 national championship and women’s hockey making the Frozen Four this past season.

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Mock draft roundup | Where are Penn State players projected weeks away from NFL draft?
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
It is officially the month of the 2026 NFL Draft, which also means the height of mock draft season is here with the NFL Scouting Combine and most pro days in the rear-view mirror.

ESPN recently released a full seven-round mock draft, while NFL Network has dropped its five-round mock. Plenty more are sure to come as stocks rise and fall in the lead-up to April 23rd.

Here’s where some former Penn Staters landed in the most recent mock drafts.

Vega Ioane, offensive lineman, 1st round

In ESPN’s mock by Matt Miller, Vega Ioane landed with the Carolina Panthers at pick No. 19. With two guards over 29 years old, the Panthers need some stability at the position for the future to protect Bryce Young, which could make Ioane a good fit.

Ioane has been a projected first-round pick in nearly every mock draft this offseason, as his stock soared after two great years in 2024 and 2025. But it remains unclear where in the first round he could end up, as some see him going in the top-15, while others have him being selected a bit later.

CBS’ Ryan Wilson did a three-round mock draft this week as well, where Ioane went to the Baltimore Ravens with the 14th overall pick.

Drew Allar, quarterback, Day 2-3

There’s no denying the arm talent and potential Drew Allar possesses, but he’s seen more as a project at quarterback rather than an immediate fix for a quarterback-needy team.

With that comes a second round projection. Miller had him going 61st overall to the Los Angeles Rams, which would be a near-perfect fit — Allar could learn for a year or two under Matthew Stafford while being coached by one of the best offensive minds in the NFL, Sean McVay.

The projections for Allar remain a bit unclear, however, as Wilson didn’t have Allar going in the top three rounds and had Carson Beck going before him. On Friday, NFL Network’s Chad Reuter projected Allar to the Rams as well, but in the fifth round, 157th overall.

Zakee Wheatley, safety, Day 2

Safety Zakee Wheatley is projected in nearly every mock draft as a Day 2 pick, and ESPN has him being selected in the third round by the Carolina Panthers with pick No. 83.

Wilson, meanwhile, has Wheatley going in the second round to the Seattle Seahawks in a mock trade with the Pittsburgh Steelers. Considering Wheatley tested very well at Penn State’s pro day, teams could definitely spend their second-round pick on the Nittany Lions’ “Turnover King.”

Some experts still believe he could slide further down in the draft, though, as Reuter has him going eight picks before Allar to the Cleveland Browns.

Dani Dennis-Sutton, defensive end, Day 2-3

Dani Dennis-Sutton is similar to Allar, as it’s a bit unclear where he’ll end up being picked.

ESPN has Dennis-Sutton going in the fourth round at pick No. 112 to the Dallas Cowboys, while Wilson has him going in the third to the New England Patriots with pick No. 95. Reuter has the highest projection of the trio at No. 59 overall to the Houston Texans.

There’s no denying Dennis-Sutton is an athletic freak; it’s just a matter of how much teams value his production at Penn State, as there were times he ghosted and didn’t necessarily live up to expectations on occasion.

Nick Singleton, running back, 4th round

In Miller’s mock, Nick Singleton is selected six picks before Dennis-Sutton by the Texans. In Reuter’s, it’s pick No. 124 to the Jacksonville Jaguars.

Singleton is pretty much expected to go somewhere around the fourth round after a severe dip in production last season. His athletic abilities and past years are still more than enough for a team to want him, though.

Other late round projections

Miller has two Nittany Lions going in the fifth round, with Kaytron Allen being picked by the Steelers 161st overall and Drew Shelton going to the Denver Broncos nine picks later at No. 170.

The lone other former Penn Stater to be selected was Zane Durant, who wound up going in the seventh round to the Cincinnati Bengals, leaving the Nittany Lions with eight draft picks in Miller’s full mock.

Reuter has Durant barely slotting into the fourth round as a compensatory selection to the Philadelphia Eagles.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball continues offensive onslaught, dominates Maryland in five innings
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
After Penn State secured a mercy-rule victory over Maryland on Friday, the Nittany Lions returned to Beard Field at Nittany Lion Softball Park for the second game of the three-game series.

The blue and white dominated all game and earned a 16-4 win in five innings over the Terrapins.

Junior Bridget Nameth started on the mound for Penn State. She secured the first out, but Maryland’s Sami Woods knocked a home run, putting the Terrapins up 1-0.

With the Terrapins starting off hot, Mariah Penta worked a walk and the next two batters reached base, giving Nemeth trouble with the bases loaded in the first frame.

Everything was looking up for the Terrapins, as Zayda Rocke hit a line drive to secure a run for the Terrapins, making the score 2-0. The bases were still loaded for the Terrapins as they looked to send more home, but Caroline Fox grounded out, and a strikeout ended the inning with just two runs.

Breanna Hanik led things off with a line drive base hit. After Michela Barbanente got hit, Broke Klosowicz provided the big hit, sending a ball to the outfield for a two-run single after an error from Rocke, tying the score 2-2.

The Terrapins came into the second inning with as much energy as the first and Caitlyn Cornwell earned a walk, and a bunt single from Murphy gave Nemeth more trouble. However, Woods’ bunt attempt wasn’t as successful. Maryland regained the lead with a two-RBI double from Penta.

After the extra-base hit, Penn State coach Clarisa Crowell brought in the first pitcher from the bullpen, Mackenzie Duncan. The freshman proceeded to strike out both hitters after ending the reign of the Terrapins.

Klosowicz stepped up to the plate with pressure on her back with two outs on the board. The pressure didn’t get to her as she laced a line drive to left field, putting two runners on.

Alison Oneacre was up next and hit a line drive to third, and sent Hanik home, making the score 4-3.

Duncan had a flawless inning, allowing no hits, leaving the Terrapins scoreless to start the third. Cara Bohner stepped up to the plate, and hit it to the outfield, making it to second. Crowell swapped her out and put in pinch-runner Frankee Flesher on second instead.

Flesher wasted no time, stealing third, putting Penn State just a base away from tying the game. However, on a Gradishar hit in play, Flesher was thrown out at home. Despite Crowell challenging the play for obstruction, it was unsuccessful, ending the third inning without any runs.

Duncan maintained her dominant outing, restricting the Terrapins from scoring any runs, allowing the bats to get back up.

Barbanente stepped up and ripped a triple, immediately putting a runner in scoring position. Klosowicz brought Barbanente home with a single to the game, 4-4. Oneacre followed up with a walk, and Jordan George got to the plate and hit it to midfield, leaving all the bases loaded for the Nittany Lions.

Natalie Lieto broke the tie with a base hit, scoring just one runner to give the blue and white a 5-4 lead. Back-to-back walks scored two runs, and a two-RBI double from Madison Seidel kept the inning going. A Hanik RBI hit on an error, and a Klosowicz walk scored two more runs, before George ripped a bases-clearing double.

Liteto kept things going again in the fourth, as she sent a ball over the wall for a two-run homer. It was an offensive onslaught in the fourth inning, as the Nittany Lions scored 13 runs.

Duncan returned to the mound and produced yet another scoreless frame, ending the game in five innings with a 16-4 win.

Penn State continued its dominance in the series with a 16-4 win over Maryland after its mercy-rule victory to open the series, as well.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field continues success at Florida Relays
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Fresh off a record-breaking weekend at the Raleigh Relays and Knights Invite, Penn State was back at it this weekend.

The Nittany Lions competed in the Florida Relays on Friday and Saturday, competing against some of the top athletes in the SEC and across the nation.

Friday

After earning personal bests in his first two meets of the season, Collin Burkhart earned another improvement on Friday.

Burkhart recorded the third-best hammer throw in school history with a mark of 67.53 meters for second place.

In his third collegiate meet, freshman Nolan Carey finished fifth in the men's javelin, throwing 64.32 meters. Meanwhile, senior Audrey Friedman finished 10th in the women's javelin, throwing 44.95 meters.

In her second 200-meter with the Nittany Lions, Marie-Eloise Leclair finished seventh, running 23.14 seconds.

Ryan Watson ended the day in the men's 1,500-meter invite, running 3:43.33 for 10th and a personal best.

Saturday

After several top marks were set on Friday, even greater things were in store for the Nittany Lions on Saturday.

Freshman Bryce Thompson earned a massive personal best in the men's shot put, throwing 18.36 meters for second overall.

In the women's 800 meters, sophomore Tayissa Buchanan finished 14th overall in 2:04.36, setting the sixth-best mark in school history.

In the men's 800m, four Nittany Lions finished in the top 10, led by Yukichi Ishii in fifth place in 1:46.91.

North Carolina transfer Andrew Regnier competed in his first race as a Penn Stater, finishing eighth in a personal-best 1:47.15, followed by Kaleb Nastari’s personal-best 1:47.38 and Niko Schultz in 10th in 1:47.57.

Freshman Trip Campbell continued his standout first year, jumping 2.04 meters for fourth place in the men's high jump.

In the men's shot put, Ryan Henry improved his eighth-best distance in school history, throwing 18.85 meters for fourth place. Dylan Lambrecht earned the 10th-best throw in school history, finishing fourth in 18.85 meters.

Sophomore Jake Palermo improved his third-best time in school history in the 400-meters, finishing second in 45.29 seconds.

The Nittany Lions wrapped up the Florida Relays in the 100-meters, as Leclair ran the fourth-best 100m in school history in 11.56 seconds for 20th place. Sophomore Ajani Dwyer won the men's 100m with a school record 10.06 seconds to win the race.

Up next

Penn State will compete in the Bison Outdoor Classic on April 11 and 12.

MORE TRACK AND FIELD COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball sweeps Charleston, earns road win
author: Liana Tiamzon I For the Collegian
description:
Riding momentum from a dominant weekend, the Penn State Nittany Lions continued their push through conference play and prepared to face off against the Charleston Golden Eagles after their swap match against Ohio State and NJIT. The blue and white tonight in an anticipated EIVA matchup.

Penn State continued its strong stretch of conference play with a straight-set win over Charleston on Friday night, sweeping the match 25-14, 25-21, 25-17.

The Nittany Lions controlled the match from the start, setting the tone with an efficient performance and a dominant presence at the net. They hit .338 as a team while holding Charleston to just .062. The blue and white highlighted a clear advantage in both execution and defensive pressure.

Senior outside hitter Sean Harvey led the way and walked away from this match with 10 kills, while Matthoew Luoma added a team high 11 kills and four service aces. Gaige Gabriel contributed six kills on an efficient .625 hitting percentage. This helped Penn State maintain a balanced offensive attack.

At the net, middle blocker Owen Rose ranked No. 1 in blocks per set in the NCAA, was the anchor to the defense with seven block assists, the Nittany Lions totaled 14 team blocks, and consistently limited Charleston’s offensive opportunities.

Penn State jumped out early in the first set and never looked back. It cruised to a 25-24 win behind strong serves and multiple scoring runs. The second set proved more competitive with the Golden Eagles briefly closing the gap late, but the Nittany Lions responded quickly with key kills and a service ace to secure the 25-21 victory.

The Nittany Lions regained full control in the third set, pulled away midway, and closed out the match 25-17 to complete the sweep.

Charleston struggled offensively throughout the match and finished with 26 kills and 21 errors while hitting just .062 as a team. Kai Cousins led the Golden Eagles with seven kills, while Lucas Torres added five to their record.

Penn State historically controlled the series against the Golden Eagles. They are holding a 20-1 record since the two programs first met in 2016. The Nittany Lions have been especially dominant away from home, going undefeated in the road matchups against Charleston.

This win continues Penn State’s momentum following its undefeated week against Ohio State and NJIT, as the Nittany Lions strengthen their position in the EIVA standings heading into the final stretch of the season.

Up Next

Penn State faces Charleston again, and the Golden Eagles and Nittany Lions return to the court at 3 p.m. Saturday, April 4th, in the Wehrie Innovation Center.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s gymnastics ends season at Tempe Regional
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 20 Penn State spent Thursday night at the regional second round of the NCAA women’s championship in Tempe looking for a shot at a national title, but its season instead came to a close.

The Nittany Lions ended their 2026 season with a third-place finish, falling to No. 3 Florida and No. 14 California, and landing just ahead of No. 32 Arizona State and No. 33 Arizona. This meet was the regional second round, with the Gators and the Golden Bears moving on to the regional final.

Points were incredibly tight on Thursday, with Florida and California staying neck and neck with each other for most of the meet. Penn State was not letting those two teams go with a wide point margin, and the Nittany Lions ended with a less than .300 gap between them and California.

Arizona and Arizona State were also quite close the whole night, ending with only .003 points between them and only .045 between fourth-place Arizona State and the blue and white.

This meet was one for the Nittany Lions to completely leave it all out there, and they really pulled out their best tricks. All four events were a show for the team, and the floor was incredibly strong for Penn State. With the second-highest team score of the night, 49.150, Penn State was beam-focused. Alyssa Kramer had the highest score of 9.875 and Elizabeth Leary, Kalea McElligot, and Jessica Johanson all got 9.825s.

Vault was also pretty solid across the lineup, with Ava Piedrahita putting up a 9.850 and Jessica Johanson coming in close behind with a 9.825. Elizabeth Leary continued her high scoring with a 9.800, and the team ended with an even 49.025 on vault.

Bars are usually the highest scoring apparatus for the Nittany Lions, but the beam overshadowed the rest. Bars saw a confident lineup, lower scores than usual, but solid scores nonetheless. The highest scores from the blue and white came from Ava Cash and Elizabeth Leary, who both got a 9.825. These contributed to the blue and white’s team score of 48.800.

Beam was the highest team score of the night with a collective 49.225. Elizabeth Leary and Dani Latronica were the stars on the beam for the Nittany Lions, both grabbing 9.900s. Ellie Monahan grabbed a 9.850 and Kalea McElligot had a 9.800 to round out the top scores on beam for Penn State.

There was also personal record-breaking going on during this meet, with Elizabeth Leary breaking her 9.800 high score on beam with a 9.900. Dani Latronica continued her 9.900 or above scoring on beam by getting her seventh 9.900 of the season.

There was a confident and consistent score sheet for the Nittany Lions, with a lot of the scores coming out of this weekend either matching or breaking personal or season records. The blue and white saw a less-than-ideal end to its season, but looking back to the beam scores that were put up early on in the season, there has been a lot of overall improvement.

This meet gave a look into what the 2027 season could look like. With the majority of the team having at least a year of eligibility left, there can be a lot of growth happening. Penn State heads back to State College with a new idea of what it needs to do to get to the top.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: President Donald Trump signs executive order for collegiate athletics
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
President Donald Trump signed an executive order on Friday to attempt to regulate collegiate athletics.

Notably, the order calls for the establishment of clear, consistent and fair eligibility limits and structure transfer rules, the White House posted on its website. The order also calls for new rules regarding name, image and likeness (NIL).

The order will allow players up to five years of collegiate eligibility with the chance to transfer once and not have to sit out a year. A player could transfer an additional time with immediate eligibility as long as they earned a four-year degree.

Trump's order prohibits improper financial activities, such as intentionally devising or participating in a fraudulent NIL scheme. The order says that those schemes mean paying for NIL services that are above the actual fair market value.

In addition, the order calls for the protection of women's and Olympic sports during this new era of collegiate athletics. Policies for the actions in the order are set to become effective on Aug. 1, encouraging legislation to get passed to address these issues.

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Column | Tanisha Wright, Pat Kraft, returning Penn State women’s basketball to its roots
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
When Tanisha Wright and Pat Kraft took the podium for her introductory press conference on March 23, both echoed, “restoring the excellence that this program experienced over the last 60 years.”

For Wright, that vision is something very close to her, as she contributed to the program's success as a player in the early 2000s.

During her time on the court with the Lady Lions, Wright made four straight NCAA Tournament appearances, including a spot in the Elite Eight. The West Mifflin, Pennsylvania, native also had individual success, winning three straight Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year awards.

However, it’s more than just Wright that the team needs to succeed. This year’s coaching staff had five assistant coaches along with coach Carolyn Kieger.

That’s where Wright has begun to establish her team’s identity with just one hire.

On Wednesday, Wright announced Penn State’s all-time leading scorer, Kelly Mazzante, as the first assistant coach on her squad, creating a duo of two of the top-10 athletes in Lady Lion history.

While Mazzante’s recent success is boastful after numerous promotions at Georgetown and Bucknell, it can be argued her biggest attribute is her hunger for Penn State.

Kieger always spoke about her team being prideful and juiceful and putting the jersey in a better place than when they arrived. However, neither she nor her assistant coaches graduated from Penn State, let alone played for the blue and white.

While Kieger tried to generate that pride during her seven years at Penn State, Wright and Mazzante have that pride and hunger already built up inside them from their playing careers.

Wright and Mazzante know Lady Lion basketball as one of the best teams in the country, earning the previously mentioned four tournament appearances. During that time, they also earned a pair of Big Ten championships and built a 77-27 record during their three years together.

As Wright continues to build her staff, one would assume she’d continue the trend with Mazzante and add former Lady Lions who lived and breathed Penn State.

After Mazzante, who’s the top scorer in school history, Maggie Lucas sits just behind and has spent the last three years as an assistant coach with Bowling Green. The No. 3 scorer is Susan Robinson-Fruchtl, who works for Penn State Athletics and was previously a collegiate head coach. No. 5 is Teniya Page, who works in the NBA and coached an elite basketball camp, and Suzie McConnell is a WNBA Coach of the Year and an 11-year veteran NCAA coach.

While the list goes on and on, including former Penn State assistant coach Maren Walseth, who’s now at every home game as the co-radio host, Wright has an immense arsenal of former teammates and alumni who have experienced coaching careers.

With a near-full sweep following eight players entering the transfer portal as of Friday, Wright might as well commit to rebuilding the program from the bottom up, the Penn State way.

If she wants to live up to her promises and rebuild the program to where it was, hiring more former players who put the program in its prime might be the way to go.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey earns multiple USCHO yearly honors
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has piled in some yearly honors following the program's run to the Frozen Four.

Jeff Kampersal was named the USCHO Coach of the Year after guiding the Nittany Lions to a 33-6 record. Forward Tessa Janecke earned first team honors, while defenseman Danica Maynard was tabbed to the third team and rookie team.

After leading Penn State to its greatest season in program history, Jeff Kampersal is the 𝐔𝐒𝐂𝐇𝐎 𝐂𝐨𝐚𝐜𝐡 𝐨𝐟 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 👏📰: https://t.co/fG6FI0HHNB#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/jbwfTpijaS— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) April 3, 2026

It's another award for Kampersal as he was named the CCM/AHCA Women’s University Division Coach of the Year prior to the Frozen Four. He also passed the 500 career win milestone against Delaware on Jan. 23.

The legendary Tessa Janecke adds another program milestone to her name as the first Nittany Lion to be on the 𝐀𝐥𝐥-𝐔𝐒𝐂𝐇𝐎 𝐅𝐢𝐫𝐬𝐭 𝐓𝐞𝐚𝐦 🐐#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/TsszzRw9gJ— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) April 3, 2026

Janecke was a finalist for the Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award in addition to becoming the AHA Player and Forward of the Year. She's the program's all-time leading scorer and won a gold medal with Team USA in the 2026 Milan Cortina Olympics.

One of the greatest seasons by a freshman in program history earns Dani 𝐀𝐥𝐥-𝐔𝐒𝐂𝐇𝐎 𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐫𝐝 𝐓𝐞𝐚𝐦 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐑𝐨𝐨𝐤𝐢𝐞 𝐓𝐞𝐚𝐦 honors 🙌#WeAre #HockeyValley pic.twitter.com/vlajYseo6o— Penn State Women’s Hockey (@PennStateWHKY) April 3, 2026

Maynard was the AHA's Rookie of the Year and made the all-conference second team. She was a team-high +45 while on the ice this past season, tallying 24 assists and nine goals.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse set to travel for matchup against Ohio State
author: Aeryn Ajot | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State embarks this weekend for Columbus, Ohio, to face the Buckeyes in its first match in April. Happy Valley’s record has been hurt as of late, with a 9-3 mark, all from recent losses, culminating in a three-week losing streak. Like past losses, this weekend marks another ranked team to face and another heated rivalry to showcase skill and talent.

Is this the week for the blue and white woes to end?

Series history

Penn State has been competing with Ohio State since 1997, and has met almost every year since. In a total of 34 games, the Nittany Lions have a historical edge with 24 wins to 10 losses. In the past 10 years, the team has had 11 matches, with an almost even split of 6 wins and 5 losses.

This upcoming game will make another point about whether the blue and white can prove themselves against a ranked Big Ten school, or if the past will be precedent for their performance.

2026 Ohio State
