Compared to the Nittany Lions, the Buckeyes have competed far less. However, they’ve produced a similar imperfect record of 7-4.

Ohio State dropped its season opener against Pitt, which Penn State defeated earlier this year, then suffered its second defeat in huge fashion, 22-9 to North Carolina. The scarlet and gray got back into the win column for the next consecutive weeks, before dropping back-to-back contests to Rutgers and John Hopkins.

However, the Buckeyes are on a two-game winning streak, giving them momentum. Although the previous team they competed against was far less competitive, this momentum will help carry their confidence into the matchup this weekend, a momentum the blue and white do not have currently.

2026 Penn State

In the past few weeks, the Lions have gained a losing streak that has harmed their rankings. Previously in the top 10, Penn State has dropped a few places to No. 20.

Still, Ohio State is unranked and has only received marginal votes for the polls. Competitors Penn State has faced are much more competitive as well, including previously ranked Pittsburgh and Cornell.

The confidence in the Nittany Lions has waned as their losses have piled up, diminishing the predictability of a win. Past history has said it could go either way, but if current records hold any indication of success, Penn State has the upper hand, having competed against tougher competitors.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Inclement weather changes Penn State's baseball schedule against Nebraska
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is no longer facing No. 19 Nebraska at 7 p.m. Friday due to inclement weather.

Instead, the Nittany Lions and the Cornhuskers will begin their three-game series with a doubleheader on Saturday, starting at 3 p.m. Game 2 will start approximately 25 minutes after the conclusion of Game 1.

🚨Weekend Schedule Adjustment🚨Due to inclement weather, our series at No. 19 Nebraska will now begin with a doubleheader tomorrow at 3 p.m. ET.No game today.#WeAre pic.twitter.com/sJJPz4d69J— Penn State Baseball (@PennStateBASE) April 3, 2026

The two teams will round out their series with Game 3 at 1 p.m. Sunday. All three games will be played at Hawks Field at Haymarket Park in Lincoln, Nebraska, and can be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

Penn State will bring an 8-18 record into the series, including 3-6 in Big Ten play. Meanwhile, Nebraska sits at 23-6 overall and 8-1 in conference play.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball opens series against Maryland with mercy-rule victory
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State returned to Happy Valley after a series sweep over Rutgers and a walkoff loss to Pitt during the week. The Nittany Lions met Maryland for a three-game series at Beard Field at Nittany Lion Softball Park.

The blue and white's bats came alive, earning the 13-5 victory in five innings over the Terrapins.

To start off its second Big Ten home series of the season, Penn State’s women's hockey captain and Team USA gold medalist, Tessa Janecke, threw the first pitch to start Friday afternoon's game.

Freshman right-hander Brevyn Kellepouris got the start in the circle, getting a 1-2-3 inning. Junior third baseman Brooke Klosowicz got the first walk of the game, shortly after freshman shortstop Allison Onearce walked, but nothing came in the first inning.

The Terrapins opened the second inning with a spark, with two on, then Jaylee Williams singled to left center, allowing a run to score. Caityln Cornwell on her first pitch singled to left, getting the bases loaded.

Coach Clarisa Crowell put junior McKenna Young in the circle for relief with only one out and a run scored. Caroline Fox scored on a sacrifice fly, giving Maryland a 2-0 lead.

Natalie Lieto got things started for the blue and white as her second pitch of the at bat, she hit a single up the middle. Freshman Gabby Gradisar followed a fielder’s choice with a walk.

Getting to the top of the order, Breanna Hanik got hit by a pitch to get bases loaded with two outs, and a walk to Michela Barbanente brought in the first run for the blue and white.

Young got her first strikeout of the game in the top of the third, but an error from Klosowicz and an infield single brought in another run, giving the Terrapins a 3-1 lead.

Onearce started things off in the bottom of the third with a single up the middle, and Jordan George followed up with a hit, as well. An error from Maryland. Cara Bohner walked to get the bases loaded with only one out.

Gabby Gradishar stepped up to the batter's box and on her second pitch she hit a grand slam, giving Penn State the 5-3 lead 5-3.

Madison Seidel worked a walk and Hanik hit the second home run of the game to left field, making it 7-3. Still with only one out, Oneacre hit the third homer of the inning, making it 9-3 after Klosowicz reached base on a four-pitch walk. The blue and white scored eight runs in the bottom of the third.

Sophomore Abigail Britton made her way to the circle to start off the fourth inning. Maryland’s Sammi Woods singled up the middle, then Mariah Penta hit into a fielder’s choice that got Woods out at second.

After a Britton walk, Matti Benson roped a double, scoring both runners on base. Maryland closed the gap, 9-5.

The bottom of the order didn’t reach on a hit, but two stood on base after walks. Hanik launched her second homer of the game to left field, giving the Nittany Lions a 12-5 lead. Barbanente followed with a solo homer, making it 13-5 with two outs in the fourth. Klosowicz worked her third walk of the game, and a Oneacre double set up a potential for more runs. However, a groundout from Kalli Karwowski ended the inning. The Nittany Lions scored four runs in the fourth, making the game 13-5.

Junior Bridget Nemeth came into the circle to start the fifth inning. She worked a scoreless inning, ending the game in just five innings with an eight-run lead.

Penn State earned the series-opening victory with a 13-5 win over Maryland.

Up Next

The Nittany Lions and Terrapins will return to the field for a doubleheader beginning at 11 a.m. Saturday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Get to know Penn State wrestling’s 2026 signing class
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State ended another historic season where it broke the record for most points for the third straight season while also sending six wrestlers to the national finals.

What makes it more historic is only one of the 10 starters was a senior in Levi Haines, leading to likely minimal roster differences. With this, the Nittany Lions have a small recruiting class of four signees, adding more depth to the already deep lineup.

Here’s a breakdown of each 2026 signee before they fully enter the Penn State wrestling room.

Braiden Weaver

Braiden Weaver will join Penn State as an early depth piece in a jammed-up part of the lineup. Weaver wrestled his final season for Altoona High School at 133 pounds, likely setting him up for that weight or 141 pounds with the Nittany Lions.

Weaver is a wrestler who’s had tons of success in high school, finishing his career with a 153-28 record across four different weight classes. He began as a freshman at 107 pounds and consistently grew until 133 pounds for his senior campaign.

Weaver never finished higher than third at the state championships, earning fifth in his final season, but was always in the finals in the district and regional championships.

It’ll be difficult for Weaver to see immediate action, as he’ll be behind the likes of Marcus Blaze, Masanosuke Ono and Nate Desmond. Weaver could be the backup at 133 pounds, with Desmond likely sticking at 141 pounds as the backup next season, but it’s all dependent on his growth when he enters the wrestling room.

Sam Herring

Likely up just one weight class, Sam Herring enters differently than the other three wrestlers. Herring has often been in Instagram posts with Hodge Trophy winner Mitchell Mesenbrink and is in the Penn State directory, listed as a member, indicating he could’ve enrolled early.

Herring didn’t wrestle in his final season with Bishop McCort, finishing his career in three seasons with a 89-17 record. In one year of postseason competition, Herring finished in first at the regionals and district championships, but ended in third place at the state tournament in Hersey, Pennsylvania, in 2025.

He’s the No. 21 recruit in the 2026 class in the last update of the FloWrestling 2026 Big Board rankings, and is listed at 138 pounds. He likely turns into either a 141 or 149-pound wrestler at Penn State and would likely be another depth piece early on.

Herring could be another backup option at 141 pounds, or could fall behind Shayne Van Ness at 149 pounds in Van Ness’ final collegiate season.

Jayden James

The highest-ranked recruit of the group, Jayden James, will enter Happy Valley with a lot of success. James flipped from Virginia Tech to Penn State just over a year ago.

Since then, James earned a U17 World Championship title while being named the New Jersey State Wrestler of the Year for the second consecutive season.

The No. 3 recruit in the class, James has a big victory over the top recruit in the 2027 class, Melvin Miller. The Virginia Tech commit met James in the finals of the 2025 Ironman Tournament, where James came out on top with an 8-5 win in sudden victory.

Wrestling for the Delbarton School, he’s had a 74-0 record over the last two seasons, and dominated his way to his second consecutive state title. In his five matches at the state tournament, he earned three tech falls and two falls.

In his final match in high school, James competed at the Pittsburgh Wrestling Classic for the United States All-Star Team at 160 pounds. James took down Reagan Milheim, the No. 44 recruit, by a 17-10 decision.

James will enter the Nittany Lion wrestling room with a chance to compete in his first season. It’s unknown whether he’ll be at 165 pounds or continue to grow to 174 pounds. Depending on the movement of weights from the current roster, he could be in competition for the 174-pound spot with William Henckel.

He has the pedigree and the dominance that a lot of recent top Penn State recruits have had, including PJ Duke, Blaze and Luke Lilledahl, to be high up on the podium early into his collegiate career.

Elijah Brown

Elijah Brown was a late addition to the recruiting class. After decommitting from Pitt on Oct. 1, he announced his commitment and signing with Penn State on Nov. 12.

Wrestling for Belle Vernon, Brown had a dominant high school career. After a 35-13 record in his freshman season at 152 pounds, he lost seven matches in his next three seasons.

As he continued to grow and get bigger, he wrestled his final two years at 215 pounds, earning a 94-2 record. He won two straight PIAA State Championships and will be a strong competitor to Cole Mirasola. In his final season, he had 27 falls, proving to be dominant in the top position.

Brown will likely continue to grow. Whether he’s a full heavyweight or not is yet to be seen, but he comes in as the No. 77 wrestler in the class.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Hometown hero Nick Sloff continues Penn State track and field career with historic indoor season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
While junior Nick Sloff is now the face of the Penn State men’s distance squad, that wasn’t the star runner’s original plan.

Growing up in State College, Sloff was surrounded by Penn State track and field as he ran several meets at the Horace Ashenfelter III Indoor Track and was coached by multiple program alumni. Despite this, Sloff never intended to compete collegiately, let alone in his own back yard.

“That was never really the goal this whole time,” Sloff told The Daily Collegian. “I didn’t really enjoy running as much as I do now.”

As a local, Sloff knew he would attend Penn State whether or not he competed on the team. As his high school career progressed, the young runner began to see more success on the track, increasing his potential to compete beyond his time at State College Area High School.

“When he came back from (an injury junior year), he was just on fire,” State College head coach Artie Gilkes told the Collegian. “You could see it in his workouts and races, and the trajectory he was on was pretty remarkable.”

Sloth used his Nittany Lion network to his advantage, building connections with Penn State’s as he explored the possibility of donning the blue and white.

“(Artie) had great things to say about Nick all through his high school career,” Penn State coach John Gondak told the Collegian. “Coach (Ryan) Foster and I went to watch him a couple times during his senior year, and we were super impressed. After that, we were like, ‘If Penn State’s where you want to be, we’ll have a spot for you.’”

After leaving State High as a four-time district champion and member of the 2022 state championship relay team, Sloff redshirted his freshman year.

The State College native didn’t compete with the Nittany Lions during Year 1, but had the opportunity to practice with an elite group at Penn State.

“It’s really good to have a good team because it makes running every day so much easier,” Sloff said. “It’s good to just have a fun group to be with.”

The results of Sloff’s growth were evident to Gondak as he continued his first year with Penn State, competing unattached in multiple races.

“You could tell right away when he got here as a first year that he was very passionate about running,” Gondak said. “You could tell he had a very strong skill set, he liked to compete and he could close races hard, which are two things you need to be successful in this sport.”

Sloff’s time finally came in the fall of his sophomore season as he suited up for the blue and white, racing at six cross country meets.

In his first collegiate race, Sloff earned the win at the Dolan Duals, running a 17:55.1 in the 6,000-meters. Over the next two months, he set a pair of personal bests in the 8,000-meter and 10,000-meter as he finished the season 26th in the region.

“We’re trying to be the best we can be across all three seasons,” Gondak said. “Nick’s been a big part of helping cross country get closer and closer to making nationals, so he fits in very well with what we’re trying to do.”

As Sloff made his track debut the following spring, he earned a pair of top-12 finishes at the Indoor Big Ten Championship in the 3,000-meters and distance medley relay, followed by an appearance in the NCAA Division I East First Round during the outdoor season, competing in the 1,500-meters and 5,000m as one of the top-96 runners in the country.

Despite this growing success for Sloff, it wasn’t his eventual success on the track that he wanted to be known for. Rather, it was his role as a teammate that pushed him to be greater.

“I want to be known as a good friend and someone who people enjoyed running with,” Sloff said. “As someone who could be a good leader and push their teammates to be better as well.”

When the 2025 cross country season rolled around, Sloff was back to improving his times at each distance, including finishing sixth overall at the Mid-Atlantic Region Cross Country Championship with a nearly one-minute improvement from the year before.

Sloff’s sixth-place finish not only earned a personal best but qualified him for the NCAA championship, becoming the first runner from the men’s squad to compete at that level since 2017, a place Gilkes competed 31 years before when he donned the blue and white.

“It’s always an amazing story when someone from State High can come into the program and have the success like Nick is having,” Gondak said. “State High has a great tradition with their program, and I’m excited that they have a connection and an interest in helping kids get to our program, too.”

As Sloff’s progression continued during his sophomore year, he continued to set himself apart from a typical college student, not just in his running abilities, but also in his hobbies.

From his early high school days, Sloff took an interest in spelunking when he wasn’t on the track, practicing and competing.

“With many athletes you had to worry about video games, but for Nick it was don’t get lost in the cave when you go caving with your groups this weekend,” Gilkes said. “He had this sort of detached awareness.”

That detached awareness served as a major boost on the track, as Sloff ditched his 132nd-place finish from the cross country championships and went on a historic run during the indoor season.

After earning a personal best in the 5,000m and 1,000-meters — the then-10th-best time in NCAA history — he broke the school record in the 3,000m with a time of 7:48.55. Three weeks later, he broke it again, notching a 7:42.68.

In between those two races, however, Sloff took five seconds off his personal best in the mile as he broke that school record in 3:54.90 at the Penn State National Open.

While the coaching staff was ecstatic with his performance, Sloff’s detached unawareness continued as he seemed unfazed by his school-record race.

“Foster called me and gave the phone to Nick, who said, ‘Hey Artie, how ya doing. It was a good race, really just kinda normal,’” Gilkes said. “We’re going crazy and it’s just like another day to him.”

Sloff ultimately capped off his record-breaking season with a pair of top-five finishes at the Big Ten Championship, finishing fourth in the 3,000m in 8:01.94 and third in the 5,000m in 13:49.93.

While he fell just one spot shy of qualifying for the NCAA championship, Sloff’s career is far from over, competing in his sophomore year of eligibility with 2.5 more years left to make his mark.

“He’s the epitome of what you want in your program,” Gondak said. “He’s very excited about Penn State and wearing the blue and white, and he’s having success.”

MORE SPORTS COVERAGE

title: Staff picks | 4 ideas for the future of Penn State’s Blue-White Weekend
author: The Daily Collegian Football Staff
description:
As college football continues to evolve, Penn State is set to move even further away from a traditional spring.

Thursday, Penn State announced it’d be holding Blue-White Practice, set for 1 p.m. on Saturday, April 25, inside Beaver Stadium — a change from the typical event known as the Blue-White Game.

As James Franklin noted last year, and Pat Kraft and Matt Campbell made clear this season, the chance of completely ending Blue-White festivities is unlikely, citing the community impacts the event has. That doesn’t mean they can’t make some tweaks.

Here are four ideas Penn State could start with.

Avery Hill: Penn State all-sports weekend

If Penn State Winter Sports Weekend made anything clear, it’s that the athletic department must continue to take advantage of one of the most noteworthy stadiums and highly regarded fanbases in the country.

To do so means getting any and every sport linked to Penn State involved for Blue-White Weekend.

I still think the program would benefit from having its players in attendance, so a quick practice on Saturday should be in store. But additionally, the Nittany Lions should invite their biggest stars, past and present, to compete in front of tens of thousands of fans.

On Friday, Penn State could hold a legends flag football game, with one team featuring former football lettermen, like LaVar Arrington Sr. and Ki-Jana Carter. The other could include some of the greatest athletes to come from other sports, including gymnastics Olympian Stephen Nedoroscik and new women’s basketball head coach Tanisha Wright.

As Campbell continues his mission to show his players who came before them, it’d be a great chance to honor them.

Lyle Alenstein: Skills contest

If the future of the spring consists of not going live, then a skills contest could be the way to go.

Various competitions can include accuracy challenges for quarterbacks, pass-catchers and defensive backs navigating obstacle courses to catch balls from JUGS machines. Kickers can try and connect on field goals from various distances and punters can attempt to pin their boots inside the 20, 10 and 5-yard line.

On top of on-field football activity, there can be various team events where each side competes in 40-yard dash races and a bench press contest, seeing which side can put up the most cumulative weight.

This format could also allow for fan interaction, with random fans being picked to come down to the field and even try their hand at a few of the challenges. As for Saturday’s festivities, the program should continue to host events for the kids, holding a meet and greet and the combine before practice.

It’s a new era at Penn State, and Pat Kraft and company have a chance to take advantage of it in a huge way. The good thing for them is that the fanbase will show up for anything, and they know that.

Lexie Linderman: 7-on-7 tournament

I highly doubt Penn State will ever return to an 11-on-11 game format, but it could still have the feel of the previous Blue-White Game with a 7-on-7 tournament.

It would help limit injuries, as the spring always features some guys with bumps and bruises. A short tournament could still provide some fun for the players without existing injuries.

Each game would be one quarter long, and each team would begin its possessions from the opposing 40-yard line. No punts, so each team is forced to go for it on fourth down, and no kicking — only touchdowns.

The winner of the tournament could stay on the field postgame for autographs as well to draw more fan engagement.

Will Horstman: Flag football

Rather than an open practice to the public, Penn State should offer fans a more interesting event for Blue-White Weekends in the future. A flag football format could be the way to go, showcasing a scrimmage at the end of spring ball.

Flag football would give some resemblance to an actual game, which could draw more fans to be in attendance. The fanbase would get a bit of a sneak peek into the team before the season begins in the fall.

A flag football game wouldn’t put Penn State’s players at risk of much injury, and the intra-squad competition likely would be a great event for team bonding. As it’s just a flag football scrimmage, it wouldn't be very serious and could be a way for the team to have fun to close out the spring.

This format could create a better viewing experience for fans, keeping the Blue-White Weekend a popular event in the future.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Preview | No. 6 Penn State men’s lacrosse resumes Big Ten play with No. 14 Johns Hopkins on the road
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State rose to the No. 6 ranking on the USILA Coaches Poll after defeating then-No. 6 Ohio State, 13-6, on the road, improving to 2-0 in the Big Ten standings.

With a chance to maintain the perfect conference record, the Nittany Lions close out their two-game road trip against No. 14 Johns Hopkins at noon on Saturday.

Coach Jeff Tambroni’s squad enters Baltimore, Maryland, with a 6-3 overall record, having defeated some of the highest-ranked teams in the country including No. 4 Princeton, No. 10 Maryland and No. 12 Cornell.

Meanwhile, the Blue Jays mirror Penn State’s season record, sharing a pair of mutual opponents so far. Johns Hopkins is 1-1 in the Big Ten, beating Michigan, 14-8, but falling, 9-8, in double overtime to No. 13 Rutgers in the second game.

A comparison of mutuals

Penn State and Johns Hopkins have each faced Navy and No. 2 North Carolina.

While both teams fell to the Tarheels, the Nittany Lions held on for a tighter scoreline. On March 14, Penn State fell, 10-8, in a neck-and-neck battle throughout all four quarters. On the other hand, North Carolina handled business against the Blue Jays, cruising to a 17-9 victory at home.

The Big Ten rivals saw juxtaposing results against the Midshipmen, however. In Penn State’s fourth game of the season, a week after upsetting Princeton on the road, Navy took home a 12-11 victory in Annapolis, Maryland. Johns Hopkins broke free from Navy’s grasp with a six-goal third quarter, aiding its 14-10 victory over the Midshipmen.

Scouting the Blue Jays

The Blue Jays’ goal-scoring efforts are led by attackman Hunter Chauvette, who’s recorded a pair of six-goal games so far this year, totaling for 25 goals with two assists on the season. The first came against Robert Morris in Johns Hopkins’ 17-2 season-opening victory. Chauvette then scored a second “sock trick” against No. 16 Virginia on Feb. 28 — the Blue Jays pulled through, 14-13.

Midfielder Matt Collison, Johns Hopkins’ point leader with 29, scored the game-winning goal against the Cavaliers. The senior from Scarborough, Ontario, ranks second on the team with 19 goals and third on the team with 10 assists.

On defense, short-stick defensive midfielder Reece DiCicco leads the Blue Jays with 14 caused turnovers. Long-stick midfielder Luke Martin and defenseman Carson Brown each tallied seven caused turnovers through nine games. Brown’s 25 ground balls places second on the team behind DiCicco’s 31.

Senior Oran Gelinas and freshman Dash Lamitie have split time between the pipes with .580 and .480 save percentages, respectively. While Lamitie was the starting goaltender in the opening weeks of the season, Gelinas took over the role with a 12-save performance against Virginia. Currently, the Waltham, Massachusetts, native is riding the high of a 19-save outing in the loss to the Scarlet Knights.

Freshmen impact

Two of Penn State’s key contributors are true-freshmen: attackman Jack Iannantuono and goaltender Preston Hawkins.

Iannantuono is tied with junior Kyle Lehman for first on the team with 27 points, the freshman’s split between 14 goals and 13 assists. He had a four-point night against the Buckeyes with two goals and two helpers, setting the tone with the game-opening assist on Michael Faraone’s goal.

Hawkins made 10 saves on 16 shots against Ohio State, improving to a .514 save percentage and 9.05 goals-against average after being pulled in his first-career game in the season opener. Since then, Hawkins continued to gain momentum with an 11-save game against the Tigers and a 14-save performance to take down the Terrapins in the Big Ten opener.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘Sustainability for whom?’| New major invites students to question sustainability, explore environment
author: Aly Cromar I For the Collegian
description:
In February, Penn State launched a new major under the College of Earth and Mineral Sciences: sustainability, society and environmental geography. The program invites students to connect the dots between science, civilization and the ways individuals interact with the environment.

The new major is marketed towards students with an interest in environmental issues but don’t want a strictly technical or mathematical course of study.

Jared Whear, an assistant research professor and director of the new program, said the program emerged from a mix of ongoing environmental crises, growing job opportunities and student interest.

“If you pay attention to any place you go, the news or any class you take, this framework of sustainability comes up,” Whear said. “As a concept, it’s growing everywhere. There's a real need to know what sustainability is, how we measure it and how we can push for more of it.”

Within the geography department and the College of Earth and Mineral Sciences, faculty members saw room for a program that could prepare students for environmental careers while asking questions, according to Whear.

“A big question from Geography 340, one of the keystone courses of the new major, is ‘Sustainability for whom?’” Whear said. “When it comes down to it, we want these students to question sustainability, because the word gets so tossed around.”

Amaya Heffelfinger was drawn in by the curriculum.

Heffelfinger, a second-year studying sustainability, society and environmental geography, said Penn State was the only school she applied to without choosing an environmentally focused major. Before switching her major, she studied geography.

“I enjoy science, I do, but I’m not the best with the technical applications,” Heffelfinger said. “It kind of aligns more with what suits my interests and my capabilities.”

Heffelfinger said she was aware of the major before Penn State officially released it, as Whear recommended it to her. She moved quickly to declare it. She said the major fills a gap between environmental science and a more humanities-based education. It offers a different path forward for students who care deeply about the environment, but do not see themselves becoming engineers or spending all day in a lab.

“I think the fact that it focuses more on a humanities and social approach is really important,” Heffelfinger said. “Human and environmental relations are an overlap. They’re two sides of the same coin.”

Similarly, Mellisa Wright said the biggest difference is that the major is centered around “people, power and equity alongside environmental issues.”

“There are no easy fixes,” Wright, professor of geography and women, gender and sexuality studies, said. “We ask students to really dig into the social questions behind sustainability.”

Heffelfinger said the major could also push Penn State to think more deeply about its responsibilities as a land-grant university.

“If we can kind of lean more into those land-grant roots and become more environmentally involved as a college, I think that would be amazing,” Heffelfinger said.

Because sustainability is such a broad topic, Whear said students will study sustainability across many settings, be it on campus or in the State College Borough. He said field trips to places like water treatment facilities and recycling centers can help make lessons more hands-on.

At the moment, the program's biggest hurdle is entering the student body’s consciousness, according to Whear. The major remains in its earliest stages and has only just enrolled its second student. Still, he said he’s hopeful the program will continue to grow as more students learn of its existence.

Heffelfinger, Whear and Wright all said the major could fit a wide range of students, especially those who care about the environment but have not found the right academic lane elsewhere.

“If you’re someone who’s concerned about climate change, interested in justice and fairness and curious about how policies actually play out on the ground, this program gives you space to explore those questions seriously,” Wright said.

Heffelfinger offered simpler advice for anyone considering the major: “Do it. Why not? Just declare the major.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Review | Extravagant flavors with Fresh Bite Pizza
author: Talia Rittenhouse | The Daily Collegian
description:
There are many different styles of pizza awaiting you at Fresh Bite Pizza on 300 S. Pugh St.

From Detroit and New York-style pizza to wings, fries and more, Fresh Bite Pizza has got you covered with meals during regular daytime hours and even the haunted hours of 12 a.m to 3 a.m. on the weekends.

As a girl from Philadelphia, I have rare experiences with good pizza, except from trips to New Jersey or the Big Apple, so I was eager to find a new spot in downtown State College.

Let me tell you, Fresh Bite Pizza will not let you down with the unique flavors and welcoming workers who greet you with a smile on their faces. So, without further ado, let me share some menu items with you that were love at first slice — get it?

Cheese Pizza

Starting off with the classic cheese pizza — it’s consistent, reliable and everyone’s go-to pick.

The crust had a perfect mix of crunch and chew with each bite. The dough had a great taste to it that was complemented by the marinara sauce and the gooey, stringy mozzarella cheese.

You can really taste the tomato and seasonings in the sauce, reminding me of the pizzas I eat at the Jersey Shore — not too bland or powerful, but just right.

While I feel this may not be my favorite slice of cheese pizza I’ve ever had, it was still delicious and I will go back for more.

Rating: 8.5/10

Mac & Cheese Pizza

Yes, you heard me right. Everyone’s favorite childhood dish has made an appearance on a slice of pizza.

I didn’t expect to be as in love with this pizza as I was, considering the typical texture of macaroni and cheese, but it was flavorful and so cheesy. I’m already planning when to get my next slice.

The pasta on top was not too crunchy after being in the oven, and was covered with melted cheese and cheese sauce. This unique pizza will bring out your inner child, turning a less mature dish into something so creative and flavorful.

If you’re not a fan of cheesy foods, or macaroni and cheese for that matter, then this pizza may not be the right pick for you. As for my roommates and I, we were in love.

Rating 10/10

Hot Honey Pepperoni

I don’t know if it’s just me, or if I live under some rock, but I didn’t realize how big of a craze hot honey had become.

As you’ll read further, Fresh Bite has not only hot honey pepperoni pizza but also hot honey chicken fries.

I wasn’t a huge fan of this slice since I don’t enjoy pepperoni as is, but I’m sure others would find this to be a top pick, just not for the picky eater I can be at times.

The honey was very spicy but also very sweet, and if you’re a messy eater, be ready to grab the napkins for the sticky honey drizzled on top. There was cheese, sauce, pepperoni and drops of pesto dolloped across the surface of the slice, making the appearance very unique and intriguing.

Rating: 3/10

Hot Honey Chicken Fries

So I have to say, I was hesitant to try this since I didn’t love hot honey on pizza, but I was pleasantly surprised.

The fries are covered in melted cheese, hot honey and pieces of chicken — all of which are required for the perfect bite.

This dish is a bit messy, as the cheese melts all the ingredients together. I wouldn’t recommend eating with your fingers, just “take the L” and grab a fork.

I enjoyed the hot honey in this dish more than in the pizza, which may be because there are fewer flavors in the fries than in the pizza slice.

I think this could be the new loaded fries order of 2026, taking away the bacon and adding in some hot honey and chicken Fresh Bite just might be on to something.

Rating: 10/10

Final Thoughts

Fresh Bite Pizza offers many pizza flavors and other food options for customers to choose from. You can order a full pizza pie for a night with friends, or just a slice on the go if you need a quick bite.

Any meal here is worthwhile as they’re all filled with strong flavors and a delicious crust — Fresh Bite Pizza isn’t something you want to miss.

MORE BLOGS

title: ‘The best kept secret in town’ | Scraps & Skeins combines craft supply with inclusive employment
author: Ally Sons I For the Collegian
description:
From colorful fabrics and yarns to locally sourced donations and employment, Scraps & Skeins something for everyone. Guided by the mission to “empower, reimagine and reuse,” the State College craft supply store is weaving a unique place in the community.

Located at 3042 Enterprise Dr., Scraps & Skeins proudly employs people with disabilities and mental health conditions. Operating under the umbrella of Strawberry Fields, a nonprofit that supports people with disabilities, the store uses the motto “reuse is beautiful,” hoping to reduce waste and save textiles from the landfill.

The fabric store began as a simple idea between some of the “founding mothers” in the summer of 2014, inspired by a small boutique in Ithaca, New York. The group of women, whose children live in the Strawberry Fields group homes, came together to start the company.

Together, they grew from a pop-up stand at the Millheim Fiber Festival, to a rented room across the street from their present location and officially opened their current doors in January 2015.

Ellen Campbell is the former board president at Strawberry Fields and one of the founding mothers. She said Scraps & Skeins is a great opportunity for both Strawberry Fields clients and others in the community.

“One of the biggest needs for people with disabilities is employment,” Campbell said.

Along with Scraps & Skeins, Strawberry Fields also works with another local company called Good Day Cafe, located at 286 W. Hamilton Ave.

“The social enterprise is our business that is focused on employing people with handicaps,” Campbell said. “Our employees will go through the application process through Strawberry Fields, and our managers will make the selection.”

Scraps & Skeins began as a strictly volunteer-based store and donation center, but now has seven employees. Its hours have also expanded as time has passed, now open from 10 a.m to 2 p.m Tuesday through Thursday, and 10 a.m to 2 p.m every third Saturday. It also now has an online shop.

Campbell said the business’ goal is to provide employment opportunities and affordable supplies for the makers in Centre County and beyond.The donations are run primarily by Tammy Sue, who said customers bring in donations that are then weighed and sorted by fabric type.

“We’ve rescued, it must be now, 20 tons from the landfill,” Sue said.

Sue emphasized the importance of making sure customers have a good experience. As soon as the store’s bell rings twice, Sue said she makes sure to be out there to get the cart and book all of their donations.

Employees like Dottie Ewing, one of the store's managers, emphasized the importance of being out in the community and taking care of one’s mental health.

“It can positively impact you,” Ewing said.

Scraps & Skeins has seen many success stories, according to Ewing. She shared a story of a former hire who built a career with the company and eventually retired there and said watching people grow in their career is special.

Laura Klein, a retired biobehavioral health professor, is one of the volunteers at Scraps & Skeins. Many of her students needed volunteer hours when she worked at Penn State, so she would bring Strawberry Fields employees and clients into the classroom and send her students out to the organizations.

According to her coworkers, Klein also has a knack for the sales floor.

“Everything she touches sells,” Sue said. “She’ll touch it for a few minutes, and that thing? Sold.”

After 27 years of working at Penn State, volunteering at Scraps & Skeins was always high on her list of things to accomplish. Klein said it’s truly a great place, and she’s loved her time there.

“Penn Staters are very involved in Strawberry Fields, but now another way to support them is to come to Scraps & Skeins,” Klein said. “This is like the best kept secret in town.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Penn State women's hockey captain Tessa Janecke to throw first pitch for Friday's softball game
author: Zachary Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Tessa Janecke will be recognized again.

The Olympic gold medalist and Penn State captain will throw out the first pitch at Friday's Penn State softball game against Maryland at Beard Field at Nittany Lion Softball Park.

an olympic icon🤝beard fieldJoin us TOMORROW at 2 p.m. (ET) as U.S. Olympic Gold Medalist and Penn State Women’s Hockey LEGEND Tessa Janecke throws out our ceremonial first pitch🏒🥎#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/YlpHM9iLz6— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 2, 2026

Janecke will throw out the ceremonial first pitch at 2 p.m. Friday. It's the third time the Nittany Lion has been recognized across athletics in Happy Valley.

She made a ceremonial puck drop alongside Nicole Hall and Matilde Fantin at the men's hockey game against Wisconsin on March 5. Later in March, she walked out onto the ice for the ceremonial puck drop in the NCAA Women's Hockey National Championship Game between Ohio State and Wisconsin.

She has experience on the diamond as a three-time Illinois All-State player in high school. She lettered for both ice hockey and softball in all four years of high school.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: No. 6 Penn State men’s gymnastics heads to Big Ten Championships
author: Jimmy Blair | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 6 Penn State, after finishing a strong regular season campaign, looks forward to the conference championship this weekend in Lincoln, Nebraska.

The Nittany Lions arrive off an underwhelming performance against No. 5 Ohio State, losing 317.950-315.500. The blue and white aim to put their end-of-season skid behind them as they look on to the Big Ten Championship.

Coach Randy Jepsen and the squad have been in the upper echelon of the national rankings all year, yet find themselves behind conference rivals Michigan, Ohio State, and Nebraska. It is worth noting that all three of the Nittany Lions’ losses in dual meets this season have come against the three aforementioned conference opponents.

The blue and white will be on the vault for the first rotation, where Big Ten freshman of the year Shaun Smith will eye a big performance to get the squad firing out of the gate. Smith has been solid on the vault all year long, with an average score of 14.100.

The parallel bars await the Nittany Lions in rotation two. Bellefonte, PA native Landon Simpson will look to continue his strong campaign in the event. Luke Esparo will look to lead the blue and white through the third rotation, where they will be on the horizontal bar.

Following the horizontal bar, the blue and white will be on the floor. Senior Matt Underhill will look to come through for the squad in the fourth rotation, as he has been a consistent performer for the blue and white on the floor, including a floor title in the season opener against Army with a score of 13.350.

The pommel horse awaits the Nittany Lions in the fifth rotation, where freshman Ricky Perez will hope to have his strongest performance of the year, with a season high of 13.900 against Illinois.

The Nittany Lions will take off the final rotation of the evening on the still rings. Underhill is tied in the nation for the highest score on the event this season, with a 14.700 at the Sam Mikulak Invitational.

Despite winning the regular season title in the conference last year, and being a consistent powerhouse in gymnastics, the blue and white’s most recent Big Ten championship victory came in 2019.

The Nittany Lions are aiming to break the seven-year conference championship drought this weekend, and Michigan’s six-year championship streak, but will have to emerge victorious over their toughest opponents all year.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball adjusts game times for upcoming series with Maryland
author: Luci Palacios | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State softball has adjusted its home weekend series against Maryland due to expected rain in the forecast.

moving things around🌂Due to forecasted inclement weather, our weekend series with Maryland will now begin Friday at 2 p.m. (ET), with a Saturday doubleheader scheduled to start at 11 a.m. (ET). Stay tuned to our social channels for the latest info!#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/qfzHClBhtH— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) April 2, 2026

Friday’s game will now start at 2 p.m., moved up from its original 5 p.m. time.

Saturday has been changed to a doubleheader beginning at 11 a.m., replacing the previously scheduled single game at 2 p.m, with the second game of the doubleheader beginning shortly after the first.

The games will still be played at Nittany Lion Softball Park & Beard Field as the Nittany Lions aim to complete the series despite the weather conditions.
