title: Penn State announces start time, other festivities for Blue-White Practice
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State fans, a start time for Blue-White is here.

While it was previously called the Blue-White Game, the Matt Campbell-edition will be called Blue-White Practice and will begin at 1 p.m. Saturday April 25.

Admission for fans is free, with parking lots opening at 8 a.m. and gates opening for the public at 11 a.m.

The Friday before, Penn State will be hosting a Kids Combine from 5:30 to 7:30 p.m. Kids in eighth grade or below are invited to participate and the event will be hosted by select staff and athletes.

There will also be a pregame autograph session with the team starting at 11 a.m. ending at noon that will take place on the concourse of Beaver Stadium.

A Blue-White Apparel and Equipment Sale will take place at Pegula Ice Arena from 9 a.m. to 1 p.m. on Saturday, April 25, where fans can purchase team-issued apparel and equipment. The sale will run until supplies last.

Team arrival is scheduled for 10:45 a.m.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Former Penn State women's basketball recruit Chikae Desdunes commits to Rutgers
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State's only recruit is no longer.

Chikae Desdunes, who was committed to the Lady Lions until she reopened her recruitment on March 24, committed to Rutgers on Thursday.

Desdunes is the No. 51 player in the Class of 2026 and was Penn State's only commit before backing off last month following the hiring of coach Tanisha Wright.

The New Orleans native is one of nine athletes to reconsider their future at Penn State, as eight players from the 2025-26 roster have entered or plan to enter the transfer portal this offseason.

Now, Desdunes will have an opportunity to play for a different first-year coach as the Scarlet Knights hired former LSU assistant Gary Redus II to replace Coquese Washington.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State men's basketball AHC Jamal Brunt departing program for job at Syracuse
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Penn State assistant is departing the program.

Associate head coach Jamal Brunt is taking a job on the Syracuse staff, according to Jon Sauber of the Centre Daily Times.

Sources: Penn State men's basketball associate head coach Jamal Brunt is leaving for a job at Syracuse.More here:https://t.co/er5ZTZk91x— Jon Sauber (@JonSauber) April 2, 2026

Brunt has been with head coach Mike Rhoades since 2018. From 2018-23, Brunt was an assistant for Rhoades at VCU, and he followed Rhoades to the Nittany Lions in 2023, where he's been the associate head coach ever since.

Syracuse recently hired Gerry McNamara as its new head coach, so Brunt will be a part of his first staff with the Orange.

Brunt helped engineer Penn State's defense during his time in Happy Valley. This season, the Nittany Lions' defense ranked No. 230 in the country in adjusted defensive efficiency, according to KenPom. Penn State was No. 69 in 2024-25 and No. 81 in 2023-24.

Brunt becomes the second staffer to leave the team as former assistant coach Joe Crispin was hired as Air Force's head coach. This adds to the turnover Penn State has already seen this offseason as guards Freddie Dilione V and Melih Tunca both plan to enter the transfer portal.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 'It’s never the survivor’s fault' | Lambda Theta Alpha holds third annual Take Back the Night event
author: Kyla Jones I The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's Note: One of the names in this story has been changed to protect the identity of the individual involved. The Daily Collegian has verified through fact-checking that this individual exists.

Content warning: This story discusses sexual and domestic violence. Resources and support are available through Penn State’s Counseling and Psychological Services.

Correction: A previous version of this article stated the “ABC method” stands for autonomy, believing and confidence. The acronym stands for acknowledge, believe and check in. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

As the sun set, Lambda Theta Alpha Latin Sorority, Inc.’s Penn State chapter brought the State College community together for its annual “Take Back The Night” event, supporting survivors of sexual and domestic violence.

Since 2023, the event has served as a space for students and community members to stand in solidarity with survivors at Penn State. Nationally, Take Back the Night events have been held since 2010. Organized in collaboration with Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education Center (R-VOICE), Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) and Centre Safe, the event is part of Lambda Theta Alpha’s philanthropy supporting the National Coalition Against Domestic Violence.

Desiree Cotto, event co-chair, said this is her fourth year attending Take Back the Night. She said the event has given her the courage and bravery to speak her mind.

“Giving a safe space to the community is super important so people can be seen, feel seen and be heard, ” Cotto, a fourth-year studying psychology, said. “I honestly haven't seen an event where the community comes out as much as this event to support each other in the quietest way, but loudest way at the same time as possible.”

Cotto said getting the community engaged in sexual and domestic violence awareness is the biggest takeaway of the event.

The march began at Old Main, where State College Mayor Ezra Nanes delivered an opening speech on the importance of amplifying survivors’ voices. He also emphasized the necessity of community and support.

Sophia Mills, associate director of R-VOICE, said the center uses the “ABC method” when supporting survivors — acknowledge, believe and check in. This approach focuses on empowering individuals to have a voice of their own while validating their experiences.

“I truly believe that education, awareness, outreach events like this are going to be the thing that eliminates this type of violence to break down the barriers and stigmas,” Mills said.

As community members continued their walk across campus, they held a large black banner with a moon and white lettering saying “Take Back the Night.” The group stopped at what Mills called “hotspot” locations — Pattee and Paterno Library, East and Pollock halls — to demonstrate where sexual assaults have been reported the most on campus.

At each stop, members of Lambda Theta Alpha opened the floor to students and community members to share their experiences.

Emma, who requested anonymity as it was her first time sharing her story, said she was inspired to share her story as someone who has been sexually assaulted in college. She said she wants people to know that there are safe spaces to tell their stories.

“It means everything,” she said. “I’ve never been to any of one of these events before. I didn’t think I would be able to speak out. I didn’t think I would be able to tell the story and here I am telling the story.”

Emma said she attended the event not only to share her own story, but to support others in sharing theirs. As a woman of color, she said it took her a while to find a safe community at Penn State.

“I feel like spaces like these, I feel safe being a woman,” Emma said. “I feel safe being a person of color and being able to share my story.

Tears filled listeners' eyes and speakers were met with quiet nods from the audience. After speakers shared their stories, they were often met with embraces from friends.

Shelly Stevick, a university police officer, said Take Back the Night makes a difference in the community, and she volunteers her time because of this impact.

“No matter what they were wearing, what they were drinking, whatever their prior experience with the other person was, it's not their fault,” Stevick said. “If they said no, no means no.”

Stevick said throughout the years, she has seen an increase in the amount of people willing to share their stories. More people are realizing they shouldn’t feel any shame, she added.

“It’s never the survivor’s fault,” Stevick said.

The night ended with the group back in front of Old Main. Individuals were handed glow sticks during the closing remarks as a visual representation of those who lost their lives to domestic violence and sexual assault.

Javier Camilo, a member of Lambda Sigma Upsilon Fraternity Inc., said Take Back The Night was one of the first events he attended at Penn State, and that it’s important to him and his fraternity brothers. He said hearing the stories of event speakers moves him to take action to be there for survivors.

“For anyone who comes to the event, I hope they realize they are not alone,” Camilo, a fourth-year majoring in security and risk analysis, said. “There is a support system wherever you go in life.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State softball hosts Maryland for Big Ten weekend showdown
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Last weekend, Penn State earned its first Big Ten series win via a sweep over Rutgers.

The Nittany Lions dropped their most recent contest in a one-run deficit against Pittsburgh, but will look to get back on track when they host Maryland for a trio of conference games.

The blue and white own a 23-12 record and are 4-5 in Big Ten play as opposed to the Terrapins’ 13-20 mark, standing at just 1-11 against conference opponents.

Maryland has lost its last six games, most recently getting swept by Wisconsin. The Terrapins' lone Big Ten win came on March 7 against Rutgers.

Penn State’s most recent outing ended in heartbreak after the Nittany Lions gave up six seventh-inning runs, allowing Pittsburgh to win 8-7 in walk-off fashion.

Maryland owns a .281 team batting average, but the high offensive numbers are sprinkled throughout the lineup.

Sammi Woods owns the Terrapins' highest batting average at .392, but is also a threat on the basepaths, tied for a team-high three steals on five attempts.

Anna McGowan is Maryland’s highest run producer, racking in 28 RBIs on the season. The Waxhaw, North Carolina, native also leads the Terrapins in extra base hits with eight home runs and five doubles on the season.

The third name to look out for in Maryland’s lineup is Mariah Penta. The Chesapeake City, Maryland, native’s .378 batting average is second on the Terrapins, and she leads the team with eight doubles.

Flipping to the home team, Penn State’s .298 team batting average falls just inside the top 10 in the conference. The Nittany Lions are led by the strong left side infield slugging duo of Allison Oneacre and Brooke Klosowicz.

Both are top 15 in the Big Ten when it comes to RBIs, as Oneacre has 46 on the season, followed by Klosowicz’s 37. Penn State is one of four teams in the conference that has multiple players in the top-15 for RBIs.

The duo also paces the Nittany Lions’ offense in batting average, led by Klosowicz’s .378. Oneacre posts a .353 average, but leads the blue and white with a .804 slugging percentage.

Switching to the circle, these Big Ten foes are most alike in that they each use a fair amount of arms. Maryland has four pitchers with at least 40 innings pitched, and Penn State has five with at least 40 innings.

Keira Bucher leads the Terrapins with a 2.92 ERA, but only two of her 16 appearances were starts. Caitlin Olensky has the most innings pitched, with 59.2 across 18 outings and 15 starts. The Montville, New Jersey, native also leads the team with 46 strikeouts.

Bridget Nemeth has seen the most action for the Nittany Lions, with 50.1 innings pitched in 24 appearances. Mackenzie Duncan leads Penn State with a 2.45 ERA and 56 strikeouts, but her 40 innings pitched are the least on the staff.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Spring semester’s most popular tradition is back | Why students do ‘55 Days of Cafe’
author: Skylar Williamson I The Daily Collegian
description:
Each year after spring break, when the weather gets warmer, a familiar ritual returns to downtown State College. What began as a casual tradition has evolved into a social challenge, drawing students together for nearly two months of daily attendance at Cafe 210 West.

In 2008, Cafe started the tradition, “55 Days of Cafe,” and over the years, thousands of community members and Penn State students have participated. This year, 953 people registered, setting a new record for their registration day.

For many, the 55-day challenge starts as a casual idea that quickly turns into a part of their daily routine that students eagerly take on each spring.

The concept is simple but demanding — participants commit to visiting Cafe every day for 55 consecutive days. Each day, participants must purchase a menu item and document their receipt to ensure their attendance is being tracked.

After the 55 days, participants who have completed the challenge don’t just get the satisfaction of spending their money at a bar every day for two months but also receive fun incentives. The successful contenders will earn their name on a permanent plaque that’ll be hung in Cafe and an all-paid-for, end-of-the-year party with food and drinks at Tussey Mountain.

Despite the commitment, people are up for the challenge. For many, the event offers a structured way to make the most of the final stretch of the academic year. Some students, like Samantha Sullivan, chose to do it alongside their friends, even with initial doubts.

“At first, we didn’t want to do it because we weren’t sure how it was going to fit into our schedules,” Sullivan, a fourth-year studying accounting, said. “Then we figured ‘let’s just do it,’ and now it’s been 15 days and so much fun.”

The environment at Cafe 210 plays an essential part in sustaining the event’s popularity. Known for its outdoor seating, good drinks and central downtown location, the space becomes especially lively during the 55-day stretch.

Cafe transforms into a social hub at all hours of the day, especially in warm weather. Groups gather around tables, some staying for hours and others stop by to grab a pitcher and do their homework just to keep their daily streak alive.

Darren Wilson said Cafe is the perfect spot to host an event like 55 Days.

“Cafe is huge, has a good outside area, good music and good food,” Wilson, a fourth-year studying supply chain and information systems, said. “The chill environment makes it a good vibe, and I don’t mind coming here for 55 days straight.”

However, rainy weather, exams and travel plans can all complicate the routine. Still, participants often find ways to adapt, whether that means stopping by in between classes or coordinating visits with friends to stay motivated.

Participants hold each other accountable for the challenge and for some, 55 Days is less about socializing and more about discipline.

Ashley Santana said accountability creates a bonding experience itself.

“I didn’t even think I was going to make it past seven days,” Sanatana, a fourth-year studying human-centered design and development, said. “My friends and I always send a text to remind each other to go or try to go together when we can, and I haven’t missed a day.”

The social aspect of the experience, however, plays the largest role in students’ motives for joining in. Creating bonds and long-lasting memories are part of the goal after the 55 days.

For seniors, 55 Days is more than just a social experience — it’s one of the last Penn State traditions they can partake in with their friends before graduation. Liana Kosienski said that being a senior motivates her during the challenge.

“This is one of the last times all my friends will be together at the same time in the same place,” Kosienski, a fourth-year studying accounting, said. “55 Days gives us a set time of the day to see each other even if it’s only for a couple of minutes.”

The tradition has grown steadily through word of mouth and social media. Students often track their progress online, documenting each completed day and encouraging others to join.

Some participants are creating new social media accounts or changing their social handles to fit the theme of 55 Days, posting videos of their food and drinks for each day.

55 Days reflects a broader aspect of student life at Penn State, much like the football season. It offers a sense of connection that extends beyond individual friend groups.

Whether students join for the social aspect, the challenge or simply out of curiosity, 55 Days continues to draw participants, breaking new records every year. In a college environment where time flies, the tradition offers a way to slow down, show up and be part of something steady.

“Cafe is just an everyday bar until 55 Days,” Wilson said. “Now, it’s like a consistent place that we can say we went to every day at the end of our last semester in college.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball prepares for in-conference series against Charleston
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off a three-match winning stretch, No. 15 Penn State looks to extend its streak during its two-match road series against Charleston.

The blue and white will travel to Charleston, West Virginia, for an in-conference matchup against the Golden Eagles at 7 p.m. Friday and 3 p.m. Sunday.

As the blue and white prepare for several more in-conference match ups, a couple of wins this weekend could be all the help it needs heading into the final stretch of the season.

While sitting at 14-7 on the year, Penn State remains 6-0 in conference play, giving them an edge over the Charleston squad, who are currently 13-10 on the season and 4-4 in the EIVA.

The Golden Eagles are also coming off a loss to Princeton, whereas the Nittany Lions are coming in with three straight wins against rivals Ohio State and NJIT, another EIVA team.

While it seems defense is solid on both ends of the court, the blue and white do seem to have some advantages. Penn State currently ranks No. 1 in the EIVA in blocks, with an average of 2.81 per set.

Beyond that, Owen Rose ranks No. 1 in the NCAA in blocks per set. With 97 blocks this season, the most of any Nittany Lion, the redshirt senior poses a major defensive threat to Charleston.

However, the Golden Eagles are No. 2 in the EIVA in blocks, with an average of 2.20 per set.

But despite being just one spot below the blue and white within the EIVA, Charleston doesn’t have anyone ranked in the top 50 in the NCAA in blocks per set. As a team, however, the Golden Eagles rank No. 14 in that very category, proving it’s an all around block defense.

The leader in blocks for the Charleston squad is Braxton Francis, who’s tallied 57, which falls significantly lower than Rose’s current total.

Rose was also awarded the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week honor after his 15 kills, 16 blocks, four aces and .619 hitting percentage in last week’s three-match stretch. And the middle blocker wasn’t the only Nittany Lion to win big last week.

Outside hitter Sean Harvey took home the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week award after his performance in all three games. He totaled 39 kills, a .411 hitting percentage, 12 digs and one ace.

The redshirt sophomore has earned the weekly honor three times this season.

Harvey currently leads the blue and white in kills with 237 on the season. He ranks No. 30 in the NCAA in kills per set.

Matthew Luoma has also been a strong offensive weapon for the Nittany Lions this season. With 120 kills so far, the redshirt junior trails behind Harvey with the second most this year.

And while a newer edition to more recent games, Bennett Wilson has been playing well from an attacking standpoint, too. The sophomore had 12 points against NJIT on Friday, and a career-high nine kills.

That’s not to say that the Golden Eagles don’t have offensive powers of their own. Max Nissen leads the team in kills with 177 and trailing just behind is Lucas Torres with 173.

But if the Charleston defense isn’t able to put a stop to Harvey, it could be a quick match.

With the regular season coming to a close in the coming weeks, an in-conference win is crucial to keeping the momentum going. The weekend series should be one to watch.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Off the record | Dear Daisy
author: Miss Behave | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a third-year Collegian staffer writing under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic are invited to do so through this anonymous form.

Dearest reader,

I hope you’ve had a chance to bask in the sun these last couple of days. I know I did. And if you didn’t get a chance to lay out on the HUB lawn for a little while, then I hope you at least got to catch a glimpse of the hot men, shirtless around campus. Quite a view if I do say so myself, one may consider it a public service. Those silly little packs of boys playing volleyball, spikeball or any other type of “ball” they’re into these days.

My spicy little readers, I’m honored many of you have written to me over the course of the semester. It’s been my absolute pleasure to write you back, offer my perspective and help guide you through all things love, life and sex.

This week, Daisy writes in with a question that’s equal parts vulnerable and universal to everyone.

She writes that she’s facing body insecurities in her relationship which have contributed to confusing feelings related to her sexual activity.

First of all, take a deep breath. You’re not alone and you’re definitely not “behind.” There’s no timeline for intimacy. No scoreboard or gold medal.

Second, you say you're a “bigger girl” like it's a disclaimer, like you're apologizing before the story even starts. Absolutely not. Your body isn’t a warning label. It’s not a problem that has to be solved before you’re allowed to be loved, touched or desired.

And here’s the thing people don’t tell you enough, confidence isn’t about loving every inch of yourself all the time, it's about deciding your worth isn't up for debate. Knowing your worth and value isn’t defined by anyone else but you.

Now, onto the bedroom insecurities. You’re worried you don’t know what you're doing. Newsflash! Neither did your boyfriend at some point and experience certainly isn’t a measure of your value. If he truly cares about you (and it sounds like he does), then he’s not grading you or ranking you on a scale.

This is also important. You don’t owe anyone confidence you don’t feel yet. If you’re feeling insecure, nervous or just not ready, that's not something to push through for the sake of keeping up with someone else's experience or expectations.

Move at the pace you’re comfortable with. The right person will meet you where you are.

Let’s zoom out for a second. Right now, this relationship might feel like everything. The kind of everything where if he doesn’t text back every few hours, your mind starts spinning on the “what ifs.” Totally normal.

As you get older though, your understanding of love will shift. You will start to realize that love isn’t about proving yourself or having to perform perfectly. It’s not about being the most confident or most experienced. And it’s certainly not about giving into something you’re not ready for, especially when it comes to sex.

It's about comfort. It’s about ease. It’s about feeling like you can exist. Fully, imperfectly, sometimes awkwardly and still be wanted.

Love, when it’s right, feels like home.

Daisy — here’s your takeaway. You don’t need to shrink your body. You don’t need to fake your confidence or fast-track your comfort level just because your partner is more experienced or feel like you need to “keep up” with his expectations to be worthy of love and intimacy. You already are. Exactly as you are.

And trust me anyone worth your time will care far more about how you feel than how experienced you are. You shouldn’t be receiving or feel like your partner is pressuring you in any way. That isn’t love, that's control.

Daisy, I hope you know how proud I’m of you for being so vulnerable and offering me your deeper thoughts and insecurities. I hope I was able to offer you some advice in return.

I am always cheering you on.

Yours truly,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: ‘She’s a competitor’ | Star attacker Erika Ho celebrates final season with Penn State women’s lacrosse
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
With the man-up advantage, Erika Ho received a pass from Kelly MacKinney and scored her fifth goal of the game, extending Penn State’s lead and ultimately upsetting No. 10 Michigan in overtime.

Stories like that highlighted Ho’s junior season with the Nittany Lions, as she ended the season with a team-high 46 goals and All-Big Ten First Team honors.

While that high-scoring offense illustrated her early career, Ho ultimately returned to the blue and white after graduating in three years, where she picked up a different role on the squad in Year 4.

“It’s definitely challenging, but something that I’ve been really grateful for,” Ho told The Daily Collegian. “I think I’ve just grown so much, not only as a lacrosse player, but as a person with all the different new faces trying to spearhead and be a mentor to all the younger ones.”

As Ho entered her senior year, expectations were there for another, if not more, dominant season than the year before. The Frankfort, Illinois, native was one of 50 players to be announced to the first release of the Tewaaraton Award Watch List in February.

In the early season, those expectations were apparent by newly hired coach Kayla Treanor, who was just having her first looks at Ho after class conflict and injury kept her fall training limited.

“She was a phenomenal player last year in the country,” Treanor told the Collegian. “She’s a phenomenal player and I can’t wait to continue to just get to work with her and just try to help her elevate her game.”

After scoring one of the final goals in the team’s one-goal victory over Richmond to open the season, Ho earned her first of many personal bests as she recorded seven points against UMass.

In addition to her first hat trick of the season, Ho added a career-high four assists — twice as many as her previous best — just one day after being named to the watch list.

“The staff keeps me very hungry,” Ho said. “Coming from last year we didn’t have a winning season, so that’s one of my biggest motivations. It’s to always get better, always strive to be better.”

Through the first two games of the season, that was very clearly on display as she earned a pair of collegiate bests. However, that success was temporarily set back after sustaining an injury against Bucknell, where she scored two goals prior to leaving the field.

While Ho was out for two games, she continued to have a limited role as she worked her way back to full strength on March 22.

During her limited playing time, Ho’s impact on the team didn’t change. Instead, she shifted to a more mentor-based role and helped coach the team from the sidelines during their winning streak.

“She’s an excellent leader, and I think she just carries a presence that you can’t deny, you can’t overlook,” freshman Rocquette Allen said. “We really trust her, lean on her, and I think it’s a big piece of it.”

In the wake of Ho’s absence, Allen stepped up in a big role and has carried that scoring responsibility this season, recording 30 goals on the year so far, as Ho served as a mentor to her.

“It’s just the little things she does,” senior Maddie Greco said. “She’s always helping everyone.”

While Ho’s injury kept her off the field and limited her ability to boost her stats this season, Treanor’s goal of a balanced offensive attack was fulfilled upon her return, sharing the scoring load in the previous weeks.

“My goal for Erika this season would be that she doesn’t have to do it alone,” Treanor said. “She can really rely on her teammates, and her teammates can rely on her. We’re playing team offense and I think that will elevate her game, and it will elevate everyone’s game around her.”

After playing primarily man-up opportunities against Cornell and Colgate while coming out of her injury, Ho instantly returned to her old scoring ways as her playing time resumed.

Against San Diego State on March 8, Ho recorded her third multi-goal game of the season, followed by another two-goal game just five days later.

Despite her offensive success, Ho hasn’t been focused on the statistical impact she’s leaving on the field. Instead, her role as a teammate is what has been most valuable to her.

“What I want people to remember most is not just what I do on the field, but how I treated people, because I was always taught that people didn’t remember what you did, but how you treated them,” Ho said. “If people remember more about how I treated them and how I acted, that will go greater lengths than what I did on the field statistically.

After Penn State opened the season 9-0, it went on a three-game losing streak, which continued in the last game when Ho came off the bench.

With the star attacker back in the lineup, Treanor is looking at her to help bring the Nittany Lions back to the winning column in Ho’s final season.

“She’s a competitor,” Treanor said. “She loves to compete, and that’s something that I really value. We’re trying to create a culture where it’s really competitive, a healthy competitive, where we come to practice and we’re competing every day, no matter who the opponent is.”

Ho has the same expectations for herself and her squad as she wraps up her Penn State career, as she hopes to finish the last month of the season with success in Happy Valley.

“I’ve really just been taking in my last season, being grateful for the time that I have here and the time I have left with all my best friends on the team,” Ho said. “We’re all one big family, and really, my goal is to win a national championship and go deep into May with everyone.”

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Hogan-Lopez ticket to lead UPUA’s 21st assembly
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article stated Hogan and Lopez would be sworn in April 15 during UPUA's first general assembly meeting. The first general assembly meeting is April 8. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

Kat Hogan and Maria Lopez have been elected president and vice president of the University Park Undergraduate Association’s (UPUA) 21st assembly.

The polls closed at 9 p.m. Wednesday. 1,853 students voted for Hogan-Lopez. Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon received 1,034 votes, while 281 students voted for Akito Hatch and Chloe Smith.

Hogan, a third-year studying energy engineering, was speaker of the 20th assembly and chair of the 19th assembly’s Justice and Equity Committee. Lopez, a third-year studying political science, chaired the committee this year and served as the Lion Caucus representative in the 19th assembly. Both were First-Year Council members in the 18th assembly.

Hogan and Lopez campaigned on furthering student engagement and increasing transparency efforts.

In total, 3,194 students voted in the election, which is down from last year’s total of 3,773.

Hogan was also running for an at-large representative position, where she received 1,511 votes, the most out of all 22 candidates.

Hogan and Lopez will be sworn into the 21st assembly on April 8, during the assembly’s first general body meeting.

The duo succeeds 20th assembly President Rasha Elwakil and Vice President Noah Youcheff.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: ‘It’s bigger than any of us’ | Matt Campbell, Penn State continues to emphasize presence of lettermen
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Lettermen have been a crucial asset of Penn State for years. James Franklin littered his staff with them, but many departed when Matt Campbell took over, and Terry Smith is now the lone assistant coach who played for Penn State.

But that doesn’t mean Campbell is ignoring lettermen entirely. In fact, it’s quite the opposite. He’s made it a goal from the beginning to teach his players the rich history of Penn State, and the biggest aspect of that is bringing lettermen back around the program.

“The history of this football program, to be able to represent this, to understand the lineage, it's bigger than me. It's bigger than any of us,” Campbell said Tuesday. “What we're upholding every day we go on a practice field, and certainly, anytime you get the opportunity to put that uniform on and step in that stadium, you have to know what you're representing.”

Iconic former players such as Todd Blackledge, Dave Robinson and DJ Dozier came and spoke with the team last weekend as Campbell continues to push for lettermen involvement.

Smith said Penn State will be having a group of lettermen come back each weekend as Campbell toward enticing guys like Blackledge to return and hang around the facility consistently.

“There were just a bunch of guys that came back in with their families, and all spent time around the team,” Smith said Tuesday. “For our team, we want to continue to build a culture of what Penn State should be, what it has been and what it needs to be going forward. And what better way than to bring these legends in that can give their knowledge, their stories and their experience to help our guys grow and get better?”

Quarterback Rocco Becht was pleased to see the Penn State lettermen, and even had a 40-minute conversation with Blackledge while he was in Happy Valley, which the Nittany Lions’ new quarterback said was “pretty special.”

“A guy like Todd Blackledge, who's done it, who's won a national championship, who's played at the highest level and has gone to the league, it's great to have conversations with him,” Becht said Tuesday. “Just getting the knowledge, listening to him, learning from him was pretty special last weekend.”

Becht said at Iowa State, former players would come back to the facilities whenever they wanted, and Campbell and his staff would always welcome them with open arms.

Players were so used to having them around that it wasn’t a big deal when lettermen would return. Becht hopes a few years down the line, Penn State will be the same way.

“We didn't really have to have a whole dinner for everyone to come back. Everyone kind of just came into the facility when they wanted,” Becht said. “They texted coach ‘Hey, I am coming today.’ So I think that's what this will get to. And the guys will just be able to walk into the facility and feel like a college football player again. So I think that's where we're trying to go with this.”

Becht isn’t the only player benefiting from having the lettermen around. Campbell said it’s helping everyone, as these former players have been through plenty of ups and downs and can share that knowledge with the current team.

Smith said the players are excited to see legends back in the building and “feel a better attachment to the program” as Campbell and the staff work to create the environment Penn State once had.

“When we played here, grew up playing under Joe Paterno, and it was the same staff over all those years,” Smith said. “So every time you would come in, they just knew who you were. And we're trying to get back to that.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State QB Rocco Becht an 'extension' of coaching staff as he recovers from injury during spring
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
During parts of Penn State’s spring ball practices, Rocco Becht can be found off to the side, working on mental reps or evaluating how his fellow quarterbacks are playing.

Becht can only participate in a limited role, recovering from injuries in both of his shoulders. Still, Becht believes he could have a successful spring.

“I think growing in my leadership, growing in the little things,” Becht said after practice on Tuesday. “I could still do my footwork out there. I could still go through the reads. It's all those mental reps and all those footwork things. I can still get better at different things. I know I can throw the ball, I know I can be accurate with it, but it's just all the little things that come before that.”

Last season with Iowa State, Becht tore his labrum in his non-throwing shoulder and had an AC joint sprain in his throwing shoulder. Despite that, he played in all 12 games for the Cyclones, throwing for 2,584 yards, 16 touchdowns and nine interceptions.

Following Penn State’s first spring practice on March 24, Matt Campbell said he felt Becht was “a little bit ahead of schedule” in his journey back to action. At Tuesday’s practice, Becht said he participated in some independent drills and has started throwing in routes-on-air drills.

As he recovers, Becht is also helping out the rest of the quarterback room as they take reps against the defense.

“I think it's great to have me on the sideline, and it's a big step for me, opportunity for me to lead those guys and help them with the plays that they're running on in team reps in 7-on-7,” Becht said. “To give them feedback on what I'm seeing, and they take it. They love to be coached. They love to ask questions, and so I think it's been going great the past four weeks.”

Behind Becht, the Nittany Lions have a relatively young group of gunslingers. While redshirt freshman Alex Maske is sidelined due to injury, freshmen Peyton Falzone and Kase Evans have been full participants. Additionally, Christopher Newport University senior transfer Connor Barry, who’s been taking first-team reps, is making the jump from the Division III level.

Campbell said this time is a “great opportunity” for Becht to sharpen his leadership tools, and he believes he’s done a “great job early on.”

“You hope by the time he gets to this last portion of his career, he's almost the offensive coordinator on the football field,” Campbell said. “He's an extension of the coaching staff, and he's got the ability to use his voice to set the standard of what are the expectations.”

Becht said he’s taking it day by day as he works back to becoming 100% healthy. One way he’s getting additional reps is using Penn State’s virtual reality system, which allows the quarterbacks to practice hypothetical in-game situations with various route combinations against a defense.

The new starting gunslinger said he wants to take his recovery slow so he can be ready to prove himself during the season.

“I feel a lot better, and that's really the biggest thing for me,” Becht said. “I could rush back and get back with the team, but I want to be fully healthy and fully prepared because I don't want to have a season like I did last year.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: West Chester man charged for State Patty’s weekend break-in, assault
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
A West Chester, Pennsylvania man was charged with three felonies on March 25 after allegedly entering a downtown State College apartment, stealing alcohol and assaulting medical personnel on State Patty’s weekend.

Manuel Deleon-Garcia, 22, was charged with burglary, criminal trespassing and aggravated assault. He was also charged with four misdemeanors, including simple assault, loitering and prowling at night time, theft by unlawful taking - movable property and receiving stolen property.

Additionally, he was charged with a summary offense of public drunkenness and similar conduct.

A resident of an East Nittany Avenue apartment notified police on March 2 that a Ring camera detected an unknown man entering their residence near 1 a.m. on March 1. In the camera footage, a young man, later identified as Deleon-Garcia, was allegedly seen stumbling into the apartment from a hallway used by the unit, according to the criminal complaint.

Allegedly, Deleon-Garcia appeared to enter the apartment with no possessions, but left with a bottle of Tito’s Vodka and an eight pack of Lucky One Lemonade vodka cans. The items were valued at approximately $30.

Police did not share how Deleon-Garcia was able to allegedly enter the apartment, but stated that he was not authorized to enter or take alcohol by any of the five residents of the apartment.

At 1:41 a.m., a patrolling police officer noticed an unconscious college-aged male lying next to a bottle of Tito’s Vodka near the 100 block of West Hamilton Avenue.

The officer woke up the man, noting that he had “glassy bloodshot eyes and an odor of alcoholic beverage emitting from his breath,” as well as slurred speech. The officer attempted to identify the man, who allegedly did not provide accurate identification.

While waiting for EMS to arrive for potential alcohol overdose, the man allegedly tried to walk away and was placed in a patrol vehicle.

Once EMS arrived with an ambulance, the man attempted to allegedly flee again before being seat-belted into a stretcher. Once in the stretcher, the man allegedly identified himself as Deleon-Garcia and was transported to Mount Nittany Medical Center.

At approximately 2 a.m., police were dispatched to Mount Nittany Medical Center after a report that Deleon-Garcia had allegedly become combative with EMS personnel. Deleon-Garcia allegedly struck the left leg of an EMT during transport to the hospital, according to the complaint. A doctor stepped in to restrain Deleon-Garcia, who allegedly struck the doctor multiple times in the abdomen and shoulder.

Once the officer arrived, he allegedly saw Deleon-Garcia “yelling” and “thrashing about” his hospital room, preventing medical personnel from treating him.

The complaint said it allegedly took six Mount Nittany Medical Center security guards holding Deleon-Garcia down for nurses to be able to treat him. The attending physician then determined he would be chemically and physically restrained for the duration of his care “for the safety of the staff.”

Later, medical records allegedly indicated that Deleon-Garcia had a blood alcohol content of 0.40%.

Following the incident at the hospital, police were allegedly able to identify the man from the apartment incident by reviewing the Ring camera footage and police body camera footage from the incident on West Hamilton Avenue.

Deleon-Garcia is facing two sets of charges. For the apartment incident, Deleon-Garcia’s bail was set at $42,500. For the hospital incident, his bail was set at $20,000. Both bail amounts are unsecured.

Deleon-Garcia's preliminary arraignment occurred Thursday with Judge Donald Hahn, and he waived his preliminary hearing on Wednesday. His formal arraignment is scheduled for April 29 at 1:00 p.m. with Judge Katherine V. Oliver.

title: Report: Penn State men’s basketball guard Freddie Dilione V enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
After two seasons with Penn State, guard Freddie Dilione V is moving on to another school.

The redshirt junior plans to enter the transfer portal with one year of eligibility remaining, according to Joe Tipton of On3.

NEW: Penn State guard Freddie Dilione V plans to enter the NCAA transfer portal, @JoeTipton reports.Dilione averaged 14.0 points and 3.2 rebounds per game last season.https://t.co/052BF6ovB2 pic.twitter.com/qrTFcLSsdu— Transfer Portal (@TransferPortal) April 1, 2026

It’s Dilione’s second time in the portal in his career as he transferred from Tennessee to the Nittany Lions ahead of the 2024-25 season. Throughout his time in the blue and white, Dilione averaged 11.7 points, 3.0 rebounds and 2.0 assists per game across 63 appearances.

This season, Dilione began as a player coming in off the bench but became a usual starter down the stretch, though he didn’t start in the first round of the Big Ten Tournament against Northwestern. He averaged a career-high 14.0 points during 32 games played, including 15 starts.

Dilione was arguably Penn State’s best scorer, finishing in double figures 25 times while shooting 47.3% from the field this season. He showcases the ability to create shots for his own, typically getting to his spot near the free-throw line and making a mid-range jumper.

The issues for Dilione throughout the season usually came on the defensive side of the ball, but he had some complete performances, such as his career-high 25 points and five rebounds in a 77-75 win over Minnesota on Feb. 1.

“Freddie can make plays, and what I was proud of Freddie today is he played defense,” Rhoades said postgame. “He played defense. He had five rebounds. He was in some scuffles. He was going after the ball today.”

title: Third annual Osaze Osagie Memorial Lecture hosts Chamique Holdsclaw
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Students and faculty came together in the Hintz Family Alumni Center to attend the College of Liberal Art’s third annual Osaze Osagie Memorial Lecture.

Osagie, a Penn State alum and State College resident, was shot and killed on March 20, 2019 by a police officer during a mental health checkup. Osagie’s death has since inspired the need for an increase in adequate mental health resources.

Chamique Holdsclaw, an Olympic medalist and WNBA Hall of Famer, was this year’s speaker. Holdsclaw, a mental health advocate, addressed her mental health journey during the lecture moderated by Clarence Lang, Susan Welch dean of the College of the Liberal Arts, and the importance of being open to receiving help.

Jennifer Hamer, a professor of African American studies and women's, gender and sexuality studies, said there has been an increased awareness of the importance of mental health in recent years, something she saw reflected in the lecture.

“I think it's very rare to have a family where you don't have at least one person that is having to manage mental health issues, and I think all of us have to think about our mental health and our wellness, more so today than maybe 10 years, 15 years ago,” Hamer said.

Hamer said she hoped the lecture would make the audience more aware of how mental health impacts those around them.

“This public lecture is about raising awareness around mental health and while it's focused on the black community, it's really about mental health across the board, regardless of identities,” Hamer said. “What I would hope that they would take away is just greater awareness of what mental health and wellness, what the struggles look like for individuals. Maybe they'll learn a little bit more about how to recognize it when they engage with students or neighbors or friends, how to be respectful, because, historically, people have been stigmatized for saying that they have issues with mental health, depression, bipolar, all of those different categories.”

In an interview prior to the lecture, Holdsclaw said she was unable to properly discuss mental health when she was younger due to the stigmas that come with such discussions.

“I wanted to do something different, because I have a dad that has schizophrenic disorder and I wish we would have just talked about it in the household,” Holdsclaw said. “It was something that we just turned the deaf ear to and I just wanted to be that person that spoke out about it.”

She said it was important for more people to speak up about their mental health struggles to ensure that the issue of mental health continues to receive attention.

“When I can learn about you, you can learn about me and we share words and share our journeys and we can see the world open up because I've taken you on a walk in my life,” Holdsclaw said. “I just love the exchange.”

The event concluded with a Q&A session between Holdsclaw and audience members, where she spoke about her initial struggles when advocating for mental health and the importance of therapists to form meaningful connections with those in their care. State College Mayor Ezra Nanes followed, giving closing remarks for the event.

The lecture is part of a larger series of events meant to draw attention to mental health in global Black communities, such as a showing of the documentary “MIND/GAME: The Unquiet Journey of Chamique Holdsclaw” on Monday.

A lunch and learn event was also held in the Paul Robeson Cultural Center prior to the lecture that saw Holdsclaw speak with and answer questions from those in attendance.

Audience members were encouraged to take materials related to mental health resources both at Penn State and within State College during the event’s reception.

Erik Borgia said he plans to apply what he learned from Holdsclaw to his work in the Edna Bennett Pierce Prevention Research Center’s Learn to Flourish Design Hub, where he’s helping to form a curriculum to help address the mental health of Penn State’s athletes.

“This is an example of someone who struggled a lot, spoke out about it and still managed to succeed, so we look to use their strengths and apply them to college athletes and student athletes at Penn State,” Borgia, a third-year studying psychology, said. “I'm grateful to have people like Chamique coming out and talking to us, and I'm grateful that Penn State thinks it's an important matter and brings them to campus.”

Borgia said he hoped the lecture would help to show those currently struggling with their mental health that they are not alone and that it’s important for those around them to treat them with dignity and respect.

“I think they'll see that if they didn't know already that they're not the only ones going through something,” Borgia said. “You hear people who are at the top and didn't realize that they were going through it. They’re all human, and if we could treat each other like we all go through these struggles and we need this compassion, we'll be a better community and come together better as people.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State women's gymnastics heads West for chance at NCAA Championship
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is packing up and heading to the Southwest for warmer temps and a chance at an NCAA title. The Nittany Lions will be competing in the second round of the Tempe Regional in Arizona on Thursday for a chance to go to the championship in Fort Worth, Texas.

Penn State will compete against Florida, California, Arizona and the hosts, Arizona State. These teams will compete in the second round of the regional meet, with the two winners from that round going to the regional final. That winner will advance to the semifinals in Fort Worth, with the championships just ahead of it in the same venue.

Penn State has been fighting to be a giant in the Big Ten all season, and although there were some bumps in the road and some unlucky meets, the Nittany Lions have a shot at making it to Fort Worth.

Florida has had an exceptional season, going 6-2 in conference and 20-2 overall and sitting at No. 3 in the NCAA rankings. The Gators have a 14-meet win streak, taking out teams like UNC, Alabama and Missouri. It also had a massive win over LSU, defeating the Tigers 198.450-198.325.

Bars and vault seem to be the apparatuses in which Florida’s athletes shine the brightest, with multiple athletes having high 9.900s as their average on bars and multiple high 9.800s on vault. The Gators also have a slew of all-around athletes to fill their lineup and bring in crucial points. Skye Blakely, Selena Harris-Miranda and Kayla DiCello all have high numbers all-around and especially on bars. Harris-Miranda also has an average 9.955 on beam, so she could be someone to really watch.

No. 14 California has also had a great season, going 4-2 in conference and 18-7 overall. Notable wins include UNC and Clemson. The Golden Bears have been pretty consistent this season, being confident against big teams and producing solid results regardless of a win or not. California could be the team that is closest, capabilities-wise, to Penn State, so points could be close between the two.

Like the Nittany Lions, bars are the event that California is most confident in, having averages of 9.800s and up from the majority of its athletes. All-around athlete Annalise Newman-Achee has solid numbers across the board, and Casey Brown is a great option for the Golden Bears on bars and floor.

Arizona, sitting at No. 33 in the NCAA rankings, had a shorter season compared to the rest of the teams, but it still had plenty of chances to show itself. The Wildcats had a less-than-ideal time in the conference schedule, only taking home a win against Arizona State and West Virginia, but there were some other wins and third places throughout the nonconference schedule.

Arizona’s Abigayle Martin is a great option for the Wildcats, having an all-around average of 39.060 for the 2026 season. Arizona is consistent and confident on beam, with multiple 9.700s and 9.800s from its athletes.

No. 32 Arizona State is the other Big 12 school in this region, and this is the second time that they will meet Arizona in the 2026 season. The Sun Devils had a pretty even season, going 2-3 in the conference and 11-8 overall. Both Arizona State and Arizona schools are pretty up to par with each other, so points will be tight between the two.

Arizona State’s Kylee Bromley and Kimberly Smith could be great options for the Sun Devils to use to cover all of their bases. Both of these athletes are solid all-arounders, which could help give their team a leg up. Beam and vault have both been strong for Arizona State this season, so seeing competition between both it and Arizona could be really interesting.

Overall, this meet has a lot of pairs that could be really pushing each other to get an advancing spot. Penn State and California and Arizona and Arizona State will be the pairs with the most compatibility, so seeing which ones will come out on top and go up against a likely Florida in the regional final for a spot in the NCAA semifinals in Fort Worth will be close.

title: ‘It's invaluable’ | Penn State’s new quarterback room takes advantage of virtual reality lab
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
There’s still just over five months until Penn State will be in Beaver Stadium for a game again, but its quarterbacks are already gaining experience playing in the venue.

The Nittany Lions have a virtual reality system, named the QB lab, that allows for the quarterback room to practice inside Beaver Stadium. It’s one of the resources Penn State has to help the quarterbacks develop over the years, and the team explained its advantages on Tuesday.

“It's been kind of a test run right now for the spring, but we're running the plays that we have in the install versus just kind of random base defenses,” quarterbacks coach Jake Waters said. “They're seeing plays, and the cool part is, you can be in Beaver Stadium and run the plays. It's a really cool deal where they can go and get live reps sitting down or in the QB lab, anything like that.”

Notably, last season’s eventual starter, Ethan Grunkemeyer, took advantage of the system during his time as a backup quarterback, as the offensive line noted he broke it with how much he played it.

With players wearing virtual reality goggles, Waters said it’s like “you’re in another world” while using the system. He said it’s possible to pick any type of defense to play against, look in any direction and even change the type of weather inside of Beaver Stadium for any practice rep.

Starting quarterback Rocco Becht has been limited in spring practices as he works his way back from injuries in his shoulders. While he can’t get as many reps as he would on the actual practice field, Becht said he can still use the lab.

“You can change the speed of it, so you're really going faster looking at the defense, faster processing, faster on the VR than you want on the field,” Becht said. “It's a great advantage that I think us quarterbacks have, and everybody uses it.”

Waters added that he can track all the reps the quarterbacks have done on the virtual reality system. He can see if the user made the right read, giving the room more practice in hypothetical situations.

Before transfer quarterback Connor Barry even played a snap in practice, he took 300-400 reps in virtual reality, as Waters said he’s prepared the “right way.” Now with spring ball underway, Barry played first-team reps in the open practice last week with Becht limited and quarterback Alex Manske sidelined due to injury.

“I know Jayden Daniels was the guy that kind of made it famous at LSU in his last year there,” Waters said. “We did a test run at Iowa State. We didn't end up getting it, but it's my first time really being able to dive into it and see it firsthand and see the benefits of it for the guys.”

For freshmen quarterbacks Peyton Falzone and Kase Evans, Becht highlighted the fact that they can get more adjusted to the collegiate level by using the virtual reality system, as they can see various defenses and routes.

Waters called the virtual reality system an “incredible” resource to have, saying it’s the “highest level possible” for quarterbacks to learn an offense and get extra practice in.

“You don't get a million reps in practice, so it's important for the starter to go and get more,” Waters said. “Then also the guys who don't get that many reps in practice, they can still get those mental reps there. So I think it's invaluable, and I'm so glad we have it.”
