title: Sound, sweat and standing ovations | The 2026 Movin’ On BATTLE
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State’s student-run music festival, Movin’ On, hosted its annual Movin’ On BATTLE Tuesday night in Schwab Auditorium.

The Movin’ On BATTLE is a showcase of student bands and artists contending to be the opener for the headlining acts on the main stage of the festival.

Movin’ On is one of the top five largest student-run music festivals in the country as it enters its 51st year of tradition at Penn State. The festival is meant to be a send-off extravaganza for graduating seniors, hence the name.

Throughout the years, the festival has seen headliners from the top of the charts, including artists like Jack Harlow, Fall Out Boy, Pusha T and The All-American Rejects, among many more.

While the festival attracts many big names, Director of Public Relations for Movin’ On Dominic DiFrancesco said it’s also a chance to introduce some of Penn State's local music scene and give student bands and artists a chance to say goodbye to their fellow classmates in a booming fashion.

“Battle is probably the biggest way we connect with the local music scene and specifically student musicians,” DiFrancesco, a fourth-year marketing and vocal performance student, said.

The first band to open up the battle was the five-piece, hard rock outfit, Vicious Cyclist. Starting with some technical difficulties and sound issues, the band soon eased into their opening set.

The heavily stacked, seven-member band, Amelia & Sequoia, followed. Ripping into their opening song, “Blame Myself,” co-lead singer Amelia Severns commanded the stage with a concentrated passion alongside co-lead singer and rhythm guitarist Aiden Hood. Bass player, Rayne Terrier, rattled off a groovy bass solo to send the crowd and the band into a frenzy before returning the spotlight to Severns and Hood to blast into their next track.

Next up was folk and country songwriter Natalie McMahon, who self-describes her music as “if Noah Kahan and Taylor Swift had a baby, with Phoebe Bridgers as a cool aunt.”

She even channeled some “Jolene” era Dolly Parton for her closing track, “Mary Jane,” about a similarly upsetting romance.

“Some girls would yell, some girls would throw things, but I just wrote a song about it,” McMahon said.

To round out the order was: the hypnotizing jam-band, Ham On A Stick; heavy rock powerhouse, TUCK&ROLL; and solo artist, Sean Frosell, who played alongside the members of the band, Field Day.

Before even fully setting up their equipment, the friends of Field Day guitarist Joe Murabito and Frosell packed the center section seating and gave a boisterous round of applause and cheers.

Each of these bands put on a charismatic, fun and sometimes heavy performance that made them all in contention for the winning slot. But, of course, there can only be one winner. After the conclusion of the sets, the bands gathered together to hear which band will open for Movin’ On 2026.

As the bands waited — some packed tight together in nervous anticipation and others who stood relaxed and confident no matter the outcome — they listened closely as Director of Events, Brooke Casey, unfolded her paper to reveal the winner.

“And the winner of this year’s Movin’ On BATTLE is…Amelia and Sequoia!”

The auditorium erupted with cheers as every member of Amelia and Sequoia jumped and hugged each other while in near shock. High fives were given all around, with both hands, their faces were painted with ear-to-ear smiles, beat red from the laughter and adrenaline.

This local, student-run band, practicing out of garages and dorm rooms, would be opening the 2026 Movin’ On Festival.

“It felt like, I couldn’t even believe it…we’ve been really grinding very hard and losing sleep and overthinking, so the payoff is huge,” Hood, a second-year studying rehabilitation and human services, said.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Penn State women’s basketball hires all-time leading scorer Kelly Mazzante as assistant coach
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
A Penn State great is coming home.

Former Lady Lion Kelly Mazzante was hired as the first assistant coach on Wednesday after new coach Tanisha Wright was hired in March.

Homecoming in Happy Valley. Welcome back, @KellyMazzante! #WeAre pic.twitter.com/Bz3Hh1s3yj— Penn State Women's Basketball (@PennStateWBB) April 1, 2026

Mazzante came to Happy Valley in 2000 and put up the best career in Penn State history. The Williamsport, Pennsylvania, native currently sits as the No. 1 scorer in Penn State history with 2,919 points, the most points in a game with 49, the top two most points in a single season with 872 and 832 points and dozens of other school records.

In each of the four years with Mazzante, the Lady Lions made it to the NCAA Tournament. After falling in the first round in 2000, Mazzante helped her squad make it to the Sweet 16 in back-to-back years before ending her career with an Elite Eight appearance.

Mazzante was selected in the second round of the 2004 WNBA Draft by the Charlotte Sting, where she spent the next three seasons, averaging 4.7 points per game.

After the Sting folded in 2007, Mazzante was re-drafted to the Phoenix Mercury, where she spent three more years. In 2007 and 2009, Mazzante helped the Mercury to WNBA titles, where she shot 42.9% from deep in the first championship postseason.

Mazzante was injured in 2010 before concluding her WNBA career with the Atlanta Dream in 2011.

Following her WNBA career, Mazzante spent five seasons as the assistant coach at Indiana University of Pennsylvania, where she helped lead the team to its first two NCAA Division II Final Fours in school history.

In 2019, she moved across Pennsylvania and became the assistant coach at Bucknell, before being named the associate head coach with the Bison.

Before coming to Penn State, Mazzante spent three seasons at Georgetown, where she saw her position grow each year.

During her first year, Mazzante served as assistant coach, followed by recruiting coordinator in Year 2 and associate head coach in Year 3. Her first year started with a strong 23-12 record, which fell to 12-19 last year before rebounding to 14-17 this past season.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State women’s basketball’s Moriah Murray hits transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 3-point star is leaving Happy Valley.

After three years in Carolyn Kieger’s squad, Moriah Murray entered the transfer portal on Wednesday, according to Talia Goodman of On3.

NEWS: Penn State’s Moriah Murray plans to enter the transfer portal, her representative @Coach_Clark42 told @On3. The 5-8 junior averaged 9.4 ppg and shot 40% from deep this season. TRACKER: https://t.co/wYv1Ze6EPC pic.twitter.com/lGiS4rNPjJ— Talia Goodman (@TaliaGoodmanWBB) April 1, 2026

Murray was the seventh Lady Lion to leave Penn State after hiring coach Tanisha Wright, following seven players leaving last year.

The Dunmore, Pennsylvania, native entered the transfer portal last March, ultimately withdrawing her name just two weeks later.

Murray has several entries in the Penn State record book, including being tied for the most 3-pointers in a single game in the Bryce Jordan Center, the most 3-pointers made in a single game and the seventh-most 3-pointers made in a single season.

The 5-foot-8 guard started in 56 games during her Lady Lion career, recording 704 points with 188 total 3-pointers.

Murray spent her freshman summer at Drexel in 2022, before transferring to Penn State and redshirting her freshman year. During her redshirt-freshman year, she recorded the sixth-most 3-pointers made in a freshman year with 47 triples.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball’s Gracie Merkle enters transfer portal for 3rd straight season
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After entering the transfer portal last season and committing to Maryland before ultimately returning to Penn State, Gracie Merkle is back at it again.

On Wednesday, Merkle entered the transfer portal for the third time in her collegiate career, becoming the sixth Lady Lion to leave Happy Valley after coach Tanisha Wright was hired.

The 6-foot-6 center started her career at Bellarmine, where she was named the 2023 ASUN Freshman of the Year. Merkle averaged 15.1 points per game and led the nation with a 70.2% shooting percentage.

In her first year with the blue and white, Merkle led the squad in scoring with 15.5 points per game. She also led the team in rebounding with 8.6 boards per contest, producing 15 double-doubles on the year.

Despite sharing the workload with star Kiyomi McMiller during the 2025-26 season, Merkle improved again with 19.2 points per game on 72.5% shooting — once again leading the nation.

Merkle missed three games in January with an injury. She returned at the start of February, helping lead the Lady Lions to their first conference win of the year over Purdue with 19 points and a season-high 20 rebounds.

The All-Big Ten Media Third Team honoree capped off her Penn State career shooting 72.5%, the best single-season field goal percentage in school history. She departs Happy Valley as one of the best centers in Lady Lion history.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Satire | How to prepare for graduation
author: Jadzia Santiago | The Daily Collegian
description:
I know it’s hard to believe, but it’s true: Penn State’s giving you a degree.

That’s right, after all those missed classes and misdemeanors, you managed to pass. Huzzah! And with commencement just around the corner, here are my top tips to stay prepped and ready for when it’s your time to stumble the stage.

Drastically change your appearance

Think of graduation like a breakup, except it’s Penn State who’s dumping you instead of whoever you last managed to crash into. Time’s a-runnin’ out to usher in this new era with something bold and daring. Dye your hair, shave it, give yourself an eyebrow slit or wax them on your own for the first time.

Graduation alone doesn’t give you enough attention — you need to make this all about you, and the easiest way to achieve that is by botching your bangs so ridiculously that your local millennial would caption the after photos with “So … I did a thing.”

If that’s just not working for you, you could get yourself injured doing something stupid. Hop that fence, bike that hill or cross the street as if State College drivers care about the lives of pedestrians.

Forget stoles, medals or signs of real achievement, none of it beats the most expensive accessory of all: a cast. I’m sure your parents will love that one.

Assume your parents will just … figure it out

Speaking of your parents, now’s the perfect time to have a little more faith in them. They’ve known you’re graduating this semester for a while now, and surely, they’ll remember the information you’ve been repeating like a broken record since January.

If you have two of them, you know just how much they love communicating — especially when it comes to their shared offspring. And if there are other family members involved, like aunts, uncles and grandparents, it’ll be nothing short of a cakewalk.

They’ve been doing this for 20-odd years, so there’s a zero percent chance that they haven’t yet figured out transportation, lodging or whether they can make it at all.

That call you missed from them a few days back probably wasn’t about anything serious, like when you’re graduating, again? Where do we park? Where is it?

Ignore the text and don’t listen to that voicemail. Rest assured that when the day comes, you won’t call your family after the ceremony to see where they are, only to be hit with, “What? I thought it was (day you absolutely never said it was!)”

And, most importantly, it won’t somehow be your fault.

Let the weight of your mistakes swallow you whole

Now that you’re at the end of your time here, there’s never been a better moment to ruminate on everything you’ve done, that you shouldn’t have done and that you should’ve done.

Around this time of year, I like to remind myself hindsight is the perfect frame for comparison. And as you walk past people taking grad photos with multiple friends, rainbows of honor cords and job offers practically floating above their heads, know that the voice in your head is right: you’ve accomplished nothing.

That’s right, you somehow chose all the wrong clubs, pursued the worst professional path possible and made terrible friends who love you far less performatively than this. Only you, by the way, nobody else.

Forget everything good you’ve done and all your hard work. I know you’ve got at least one failed interview, friendship or love triangle where you were stuck between two buffoons and still chose the wrong one to mull over.

If I might recommend a setting for this activity, I’d advise you to plop yourself off to the side of Old Main Lawn. At least the girls taking photos are wearing pretty dresses, and you get to watch them have fun doing poses and — oh my god, is that an engagement ring she’s wearing? It’s HUGE!

Prepare to be in and out

After all of that, you can take comfort in the fact that commencement itself will be quick and painless.

Ignore what everyone else says, because I’m telling you, you should prep to hunker down for an hour tops. Wear your most uncomfortable and restrictive outfit, shoes you haven’t broken in and don’t bring anything with you. Oh, and make sure to sit next to perfect strangers.

Every moment of your graduation is bound to be engaging, so the time will absolutely fly by. And when that 60 minutes passes and that one guy is still talking, don’t pay any mind.

Whether you’re done within the next five minutes or you grow old and die while you’re waiting for this speech to be over, relax. It’ll all be over soon.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Recapping former Penn State women’s basketball players' collegiate seasons
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Over the last two years, Penn State has seen a large exodus from its program as various players have found new homes.

Ten former Lady Lions competed across the NCAA this season, with several clinching NCAA Tournament appearances, earning conference honors and others ending their season on sour notes.

Here’s a look at how these 10 athletes fared this season away from Happy Valley.

Shay Ciezki - Indiana

Senior guard Shay Ciezki had one of the greatest seasons among former Lady Lions, if not any player in the country, in her second year removed from Penn State.

Ciezki became the second player in NCAA history to average 20-plus points while shooting 50% from the field, 40% from 3-point range and 90% from the free-throw line this season, in addition to 3.8 rebounds and 3.1 assists per game.

While the Hoosiers ultimately fell in the second round of the Big Ten Tournament and failed to clinch the NCAA Tournament, Ciezki elevated her draft stock in her final season. In the final regular season game of her collegiate career, Ciezki eliminated her former team from Big Ten postseason contention while earning a standing ovation.

Gabby Elliott - Arizona State

Joining former Penn State coach Daniel Barber at Arizona State this year, Gabby Elliott was a star in her first year with the Sun Devils and last year of eligibility.

In her first injury-free season in her collegiate career, Elliott took Arizona State to its first NCAA Tournament appearance in seven years as she averaged 15.8 points, 4.9 rebounds, 2.5 assists and 1.6 steals in 35 starts.

Alli Campbell - Fairfield

While senior Alli Campbell put on an underwhelming performance in her first year away from Penn State, she accompanied Fairfield to a 28-5 record, MAAC championship and NCAA Tournament appearance.

However, the Altoona, Pennsylvania, native recorded just 2.6 points and 1.8 rebounds in five starts in her fifth year in the NCAA.

Talayah Walker - Georgia Tech

After making a pair of starts in Happy Valley during her freshman year, Talayah Walker’s success continued in Year 2 at Georgia Tech, where she started in 33 games.

Walker and the Yellow Jackets earned a WBIT appearance amidst a 14-19 record, despite Walker’s standout performance with 17.5 points and 6.1 rebounds per game.

Ivane Tensaie - UTEP

While it's been three years since Ivane Tensaie donned the blue and white, she continued to have success in her senior year at UTEP, averaging 10.8 points per game.

Earlier this year, Tensaie broke the UTEP single-game 3-point record, recording eight triples against Denver in late November.

Kylie Lavelle - St. John’s

After spending her sophomore year at Penn State, Kylie Lavelle experienced an elevated role with St. John’s, including 43 starts over the last two years.

Lavelle averaged 4.8 points, 2.9 rebounds and 1.1 assists per game as the Red Storm capped off their season with a devastating 74-26 loss in the first round of the WBIT.

Cam Rust - Seton Hall

Sophomore Cam Rust hit the transfer portal after her freshman year at Penn State, heading to Seton Hall where she averaged slightly better stats with 2.0 points and 1.6 rebounds per game.

Rust announced her intent to enter the portal again this year on Saturday.

Grace Hall - SMU

After two seasons in Happy Valley, Grace Hall took her talents to SMU, where she saw an increased role in her senior year.

Hall started a career-high 20 games this season, averaging 4.6 points and 3.9 rebounds per game.

Jill Jekot - St. Joseph’s

In her freshman season with Penn State, Jill Jekot played a minimal role with no starts and just six minutes per game. Those stats increased in Year 2 as she started 26 games with 28.6 minutes per game with St. Joseph’s.

Jekot improved in every statistical category with 7.1 points and four rebounds per game.

Ariana Williams - IU Indy

Now-sophomore Ariana Williams saw just seven appearances with Penn State, but made five starts in 29 games with IU Indy this season.

Williams recorded 5.6 points and 3.5 rebounds per game as her squad ended the season with an upset over No. 4-seeded Northern Kentucky in the first round of the Horizon League Tournament.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘The heart and the soul’ | Community mourns loss of John Sanchez, reflects on his ‘amazing legacy’
author: Saxtyn Lutz | For the Collegian
description:
In 2004, John Sanchez created the Penn State Powwow, a vibrant space where Native Americans can celebrate their heritage through traditional song and dance. On March 12, Sanchez passed away and those who knew him are mourning the loss of a man known for his humbleness and authenticity.

Sanchez, who is Yaqui and Apache, began teaching journalism at Penn State in 1997. He and his wife, Victoria Sanchez, founded the powwow together, an event that draws people from across the country. The couple served as co-directors of the powwow following its founding.

The powwow had its 20th annual celebration last weekend, the first hosted without Sanchez. Kathy Pletcher, who serves on the powwow committee, said the community honored him by gathering around his family and paying respects. They also set up a chair with a quilt and pictures of Sanchez at the head table.

“John was the heart and soul,” Pletcher said. “He was able to bring people together and build relationships and community.”

In addition to serving on the committee, Pletcher said Sanchez encouraged her and her daughter to dance in the powwow and represent their nation, Ojibwe. She said she wouldn’t have had the courage to be a dancer without his guidance, and that’s now become a core memory for her.

“He helped me feel like I was native enough,” Pletcher said. “He treated everybody like family. He made everybody feel welcome and loved.”

Ray Schmitt, a former student of Sanchez, said his news media ethics class was one of the best experiences he had in an undergraduate course. Schmitt, the director of the Student News Consortium, said everyone readily engaged in discussions facilitated by Sanchez because he created a welcoming learning environment.

“Professor Sanchez was really authentic with us. He talked to us in a way that I think made us feel valued and respected,” Schmitt said.

He recalled his excitement to have the opportunity of shaking Sanchez’s hand during his graduation ceremony. While a couple of semesters had passed since Sanchez had him as a student, Schmitt said Sanchez recognized him immediately and excitedly shook his hand while offering up a hearty congratulations.

Now, nearly twenty years later, Sanchez’s consideration and amiability remain memorable.

“It seemed like he always had a smiling expression on his face,” Schmitt said. “He had a warmth about him, a happiness about him, and that's really what resonates with me now.

Stephen Reighard served as technical support to a broadcast radio journalism course taught by Sanchez. Reighard, a radio technical instructor, said he admired Sanchez’s conversational and light-hearted interactions with students. He said Sanchez would check in on students, and despite his laid-back demeanor, students still respected him because he treated them as equals.

Reighard recalled a time when students were working against an in-class deadline, and Sanchez wandered around the room and teased them. He was especially humored by the time Sanchez jokingly distracted students by trying to convince them to buy firewood.

“How he approached the students and interacted with them, I was like, ‘Oh, that's something that I would want to do,’” Reighard said.

Tim Benally was an incoming Penn State student when he first experienced Sanchez’s kindness and support. Benally, a Navajo who lived on the nation’s reservation, learned Sanchez was the only native faculty member at the time.

He said he gave Sanchez a cold phone call, which he picked up right away. Benally described Sanchez as excited to learn he was coming to State College, and offered to take Benally and his family out to dinner upon arrival. Benally said Sanchez opened up his house to him, and he often met the professor and his wife at Panera on Friday mornings.

“My Penn State story wouldn't have been written for the most part without his love and support,” Benally said. “I don’t know what kind of prayers I prayed or (what) guardian angel is looking out to send somebody like him.”

Sanchez offered to support Benally, who graduated in 2021, throughout his time at Penn State and became a mentor to him. Benally now works in Washington D.C. to support the Navajo Nation.

“He left such an amazing legacy, and I don't even know how you fill shoes like that,” Benally said.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Beyond the chair | How the Designer’s Denn shapes style and connection
author: Kelsey Bonifer | For The Collegian
description:
The Designer’s Denn has been a consistent presence in State College for decades, since its Melinda Hooper first opened in 1981.

Located on 212 E Calder Way, the hair salon and spa has changed over time to best reflect the dynamic nature of the community.

“There is definitely a lot of energy,” Hooper, the salons manager, said. “There’s so much diversity with the students, who come in for different services.”

The energy visitors bring is something the salon embraces, according to Christy Marshall. She said she finds the steady stream of new clients — which includes both long-term residents and incoming students — and the vibes they come with “appealing.”

“It keeps it fun and interesting,” Marshall, an esthetician at the salon, said. “You’re always meeting new people with different backgrounds and stories.”

The salon provides a variety of services beyond haircuts in addition to its friendly ambiance. Clients can come in for anything from nail services, waxing and skincare treatments to hair coloring, styling and extensions.

“We offer everything you might need to feel and look your best,” Marshall said.

The salon is a one-stop shop for personal care because it offers specialty services like highlights, balayage, manicures, pedicures and even more advanced beauty treatments.

As trends change, the Designer’s Denn follows. Stylists and technicians have seen Penn State students and residents rock many different looks, from bold to natural.

The salon showcases a variety of low-maintenance styles and expressive choices, from “clean girl” looks and worn-in color to creative nail art and intricate designs.

Shayna Hawthorne said clients often come in with ideas for designs they’ve seen online and ask for personalized looks, fun shapes and detailed patterns.

“People love designs right now,” Hawthorne, a nail tech for the salon, said. “It’s fun to experiment with different styles of nail art.”

Hooper said the structured system at The Designer’s Denn is what makes it stand out, and it’s something the salon really values.

“A lot of the time, new stylists are just out of beauty school,” Hooper said. “They go through a consistent training program that is meant to improve both their technical skills and their clients' experiences.”

The focus on education goes beyond technical skill, as staff members said the salon prioritizes communication, flexibility and understanding different personalities.

To become a better stylist, Whitney Maitre said one of the most important skills to have is to really listen to your clients.

“Be honest and open,” Maitre, a hair stylist at Designer’s Denn said. “Feedback helps us give clients exactly what they want.”

From the moment a client walks in, the salon’s goal is to make them feel welcome and at ease. Consultations, conversations and attention to detail all play a role in shaping the experience.

Marshall said it’s important to be real, and even small interactions, like talking about preferences or worries, can make a big difference.

“It’s all about making people feel comfortable and heard,” Marshall said.

When expectations don’t always match up with reality, a focus on communication is even more important. Hawthorne said clients sometimes come in with pictures to help give ideas, but they may also need help figuring out what will work best for them.

“It’s about understanding both what they want and what they need,” Hawthorne said.

Like many other businesses in State College, The Designer’s Denn has to deal with a community that’s full of other salons that offer a variety of services and prices. Hooper said the team doesn’t focus on the competition, but instead on their own standards and relationships with clients.

“We focus on what we do and how we can improve,” she said.

However, there are still difficulties, particularly in a crowded downtown area where parking may be scarce. In order to adapt, the salon has changed its hours, closing on some Saturdays and reopening on Mondays.

One thing that hasn’t changed over time is the salon’s emphasis on an encouraging culture.

“We keep a very welcoming and warm environment here and guests can feel that,” Hooper said.

“For many customers, The Designer’s Denn is more than just a place to get their nails or hair done. It’s a place where they can unwind, express themselves and socialize.”

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: The most random Gen Eds you can take at Penn State
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
The moment you open LionPATH and realize you'll need one more Gen Ed, your entire personality shifts. Suddenly, you’re not picky — you’re just looking for something that fills the requirement and doesn’t ruin your semester.

Then you start scrolling and realize Penn State offers some very random classes. Not random in a bad way — random in a “wait this is actually kind of cool” way.

Here are some of the most unusual, out-of-the-box Gen Eds to take in Fall 2026.

ASTRO 7N: The Artistic Universe

This isn’t your typical science class, it’s more hands-on than you’d expect. Instead of memorizing planets and stars, you make projects to show what you’ve learned.

Think art projects inspired by space and galaxies instead of exams. There’s also an interactive game element that makes it feel less like school and more like something you actually want to do.

Perfect for non-science majors, it gives you the chance to explore astronomy in a way that doesn’t feel overwhelming,and honestly, way more fun than expected. You walk in thinking it’s a random Gen Ed and leave knowing way more about the universe than you planned.

ANTH 140: Anthropology of Alcohol

This is the class you put on your schedule and immediately screenshot. When you’re looking at it, it doesn’t seem like it should count for credit. It goes beyond drinking and looks at the history and culture behind alcohol, including how it started and why it’s important in different cultures.

You go from learning about ancient civilizations making early versions of beer to modern-day traditions that feel a little too familiar. It’s one of those classes where you’re sitting there thinking, wait this is actually kind of interesting, while also realizing you now have knowledge about something you usually only hear about during the weekend.

It’s educational, slightly iconic and easily one of the most talked-about Gen Eds — for obvious reasons.

FDSC 105: Food Facts and Fads

At first, it sounds like a basic nutrition class — but it’s way more than that. You learn how food actually goes from production to your plate, what’s really in the things you eat and why certain food trends blow up (even when they make no sense).

One week you’re talking about food labels, and the next you’re realizing you’ve never actually paid attention to what you’re buying. It also gets a little too real. You start reading ingredients, questioning your go-to snacks and lowkey rethinking your entire diet without meaning to.

It’s part science, part everyday life and part “wait, have I been doing this wrong?” Surprisingly useful for a Gen Ed, and one of the few classes that might actually change how you live day to day.

KINES 45: NAUI Basic SCUBA

This is one of those classes that sounds fake until you realize it actually counts for credit. Instead of sitting in a lecture, you’re learning how to scuba dive — like actually in the water.

You go over the basics of diving, safety and how your body reacts underwater, but the best part is getting to practice the skills yourself. There are some requirements (you do have to know how to swim,) but once you’re in, it’s easily one of the most hands-on classes you can take.

You’ll learn things like how to control your breathing, communicate underwater and use real diving equipment. It’s part science, part fitness and part “I can’t believe this is my Gen Ed right now.” Also, not many people can say they learned how to scuba dive for a grade.

BISCI 3: Environmental Science

The title makes it sound like it’s going to be all about nature, climate and ecosystems — which it is — but also not really. Somehow, this class turns into random debates and conversations you didn’t see coming.

One minute, you’re talking about the environment, and the next you’re discussing your opinions, your experiences and somehow even your feelings. You also keep your own journal, which makes it feel a little more personal than your typical Gen Ed. It’s less about memorizing facts and more about thinking and reflecting, which you probably didn’t expect from a science class.

People take the course thinking it’ll be easy, and while it’s not impossible, it definitely keeps you on your toes. You actually have to pay attention because you never really know where the lecture is going. It’s weird, a little unpredictable and honestly kind of entertaining — which is exactly what you want in a Gen Ed.

MORE BLOGS

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State men’s volleyball goes undefeated against ranked opponents
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 15 Penn State came up big this week, winning all three ranked games it played and officially moving to 14-7 on the season.

This week, the Nittany Lions opened with a 3-1 win against rivals Ohio State from neutral ground in Pittsburgh. From there, the blue and white went back to Rec Hall for a two-game series against the in-conference NJIT squad. Both games saw 3-0 sweeps from the blue and white.

Here’s a look at what happened during the week.

Strong serving game

This season, Penn State’s service has been somewhat up and down. But this week, it looked like the Nittany Lions were honed in.

Over the three games, the blue and white totaled 18 service aces.

Starting the three-game stretch out strong, the Nittany Lions tallied six aces against the Buckeyes on Wednesday. And while they only logged three against NJIT on Friday, Saturday was a different story with nine total.

The nine aces on Saturday marked the second-highest number of aces in one match for the blue and white, falling two short of the mark it set against Harvard in February.

Owen Rose had one ace against Ohio State and three against NJIT in the second game of the series. The redshirt senior currently ranks No. 3 in service aces in the EIVA.

Similarly, middle blocker Gaige Gabriel had two aces against the Buckeyes and one against the Highlanders on Friday. The middle blocker currently ranks No. 10 in service aces in the EIVA.

Beyond the pair of middle blockers, several Nittany Lions stepped up this week.

Sophomore Bennett Wilson played in all three matches this week, totaling four service aces, with his first of the season coming against Ohio State on Wednesday.

Matthew Luoma also tallied four aces on the week. With two of those coming on Saturday against NJIT, the redshirt junior managed only one service error that same match.

Even with nine aces on Saturday, there were only seven service errors across all three sets.

Service errors still impact the blue and white, however. Despite the good track record on Saturday, Penn State still recorded 34 errors across the remaining two games.

If the Nittany Lions are able to iron out those errors, they will have better odds heading into the upcoming postseason.

Week full of kills

Over the three matches on the week, the blue and white tallied 111 kills.

It shouldn’t be a shock that the Nittany Lions’ Sean Harvey was a massive part of the kill count this week. With a total of 39 kills, the redshirt sophomore continues to be a standout.

Harvey currently ranks No. 3 in the EIVA in kills per set and No. 29 in the NCAA in the same category. He also leads the EIVA in hitting percentage at .311.

Harvey wasn’t the only Nittany Lion putting up big numbers this week.

Luoma totaled 31 kills across the three-game stretch, with 13 against Ohio State.

Wilson also had a big week of kills. Despite not getting as much playing time as some of his teammates, the outside hitter still managed an impressive 18 kills across the three games.

The sophomore also tallied a career-high nine kills on Friday against the Highlanders.

Middle blocker Rose also had a season-high nine kills on Friday.

By the end of the three-match stretch, the Nittany Lions swept the EIVA weekly awards, too. Harvey earned the EIVA Offensive Player of the Week award, while Rose took home the EIVA Defensive Player of the Week award.

Penn State will look to keep their winning streak alive on the road this week as they travel to West Virginia for another two-game EIVA matchup against Charleston.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball falls apart in final frame, loses on walk-off to Pittsburgh
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After sweeping Rutgers last weekend, Penn State traveled west for the highly anticipated Keystone rivalry against Pitt on Tuesday night. The two squads met earlier in the season at the Houston Invitational, where the Nittany Lions routed the Panthers in their 11-2 win.

Despite being up four runs in the last frame, Pitt rallied back and ripped off five runs in its 8-7 comeback victory.

The blue and white’s offense started off hot and jumped on right-hander Kyra Pittman in the opening frame. The veteran pitcher hit left fielder Breanna Hanik to begin the game and second baseman Michela Barbanente advanced her into scoring position off a groundout.

Third baseman Brooke Klosowicz has been on fire recently, delivering once again. The junior blooped a RBI single into shallow right field and Penn State claimed an early 1-0 lead.

Building off the momentum, cleanup hitter Allison Oneacre lifted a routine fly ball to left fielder Camryn Murphy, but after she secured the out she committed on her throw into the infield and Klosowicz scored from second to extend the Nittany Lions’ lead, 2-0.

Pittman struggled with closing the inning when third baseman KK Esparza committed the Panthers second error on a routine groundball hit by designated player Jordan George. Center fielder Natalie Lieto continued the two-out rally with an infield hit to third, but George was caught stealing for the third out.

Unlike the first inning, the blue and white went down in order, but junior Bridget Nemeth delivered her best outing of the season. In the second, with a runner in scoring position, the Glendora, California, native kept the Panthers from scoring.

While both offenses were quiet in the third as both Pittman and Nemeth settled into a groove, the Nittany Lions plated another run in the fourth. Lieto smothered a one-out double to right and right fielder Cara Bohner followed with a double of her own into left center to widen Penn State’s lead, 3-0.

The blue and white left a few runners stranded in the frame, but Nemeth was dealing on the mound and mowing through the Panthers’ lineup. After a quiet fifth frame, both squads plated runs in the sixth.

Right-hander Mia Kliethermes was brought in for relief, but Penn State just extended its lead. George reached on a routine groundball to second baseman Tieley Vaughn, who committed Pitt’s third error of the night.

After a few groundouts, pinch runner Kalli Karwowski advanced all the way to third and catcher Macy Chamberlin knocked an RBI single into shallow right field, making the score 4-0.

While Nemeth pitched five scoreless innings, Pitt finally broke through in the bottom of the sixth. Esparza sent a two-out single up the middle and Calle Henne launched a two-run blast deep to right and cut Penn State’s lead in half, 4-2.

However, the Nittany Lions struck back in the seventh and plated three runs. Esparza committed another error at third and allowed Barbanente to reach. Oneacre was then hit, which set up George to do some damage.

The Pittsburgh native powered her third home run of the campaign deep over the center field wall and put the Nittany Lions up 7-3. Lieto followed with a double to left, but the blue and white couldn’t bring her home.

Despite being in a four-run hole, the Panthers mounted a complete comeback in their last breath. Coach Clarisa Crowell brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris to close out the game, but the freshman struggled.

Murphy launched a solo dinger to left and gave Pitt some life, 7-4. After Mackenzie Reese squeezed a double down the right field line and Addison Toczek followed with a walk, Crowell went back to her bullpen and turned to right-hander McKenna Young.

Yet the junior immediately threw a wild pitch, which allowed both runners to advance into scoring position. Vaughn then provided a clutch two-RBI single and advanced to second base off an error by Barbanente.

Now down just one run, Crowell turned to her third pitcher in the inning and called upon right-hander Abigail Britton. However, the sophomore walked Esparza and Henne knocked the game-tying RBI single.

Gracie Helton lifted a sacrifice fly to right and the Nittany Lions lost in heartbreaking fashion, dropping to 23-12 on the season.

Up Next

Penn State returns home for a three-game series against Maryland. The first game is slated for 5 p.m. on Friday.

McFadden became a big part of the Lady Lions’ squad down the stretch of the season, starting in eight games with 4.5 points per game. In her 28 games played, McFadden recorded six double-digit scoring outings.

title: Students innovate, create apps for 24-hour long Hackathon
author: Vasco Chen | The Daily Collegian
description:
Hack at Penn State hosted a 24-hour student-run Hackathon, which took place Saturday to Sunday in the Engineering Collaborative Research & Education Building.

The Hackathon is a biannual event that runs twice a year and welcomes participants from all backgrounds, celebrating innovation, creativity and the thrill of learning. Pranav Singhal, an organizer and judge of this year’s hackathon, said the name “hackathon” comes from the old definition of “hacking,” which means to throw something together quickly.

Singhal, a second-year studying computer science, said the purpose of Hack at Penn State is to give students a chance to see how quickly they can build, iterate and present what they’ve built. Being able to practice those skills at a hackathon, where they’re on a timer, is very important for development.

Kevin Poonthanomsook said he wanted to get his foot in the door and see the end-to-end life cycle of app development; his team created Social Lens, which analyzes facial features to determine how engaged a person is and their emotional state during a conversation.

“I have friends who are on the spectrum who don’t really know when to stop talking, especially in a video call environment when it’s hard to pick up these social cues,” Poonthanomsook, a second-year studying statistics, said. “So I thought to create a tool for them.”

Sahil Patel’s team created Defy, a live captioning service used to help the hard of hearing and deaf keep up with conversations in real time. It allows users to upload voice samples to new contacts, so Defy can recognize and label the names of speakers in live conversations.

“A lot of people here have incomplete ideas, unfinished products, but you never know who that will inspire,” Patel, a second-year studying computer science, said. “You never know what idea can change the world.”

There were multiple prize categories at the hackathon, including Grand prizes, Major League Hacking (MLH), Base 44 and College of IST challenges.

The first place winner of Hack at Penn State’s Grand Prize is Veriti, created by Darsh Tejusinghani. Veriti is a platform that allows users, specifically in the United Arab Emirates or Dubai, to anonymously upload reports of any incidents they see, which are then cross-validated using AI. Residents in varying locations can see what’s happening in their area.

“This is something that I care about because my family is back home in Dubai,”

Tejusinghani, a third-year studying computer science, said. “And I thought about how I don't know what's going on over there, but I realized speaking to my family that they don't either. And that's the bigger concern. They don't know if they need to take action, if they need to stay safe or anything as such.”

Tejusinghani advises students who want to participate in the event to spend more time on the idea, instead of rushing in without a clear vision of what the final project’s going to be.

“It took me about four hours figuring out each intricate detail and speaking to so many people,” Tejusinghani said. “I didn't want to just go in completely blind and keep changing everything.”

Although he did his project solo, he still went to all the team-building sessions.

“It’s a great place for people to network,” Tejusinghani said. “You meet a lot of people from diverse backgrounds with diverse levels of experience. I feel that connection over such a hobby is something that is really wonderful and hackathons are a great environment for that.”

title: 2027 CB Zachary Gleason Jr. commits to Penn State
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Penn State has its second commit in 2027, and it's another for the cornerback room.

Three-star Zachary Gleason Jr. announced his commitment to the Nittany Lions on Tuesday, following back-to-back visits for spring practice.

The Pittsburgh native, who plays for Central Catholic High School, ranks as the No. 738 player nationally, No. 75 overall cornerback and No. 21 player in Pennsylvania per the 247Sports composite rankings.

He held offers from schools such as Indiana, Ole Miss, Virginia Tech, North Carolina, Michigan State and Cincinnati.

His recent two visits to State College made it his eighth and ninth trips, and he previously had an official visit scheduled for the summer.

title: Penn State CB Zion Tracy emerging as hot name in spring ball with ‘chess piece’ versatility
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
Last Tuesday, Matt Campbell didn’t shy away from highlighting a returning senior as a player who has caught his eye.

At that point, Campbell had seen a handful of Pinstripe Bowl practices, winter workouts, watched film and only had one spring practice as the head coach of Penn State, but it was gushing praise he had for a cornerback.

“The one young man that has stood out to me from the day that I’ve gotten here to where we’re at — I think Zion Tracy is one of the best football players that maybe I have coached, to be quite honest with you, in terms of talent, ability,” Campbell said.

For Tracy, who has appeared in 40 games over the last three seasons and the last two as the starting nickel cornerback, consistency has been important.

“I’d say trusting my process and things I've been doing in the past, like my routine,” Tracy said. “Just keeping up with that keeps me on the same constant focus every day.”

Tracy is one of the older presences in the cornerback room, trailing behind just redshirt senior Audavion Collins as the eldest in the unit.

Now entering Year 4 at Penn State, Tracy is taking it upon himself to emerge as a leader under position coach Terry Smith.

“I've been more vocal with the team, just trying to raise everybody up,” Tracy said. “If someone feels like they having a bad day, I'll speak, everyone can hear me, get that energy up type of thing.”

As far as on the field, Tracy has been taking on a versatile role in the defensive backfield. His role at Penn State has been as a nickel corner with a sporadic showing of being a backup on the outside.

With Campbell taking over, and defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn and safeties coach Deon Broomfield joining the staff, Tracy is now seeing work at safety. Tracy has had conversations in the past about seeing work at the spot, but the current set of spring practices is the first time he’s seeing burn at the position.

“Being 10 yards back, I'll be able to see more, like when emotions happen,” Tracy said. “When I'm at nickel, I'm closer to the ball, so if something will happen it’s quick, right in front of me. So it could be easy to miss, and things like that. Safety is deep, so you could see everything.”

There are a lot of moving parts in the safety room, as Vaboue Toure’s the only returning scholarship safety for Penn State to join the influx of transfers. To help Tracy learn more about the position, he hit up former teammates Zakee Wheatley and Jaylen Reed to pick their brains about the spot.

Despite the limited experience, Campbell believes Tracy’s versatility in the secondary, paired with being an option at punt returner, where Tracy said his goal is to be the starter, can make him a dangerous player in all facets.

“I think Zion can be one of the best corners in the country. I think he can be one of the best safeties,” Campbell said. “I think he can play nickel. He can do a lot. And I think when you have a chess piece like Zion, it gives you the ability to start to kind of navigate the rest of the defensive structure around him.”

Campbell has seen Tracy make big plays, highlighting his 31-yard pick six against Ohio State at Beaver Stadium two years ago. He believes there are more impact moments in store for the veteran defensive back.

In a similar sentiment, Tracy wants to achieve what he thinks he’s always been capable of.

“Proving to myself to be the best I can be,” Tracy said. “Coming here as a freshman, I already knew the things I could do on the field, but I want to really prove that to the world, really prove it to myself.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Matt Campbell talks practice goals, D’Anton Lynn during Week 2 of spring ball
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
With his core values now plastered on the walls of Penn State’s team meeting room, Matt Campbell took to the podium in Lasch Building on Tuesday after his squad’s fourth spring practice.

There, Campbell discussed his goals for spring practice and some players who have stood out so far.

Here’s more of what Campbell had to say.

Who’s stepping up?

Campbell confirmed reports that quarterback Alex Manske is out for spring ball, but said the Iowa State transfer will be ready for fall camp.

With Manske out and Rocco Becht limited, plenty of younger guys like Peyton Falzone and Kase Evans have been getting meaningful reps this spring. But it’s a veteran presence who has stood out to Campbell.

“Connor Barry, he's come in, and he's really done a great job, probably been the surprise so far, of four days of practice, just his ability to get quality reps,” Campbell said. “I think we were really excited when Connor decided to come here, allowing him to be with some of our veteran players, and watch how he operates.”

Becht isn’t the only veteran starter out for spring practice, however, as the Nittany Lions’ injury list is quite long right now. That group includes wide receivers Brett Eskildsen and Chase Sowell, who are expected to be two of Penn State’s starters.

In their absences, Campbell mentioned Karon Brookins, Zay Robinson and Koby Howard as younger players who have stepped up so far.

“To be able to evaluate your football team, I don't know if you could ask for a better situation, for those young guys to be able to step up and kind of show what they can do,” Campbell said. “And honestly, they've been impressive so far, and so it's great to see.”

D’Anton Lynn

The splash hire for Campbell this offseason was defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn, who Penn State poached from USC and is a former letterman, marking Campbell’s first time with a defensive coordinator besides Jon Heacock in 12 years.

As Lynn works to install his scheme and the players get a taste of his coaching style, Campbell has kept a watchful eye and loves what he’s seen so far.

“Watching coach Lynn teach, I think one of the great gifts that he has, and what was so impressive to me even through the interview process, is just his ability to articulate what he wants out of the defense, what the expectations are,” Campbell said. “He's a great holistic teacher, and obviously his time in the National Football League makes you be that. Your time restrictions are so short in that league that you got to be a great communicator and a great teacher.”

Safeties coach Deon Broomfield said last week that Lynn is striving for “obnoxious communication” this spring, and Campbell has enjoyed watching his new defensive coordinator get the coaching staff on the same page.

“I think that's been the fun thing to watch him, number one, align the coaching staff on verbiage, communication, everybody speaking the same language,” Campbell said. “And then to watch that coaching staff start to implement what the defense needs to look like, what are the standards? How are we communicating through everything? And I think they've done an unbelievable job.”

Spring practice goals

Campbell has the tough task of getting this brand-new Penn State team ready to play football in five months, and spring practice is a crucial aspect of that.

But Campbell’s goal for these 15 practices is to build a mentally and physically tough team that can go through hard times and come out stronger.

“Don't get slow and right twisted with intentful, meaningful, passionate reps,” Campbell said. “And there are wins and losses on every rep that happens in the sport of football. And what I want to see is, how do we respond? How do you respond when it goes good? How do you respond when it doesn't go well? Because all those things are coming, right?”

Campbell referenced Duke’s recent men’s basketball loss to UConn in the Elite Eight, emphasizing that no team is perfect and there are going to be losses along the way, even if that’s a single rep in practice.

“We all want to be great coaches, and we want it to all look perfect, but that's not the world we live in,” Campbell said. “Tough times are going to happen. Good things are going to happen, and it's the team that's mentally tough enough and physically tough enough to be able to just consistently keep showing up and being the best in the moment, those are the ones that have the ability to win.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State baseball drops home stand behind inconsistent bats, pitching woes
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions earned mixed results over their five-game homestand against three different opponents and finished 2-3 on the week, which dropped their record to 8-18 on the season.

Penn State won 8-6 over St. Bonaventure on its first Dollar Dog Night of the year, but then endured a shutout 9-0 loss to Bucknell in the next game.

The homestand wrapped up with a Big Ten series loss against Illinois. While the blue and white started strong and recorded a comeback 9-8 win in its series opener, the squad lost 16-5 and then 6-0 in the next two games.

Now, the Nittany Lions stay home and face UCF at 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday before traveling for an away conference series against Nebraska this weekend.

Here are some common themes that occurred throughout the last week of games.

Inconsistent offense

While Penn State recorded 22 runs over the span of these five games, the team was also shut out twice by both Bucknell and Illinois. The blue and white totaled just five hits in its first shutout loss and only three hits in the series finale loss to Illinois.

The bats have been up and down throughout the campaign as the offense led by junior Bryce Molinaro and senior Michael Anderson sometimes clicks on all cylinders, but is also completely shut down in many losses. Despite the few shutout losses, Penn State’s offense rallied down 6-0 in the series opener against Illinois and accumulated 11 hits in the win.

Additionally, the blue and white rely on power with a tied sixth most home runs in the Big Ten with 31 and the squad continued raking. Anderson homered twice last week to bring his team-best total to 11 on the season. Molinaro trails behind Anderson with seven dingers, after he belted two as well.

However, the Nittany Lions need more consistency from their lineup. It’s essential for the blue and white’s bats to provide runs because their pitching staff has struggled with limiting runs all season.

Bad pitching continues

There’s been one main theme to the Nittany Lions' struggles this season and that is their pitching staff woes. The squad continues to possess the worst team-ERA in the Big Ten at 8.36 while also surrendering the most hits and runs.

In the loss to Bucknell, freshman Ethan Bauerschmidt delivered a rough start, surrendering five runs on three hits and two walks over 2.1 innings.

Junior Mason Horwat lasted just 1.2 innings in his start in the series opener against Illinois. The Fighting Illini jumped on the Pittsburgh native and plated six runs on three hits and five walks.

Sophomore Ben Hudson followed his quality outing against Purdue with a rocky start to Illinois. The Medford, New Jersey, native allowed six runs on five hits and three walks over just three innings.

While freshman Isaiah Shayter had a solid outing in the series finale against Illinois, allowing just three runs over 5.2 innings, the bullpen didn’t offer much relief. Senior Dimond Loosli allowed two runs over just 0.2 innings.

With the pitching staff still struggling, it will be hard for Penn State to turn the season around until its pitchers can settle down opposing teams’ lineups.

title: Three executive branch tickets participate in annual UPUA debate
author: Tyler Nolt | The Daily Collegian
description:
Leading up to the University Park Undergraduate Association’s (UPUA) annual election on Wednesday, the student government has historically hosted a debate to showcase the candidates to the student body.

Each executive ticket — Mann-Calderon, Hogan-Lopez and Hatch-Smith — was given a chance to introduce themselves and their platform. Each ticket was then asked questions by the UPUA’s election commission, which comprises the judicial branch.

Candidates were allocated three minutes per question and another two minutes for rebuttal.

What is the single biggest issue facing Penn State students today, and how do you plan on addressing it?

Tyler Mann shared his belief that the most significant issue is connectivity among students. He described this both in the context of student-to-student and student-to-representative relationships.

To fix this, Mann, a fourth-year studying integrative science, said he would be present on campus, including showing up to student organization events along with hosting more public office hours.

Kat Hogan followed by saying accessibility of basic needs was the largest issue. She referred specifically to the accessibility of food, housing and mental health services. To address this issue, Hogan, a third-year studying energy engineering, pointed out many campus organizations she intends to collaborate with.

Last to answer was Akito Hatch, who said the student body’s biggest issue is “receiving the best student experience that students can.”

Hatch, a third-year studying criminology and political science, elaborated on the ideas of the other tickets, pointing out their benefits to the student body, and contributing to their time in college.

“I think that this ultimately all kind of adds to our one goal, which is receiving the best student experience that we can kind of give each other,” Hatch said.

How do you plan on leading an executive branch that can do an effective job of creating a more cohesive environment between all three branches of UPUA?

Hogan pointed to her and Lopez’s experience within UPUA to showcase familiarity with the workings of each branch. She then presented her idea to utilize meetings between the leaders of each branch more than the 20th assembly did, as well as continuing to develop an outline of UPUA’s leadership positions.

Hatch said there are a number of positive relationships formed through collaboration within UPUA, and those should be maintained. However, he acknowledged there are some downsides to this, and stricter leadership from the executive branch is necessary.

Mann highlighted his time as president of the Association of Residence Hall Students (ARHS). He said his leadership style was one of motivation, and he would want to focus on empowering leaders within UPUA to continue improving the lives of fellow students.

“I don't want to just hold people accountable,” Mann said. “I want to motivate them to be the best versions of themselves and use the position of president in order to provide them the opportunities to best accomplish it.”

How will you improve the transparency of the UPUA and ensure that the organization is making effective relationships with the student body, student organizations and our community partners?

Hatch started by saying UPUA is already a transparent organization, and that transparency and outreach shouldn’t be major focuses. He said UPUA should instead focus on “things and items that students care about.”

Caleb Calderon, vice presidential candidate on the Mann-Calderon ticket, didn’t share in Hatch’s perspective, and instead said the most important aspect of transparency within UPUA should consist of elevating the organization's visibility among students. Calderon, a second-year studying computer science, said his concern is that a large number of students may be unaware of UPUA and its work.

Again referencing the work she has already done, Hogan said as speaker of the assembly she has implemented new initiatives to improve transparency. The initiatives mentioned include publishing the voice recordings of general assembly meetings, posting meetings agendas to social media and making voting and attendance records public.

What is one specific, tangible policy or initiative that you plan on addressing once you take office, and what steps will you take to ensure it gets done?

Mann started by expressing a deep admiration for the work of the legislative branch, followed by his plan to further connections between said branch and the student body. This includes reaching out to other student councils and student representatives, along with providing opportunities for representatives and fellow students to connect.

Maria Lopez, vice presidential candidate on the Hogan-Lopez ticket, said she would address the safety of international students. Lopez, a third-year studying political science, said she would do so through the creation of safe spaces on campus.

“We have already worked with Student Legal Services,” Lopez said. “We plan to keep on working with them in order to have forums and have discussions and organize these meetings funded by the UPUA, organized by the UPUA, where people can help us make safe spaces.”

Hatch said an initiative he’d like to implement is better promotion of the events of registered student organizations. UPUA does well to ensure these events are funded, but more could be done to garner interest, he added.

What do each one of your tickets feel is the biggest problem of UPUA, and how does your ticket plan to address it?

Hogan said transparency was on her mind, but she also sees a need for more internal efforts to motivate and empower legislatures. She said as president, she would continue showing up to as many meetings as possible across UPUA’s branches.

Hatch built on Hogan’s answer and said stronger leadership would be the best motivator for student representatives. The second part of his response described a fading of passion, both in the UPUA and throughout the student body as a whole.

Mann pointed to the connections the other tickets have within the student government and said if elected, those prior relationships could get in the way of their work. He said his lack of established relationships would allow him to come in unbiased, and help him to better advocate for the student body.

Undergraduate students at University Park will be able to vote digitally through https://vote.psu.edu/. The ballot will be available on Wednesday at 9 a.m. to 9 p.m.

title: Meet Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon: UPUA presidential and vice presidential candidates
author: Kiera Ginn | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor's note: This article is part of the Collegian’s profiles of all candidates in 2026-27 student government presidential election. Read about the Hatch-Smith and Hogan-Lopez tickets.

Tyler Mann and Caleb Calderon are one of three pairs running for president and vice president of the University Park Undergraduate Association's (UPUA) 21st assembly.

UPUA holds an annual election, where students run with the intention to serve their peers and represent them at Penn State. This year’s election day is Wednesday, and students can vote online at vote.psu.edu starting at 9 a.m.

Mann, a third year studying integrative science, and Calderon, a second year studying computer science, have not previously held positions in UPUA, but Mann said it’s an opportunity for new perspectives.

“The UPUA kind of exists in a bubble, and both other tickets also exist within that UPUA bubble,” Mann said. “I think that they have the desire to reach out and collaborate with other organizations. But coming from the outside, I have the perspective of being the organization that the UPUA collaborated with. By bringing in those external leaders to the executive branch, I think we can best shape the UPUA to push that sense of collaboration.”

Uma Aparicio, campaign manager, said the strength of their campaign is the different experiences across campus as leaders and students that are represented within their team.

“I think those attributes really make us stand out,” Aparicio said.

Mann is the current president of the Association of Residence Hall Students (ARHS,) the vice president for Network Excellence in Undergraduate Science and founder of Multicultural Innovators in Computer Science. He said these experiences have prepared him to undertake the role of UPUA president.

“My leadership journey is anything but linear,” Calderon said. “I really liked leading. I like to make an impact on campus. I just like representing communities and really bringing people together.”

The Mann-Calderon campaign seeks to establish a connection between student leaders and the students they represent.

“‘Redefining tradition’ is the tagline for my campaign, because I want to make sure that the UPUA continues to work to represent students and pass amazing legislation,” Mann said. “But I want to make sure that students feel connected to what it is that the UPUA is doing.”

If elected Mann and Calderon plan to strengthen the bonds between UPUA and the student body, according to Aparicio.

“I feel like a lot of people on campus, especially small organizations, feel like they don't have that connection with UPUA,” Aparicio, a fourth-year studying international relations, said. “Hosting more town halls or just things that bridge that connection to the smaller organizations to the UPUA. That way, if they have questions, they can come and ask us.”

On a university-wide level, Calderon said he wants to empower students through a sense of community.

“I think building real community and really turning student voices into action is the most important,” Calderon said. “I've spent my time here on campus creating spaces where people feel like they belong and I really want to bring that same energy to UPUA.”

Awareness and overall student engagement in UPUA are what Mann and Calderon said they’re looking to address. UPUA’s 20th assembly election saw a low voter turnout, with less than 10% of the undergraduate study body casting a vote.

“I think this campus needs to know more about the UPUA,” Aparicio said. “ I've had so many interactions where I've asked people, ‘Do you know what UPUA is?’ the number one answer is literally no.”

Mann said connecting the Penn State community helps increase the visibility of UPUA.

“I think most students don't know how they can benefit from the UPUA, but truly, the UPUA has the capacity to benefit every student,” Mann said. “Every student has the chance to benefit, and every student has a voice.”
