title: Dollar Dog Night, yoga, stargazing | This week in State College
author: Emma Naysmith | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the weather warms up and State College gets that spring feel, there’s a number of events for everyone to enjoy.

Monday, March 30

Yoga & Meditation

Weekly Yoga & Meditation hosted by Penn State Health Promotion and Wellness continues to take place 12:30 to 1:30 p.m. in Room 232 of the HUB-Robeson Center.

Available to all-levels without registration, participants have the opportunity to engage their mind and body while taking part in this restorative practice.

Additional certified instructor-led sessions will continue from 5:30 to 6:30 p.m. in 107 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center and Wednesday noon to 1 p.m. at the Palmer Museum.

Charleston Lesson and Social Dance

Hosted by the Swing Dance Club, this event features Charleston dance lessons that mimic what was seen in the jazz clubs of the ‘20s.

This free event is available to all individuals, with no prior experience required. The lesson will begin at 7 p.m. with a dance party beginning at 8 p.m. in Room 201 of the State College Municipal Building.

Tuesday, March 31

Craft & Cultivate: Make Your Own Paper Plant Plot

Hosted in collaboration with Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) and the Student Farm Club, this event encourages attendees to experience the mental health benefits of nature throughout all seasons.

This free event will be happening in the HUB Main Lounge and will take place from 3:30-5:0 p.m.

Salsa Lessons

Salsa Lessons facilitated by the Penn State Social Dance Club will continue to be held on the second floor of the State College Municipal Building.

Open to students and community members alike, this 6-8:30 p.m. event does not require a partner for attendance.

Wednesday, April 1

Flourish Fest

Health Promotion and Wellness brings back its Flourish Fest from 11-2:30 p.m. in the HUB’s Alumni Hall.

This free event welcomes all for an opportunity to learn about resilience and stress management. In addition to free resources, this is an opportunity for snacks, giveaways and meaningful conversation.

Dollar Dog Night – Penn State Baseball vs. University of Central Florida

The Nittany Lions will face off against the UCF Knights at 5:30 p.m. at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

In addition to free entry, spectators can purchase hotdogs for $1.

Thursday, April 2

Bird Walk

Hosted by The Arboretum at Penn State, bird-watching attendees are welcome to a walk headed by Avian Expert Joseph Gyekis. Observers have the opportunity to catch birds in their travel back from winter migration locations.

The walk will take place 8-9:30 a.m. starting at the Overlook Pavilion. Those joining are encouraged to dress for the outdoor weather conditions.

Fly Fishing Film Tour (F3T) – 20th Anniversary

The Spring Creek Chapter of Trout Unlimited and TCO Fly Shop are partnering to bring The Fly Fishing Film Tour to State College beginning at 7 p.m. in The Friedman Auditorium.

This film will feature spotlights of wild places, inspiring stories and a close look into the dedication behind fly fishing.

Tickets can be purchased here.

Friday, April 3

Penn State Softball vs. Maryland

The Lady Lions and Terps will meet at 5 p.m. at the Nittany Lion Softball Park and Beard Field.

This event is free to the public.

First Friday

Downtown State College Improvement District will host the First Friday series bringing downtown State College to life.

During these monthly events, others are encouraged to enjoy community, after-hour access to shopping and galleries, free food and beverages and live music.

The event will take place from 5-8 p.m. in the MLK Plaza.

Saturday, April 4

Penn State Softball vs. Maryland

The Lady Lions will play the Terrapins for the second game of its series at 2 p.m. at the Nittany Lion Softball Park and Beard Field.

This event is free to the public.

The Arboretum at Penn State’s Star Party

Stargazers and friends are welcome from 8:30-10:30 p.m. to the Arboretum’s Event Lawn to see the waning gibbous moon and potential planet Mercury near its western sky’s highest elevation.

This event is free for adults 18+ and will feature telescopes and astronomical expertise by the Central Pennsylvania Observers.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: 6 candies to fill your basket with this Easter
author: Ava Krysko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Most people would consider the best part about Easter the candy. After all, there’s nothing better than getting to have a sweet treat, especially when it's being supplied by an oversized, anthropomorphic rabbit.

As the holiday approaches, stores become filled with bags and bags of the most delectable treats in bright, multi-colored packages that scream "buy me!" How can you possibly choose just a few packs with all of the options available?

Whether you're into gummies or chocolate, this list will provide you with six mouthwatering candies to have in your basket and satisfy your sweet tooth this Easter. They're all available at State College’s Target, so they'll be perfect to stock up on.

1. Jelly beans

What Easter basket is complete without some sort of jelly bean? Although these can be controversial (especially the black licorice-flavored ones), jelly beans have a certain appeal with their chewy textures and fun colors such as pink, green and red.

If you really want to make Easter exciting, consider buying Jelly Belly's game, “Bean Boozled,” and test out all sorts of flavors from tutti-frutti to old bandage.

2. Peeps

Another Easter classic, the beginning of March means that Peeps will be lining the shelves. These marshmallowy, animal-shaped treats are perfectly on theme for the springtime holiday.

Of course, there's the traditional Peep colors like yellow and pink, but this year they’ve also added more interesting flavors in collaboration with big brand names such as Rice Krispies Treats, Pop-Tart and Dr. Pepper.

Recently, there's even been a TikTok trend going around in which people eat stale peeps that have been left open to dry out, allowing the Peeps to become crunchy. If that's something you think you might be into, definitely grab some of these flavorful chicks.

3. Cadbury Mini Eggs

If you’re a fan of chocolate but still want to get into the Easter spirit, Cadbury Mini Eggs may be the best choice for you.

With a hard shell surrounding decadent milk chocolate, these eggs will definitely fulfill your sweet treat cravings and look cute doing it. They're decorated in pastels, with brown dots to look similar to actual eggs.

Not to mention that these eggs are bite-sized, so they’re the perfect thing to snack on as you wait to dye eggs or go Easter egg hunting.

4. Swedish Candy

If you’ve been on social media recently, you’ve probably seen Swedish candy trending. These melt-in-your-mouth gummies are somewhat fresh to the Easter scene and will add something different to your basket.

Although these candies were popularized in America by companies such as BonBon in New York City, they've grown increasingly widespread with major chain retailers such as Target carrying similar products.

For example, one of the most well-liked brands, Bubs, which features soft, oval-shaped gummies, can be found at Target.

Another brand Target carries is Sockerbit, a Swedish candy store that opened up in Los Angeles. Sockerbit candies include everything from sour cherry cola gummies to foamy strawberry dream gummies.

5. Sour Patch Kids (Bunny or Egg-shaped)

If you're into a more sour option, Sour Patch Kids has got you covered. For springtime and Easter, the brand offers bunny or egg-shaped candies that are as adorable as they are delicious.

Even if it may seem a bit boring to default to a slightly different design with the same old flavor, there's nothing wrong with staying true to the classics.

If you want to get a bit more adventurous, though, you can always pick up a pack of Sour Patch Kids jelly beans to experiment with.

6. Gerrit's Satellite Wafers

Although this specific candy can't be found at the State College Target, it can be found at Five Below.

You may remember your grandparents giving this to you around Easter-time. The outside looks unassuming with its pastel-colored surface but, inside, it is filled with tiny, candy beads that add a delightful crunch.

They're definitely not as sweet as the other candies on this list (and the outside does taste a bit like paper), but, overall, Satellite Wafers have a certain quality to them that is reminiscent of Easter.

Apparently, the company has also released a similar version but instead of candy beads inside, there's sour powder, which could appeal to those of you who are interested in the Sour Patch Kids option.

As you gear up for Easter festivities and grab your basket items, don't forget to check these candies off your list. If none of these specific candies are up your alley, though, don't worry — simply walking through the aisles of any store's Easter section should help you find something you like.

And, of course, the Easter Bunny is magic, so he'll know exactly what candies will fulfill your cravings. Happy Easter!

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: 8 songs about love, womanhood by women
author: Ashlyn Kafer | The Daily Collegian
description:
From the gentle croons of Ella Fitzgerald to the powerful vocals of Beyoncé, women have brought innovation, artistry and raw talent to music for generations.

Women are responsible for numerous songs that revitalized the industry and changed the way we think about the artistry of music. Here are eight songs about love and femininity by women.

“Stoneface” by Veruca Salt

If you’re against the nonchalance epidemic, “Stoneface,” on Veruca Salt’s sophomore album “Eight Arms to Hold You,” is the song for you.

Veruca Salt, a riot grrrl-adjacent band of the '90s, brings together alternative sound with feminine pop vocals to create raunchy, yet revolutionary, melodies throughout its discography.

“Stoneface” emulates the versatility of Veruca Salt. Centered around the vocals and crisp guitar of leads Louise Post and Nina Gordon, the singing in “Stoneface” flips between breathy and seductive to raspy and edgy, accentuating the dichotomy of womanhood.

“I’ll Be Seeing You” by Billie Holiday

Billie Holiday, also known as “Lady Day,” made history as the first Black woman to sing in an all-white orchestra, desegregating the music industry one note at a time.

In “I’ll Be Seeing You,” Holiday’s jazz tone and nostalgic cadence carries you through the eyes of a woman, seeing the world in her perspective and how she can find love in each step, tree branch or ray of sunshine.

“Since U Been Gone” by Kelly Clarkson

Whether you’re familiar with this song from the 2012 film “Pitch Perfect,” or simply from the radio, there’s no doubt Kelly Clarkson’s lead single from her album “Breakaway” is the ultimate anti-situationship, female empowerment track.

Between Clarkson’s impressive riffs throughout the song and the lyrics that anyone who has loved and lost can relate to, “Since U Been Gone” remains a power ballad encouraging all women to see the bright side of their heartache.

“Saccharine” by VIOLENT VIRA

VIOLENT VIRA released her debut single “I Don’t Care” in 2021 and has since become one of the burgeoning voices in the rock and metal scenes, breaking through the male-dominated genre as a Mexican-American woman.

“Saccharine” is hardcore — using the juxtaposition of emphatic growls and soft vocalism to create an edge that cuts deep and challenges the idea of giving up being understood to be complacent, advocating for women to stand up for their emotions regardless of their perception.

“She’s So Gone” by Naomi Scott

Disney Channel original movie “Lemonade Mouth” not only brought us one of the best plotlines of the 2010s, but an amazing soundtrack featuring songs like “She’s So Gone.”

In the film, Naomi Scott sings about coming out of her shell after being betrayed by her boyfriend, exclaiming that the girl he knew is “so gone.”

This track is perfect for any girl to scream along to in the car or belt out in the shower, accepting yourself for who you are and moving on from criticism that puts you in a box.

“Trust In Me” by Etta James

One of the foundational soul-style singers and industry pioneers, Etta James brought smooth, sultry singing and epic ballads to music. With tracks like “At Last,” James made an everlasting impression on the future for women in music, especially black women.

“Trust In Me” is another popular track by James, each beat fueled by her characteristic trills and gritty riffs. She uses imagery of nature to illustrate her insecurities when it comes to love.

“No One Noticed” by The Marías

The Marías are headed by lead singer María Zardoya, whose angelic vocal cadence, blunt lyricism have launched the band into the limelight. Zardoya sings about the feelings women often keep unsaid, creating melodies surrounding a sickening sense of devotion and willingness to drop everything for the one you love.

“No One Noticed” is no different, telling the story of feeling so desperate for someone that you’re losing your mind, but once you’re with them again, you can’t help but want to disappear — reaching out to all the women who feel they go unnoticed despite expressing their love.

“Cry Baby” by Janis Joplin

With a voice that rivaled rockstars of the time like Paul McCartney and Bob Dylan, Janis Joplin disrupted the 60s with her intense belting style and sharp lyricism that criticized gender stereotypes.

Joplin calls out deception by her lover in “Cry Baby,” acknowledging her own power in the relationship when he comes crawling back to her after being left by another woman, echoing the strength of women outside of their affiliation with their male counterparts.

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: 'We are the first Americans' | Penn State Powwow celebrates 20th anniversary
author: Sara O'Connell | The Daily Collegian
description:
On Saturday and Sunday, the 20th anniversary of the Penn State Powwow was celebrated by vast groups of people at the C3 Sports Complex in State College to celebrate traditional Native American culture and community.

The event, which lasted 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. featured Native-made art by vendors and performances of Native American drum and dance. This year, the powwow celebrated both Native American people and the founder of the Penn State Powwow, John Sanchez.

Jo Dumas attended the powwow with her family and has been coming to the event for 16 years, as she said she loves the community the powwow offers.

“It’s a beautiful time to build and share community and love and nurture with one another,” Dumas said.

Her husband, a Navy veteran, joined in the ceremony, along with six other veterans, by carrying the United States Navy flag when honoring veterans.

“The powwow is our renewal of our sphere, our connection with [the] creator and our connection with one another,” Dumas said.

Sanchez created the Penn State Powwow to connect to the culture approximately 20 years ago, where the first powwow was hosted on campus.

“We lost John this year, but the Powwow lives on,” Dumas said.

During Sanchez’s memorial, the family of Sanchez stood in the center of the dance area and people went up to shake hands and hug them to commemorate the loss.

The event began with the first dance song, an Honor Song where they played traditional American Indian music. Several people at the event dressed in traditional powwow regalia, including jingle dresses.

Ron Williams said as both a former Penn Stater and Native American, the Penn State Powwow means a tremendous deal to him. He created his own powwow in Pottsville, Pennsylvania, where he’s from, to connect to his culture near home.

“We are the first Americans,” Williams, a dancer, said. “We are the first people. Wherever we go, we are on native land.”

Williams, as a retiree from the United States Army and a United States Navy veteran, carried one of the flags during the opening ceremony to commemorate his time in service.

Williams said his family comes from the Southern Ute Tribe in southern Colorado, and his mother was raised on a reservation called Shoshone in Wyoming.

“We are still here,” Williams said. “You can do everything you can to eliminate us. That’s the Native cry that we have popularized very vehemently. There is no such thing as a lost people.”

He said they will continue celebrating, even though colonizers attempt to drive out Native American culture. Powwows mean a lot to him in terms of Native American culture, as powwows are the connections, the people and the community, he added.

“We use the term powwow, but it’s more than that,” Williams said.

Michael Galban, though not from the area, attended the Penn State Powwow to engage in a community event before his powwow season begins in the upcoming months.

Galban, a Southern Straight dancer, said his dance style originated in Oklahoma, where the clothing is more tailored, fitted and coordinated.

“There’s no pressure here,” Galban said. “That’s what I like about (the powwow),” Galban said.

He said bringing community to powwows was a way American Indians could celebrate their culture, sometimes the only way they get to celebrate it. He said though most have other traditions, this is a way to bring communities together.

“It’s one way you can have people from different communities and Indigenous culture sharing something,” Galban said.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State softball logs win over Rutgers in series finale, sweeping road trip
author: Lauryn Hann | For the Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions came out swinging in the early innings of their away series finale matchup against Rutgers this afternoon.

Ultimately, Penn State kept its form inside the batter's box the entire day, en route to a 7-5 win to secure the series sweep.

The Nittany Lions quickly showcased their depth offensively this afternoon, scoring multiple runs in the first three innings, while keeping Rutgers under control with good pitching and even better hitting.

The game's first run came in the top of the first inning after Jordan George was hit by a pitch with the bases loaded, driving in Michela Barbanente for an unearned run. Natalie Lieto and Allison Oneacre also advanced to scoring positions, truly setting the tone for a productive start for the blue and white.

Rutgers then responded immediately in the bottom half of the inning. Baileigh Burtis doubled to center field, which brought in Riley Hwang. And shortly after, Addie Osborne followed that up with an RBI single, giving Rutgers a 2-1 lead to finish the first inning.

Penn State regained control in the second inning as Allison Oneacre singled to center, driving in two runs. However, Brooke Klosowicz was thrown out trying to advance to third.

This play really highlighted both Penn State’s aggressive baserunning and challenged Rutgers’ defense.

To close out the second inning, Barbanente and Bree Hanik crossed the plate, putting the Nittany Lions ahead 3-1.

In the third inning, Penn State extended its lead when Gabby Gradishar singled through the right side, and Kalli Karwowski and Natalie Lieto scored.

During the third inning, the Nittany Lions were getting hot at the plate, putting up a run to complete the inning.

Rutgers managed to get on the board again on a wild pitch later in the inning, making the game a close one at 5-3.

The top of the fourth inning saw even more action as Lieto fouled out to right field, Oneacre advanced to second and Klosowicz moved to third. Klosowicz then reached home base and scored on an unearned error to add to the Nittany Lions' lead.

Rutgers quickly responded to that with a solo home run from freshman Charleigh Schuettler and closed out the inning at a score of 6-4.

The top of the fifth inning was less productive for the Nittany Lions. Madison Seidel struck out swinging to start the inning, while the following hitters reached first base on multiple occasions. One through a walk, an error and a hit by pitch, but the Nittany Lions couldn’t seem to convert these opportunities into runs.

Klosowicz and Oneacre both fouled out, which left three runners stranded on base with no change in score as Rutgers began to bat for the bottom of the fifth.

In the bottom of the fifth, Rutgers could not respond. The Nittany Lions' defense remained strong, recording two strikeouts and a groundout to end the inning without allowing a run.

Penn State maintained its 6-4 lead heading into the 6th.

The sixth inning remained a struggle for both teams. In the top half, Penn State seemed to struggle to put up runs.

A key player in this game, Lieto singled to right field, the only hit of the inning, but the Nittany Lions were limited by Rutgers strong pitching and defensive plays. Strikeouts and double plays kept the Nittany Lions from adding to their lead, leaving two runners on base to end the top of the 6th.

Rutgers matched Penn State’s effort in the bottom of the sixth. Schuettler walked, followed by a single from Sam Rohwer that advanced her to second base, but a flyout to left field and a lineout to third kept Rutgers from scoring.

Both teams left two runners on base, keeping Penn State’s 6-4 lead going into the seventh inning.

In the top of the seventh, Penn State finally broke through. Hanik launched a solo home run down the left field line, extending the Nittany Lions' lead to 7-4.

The rest of the inning saw some quick outs, with Barbanente flying out to left, Klosowicz grounding out, and Oneacre flying out to center. This leaves the Nittany Lions with one run and one hit to end the top of the seventh.

Rutgers seemed to respond right away in the bottom half. Burtis singled through the right side, while pinch runner Hailey Stripling came in for her. After a fielder's choice at second put stripling out, Osborne doubled to the outfield with pinch runner, Eva Garofalo, who scored to cut Penn State’s lead. Rachel Millan walked, and a fielder's choice and a final out ended the game, securing the 7-5 victory for Penn State.

Up next

Penn State returns to the Commonwealth with a one-game clash against Pittsburgh on Tuesday on the Panthers' home turf. First pitch for the clash of instate rivals is set for 6 p.m.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: No. 21 Penn State women's lacrosse suffers narrow road loss to No. 3 Maryland, extending losing streak
author: Annie Alberts | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State had a close loss to Maryland on Sunday afternoon. Its record fell to 9-3, although they fought hard for the win.

The final score of the game was only decided by one, as the Terrapins defended their home turf with a 14-13 win.

The Nittany Lions and Terrapins were neck-and-neck throughout the entire game. Each time Penn State would score, Maryland would shortly after and vice versa.

The first two goals were scored less than a minute apart from each other. Maryland scored first, followed by Erika Ho’s 15th goal of the season.

Although Maryland is ranked No. 3 in the nation with an undefeated record of 12-0, No. 21 Penn State kept a similar energy and aggressiveness in its game.

Penn State has been going through a rough patch recently, losing two games in a row to ranked teams.

Part of the reason why the blue and white were able to stick it out was due to its goalies, Sydney Manning and Makenna Gagliardi.

Today was Gagliardi’s first collegiate lacrosse game. Combined with Manning, they had 14 saves after Maryland had 28 shots on goal. Gagliardi was brought into the game at the end of the second period after Manning had a penalty called on her.

Maryland’s biggest strength during this game was its defense. They were making back checks left and right, which resulted in Penn State players tripping and making errors.

Because of how strong their defense was, Penn State didn’t see the other side of the field as often as Maryland did. Its shots and shots on goal were significantly higher than Penn State’s, tallying up to 39 and 28.

Maryland also led the game in draw controls. It had 9 more than Penn State, which led it to have more opportunities on offense than the Nittany Lions.

However, Penn State kept up with Maryland consistently.

Freshman Rocquette Allen stood out amongst her teammates, scoring five goals. Allen has racked up a total of 30 goals this season. Allen hasn’t scored five goals since Penn State’s victory against Bucknell. In addition to that, the most goals Allen has scored in a conference game before today was one.

University of Florida transfer, Delaney Radin, was remarkable as well. She scored one goal and notched two assists. Radin leads the Big Ten with the most assists and is eighteenth in the NCAA for most points.

Today was a big day for Penn State’s freshman class, thanks to Allen and first-time goalie Gagliardi.

Up next

Penn State continues its road trip and Big Ten games with a clash against Ohio State next week. The Sunday clash is set for a noon start time.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball gets shut out by Illinois, drops conference series
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State entered Game 3 of its weekend series against Illinois coming off a 16-5 loss and a split series.

Ultimately, the Nittany Lions added to their loss column after a 6-0 defeat.

The first inning was effective for the blue and white. Freshman Isaiah Shayter started on the mound for the Nittany Lions. After a walk and a strikeout, Joey DeMucci threw Michael Farina out on a stolen base attempt to second to end the inning.

“He’s a weapon back there,” coach Mike Gambino said. “Completely shutting down the running game.”

Aidan Flinn started on the mound for the Fighting Illini and didn't allow any Nittany Lions to get on base.

Shayter returned to the mound for the second inning, walking AJ Putty for the first at-bat. After Jennings and Schuppman were both flied out, Will Johannes hit his third double of the weekend for Illinois, sending Putty home to put the Fighting Illini up 1-0.

Flinn held Penn State scoreless at the bottom of the second. While the freshman walked Molinaro, he struck out Maddox McDonald to end the inning unscathed.

While Farina got on base after an infield single, no others followed and Penn State started off the third inning with no runs allowed. Illinois replicated Penn State’s inning, striking out DeMucci and forcing groundouts out to both Yaucher and Mercado, to hold the game standstill at 1-0.

Putty was first at bat for Illinois, splitting the field with a ground ball to center field to get on base. This momentum didn’t last, however, after Jennings was flied out and Davis led a double play to end the inning.

“(Davis)’s worked really hard on his footwork around the bag and made a little adjustment on his arm strength,” Gambino said.

Despite Penn State getting players on all bases, after an Anderson and Porter single, Illinois converted a double play to end the inning, and went into the fifth inning still up 1-0.

The fifth inning was a bunting game for Illinois. After Johannes singled to shortstop, Brayden Mazzacano’s bunt allowed Johannes to stay at third. Nelson followed with a bunt of his own, sending Johannes home for Illinois’ second run. The Fighting Illini brought in another run after a sacrifice fly.

Reed Gannon had a successful start to his relief duties, striking out McDonald and setting up two ground balls to keep Penn State scoreless in the fifth inning.

Isaiah Shayter remained on the mound for the blue and white, but after one strikeout, Penn State subbed in Dimond Loosli to take over for Shayter, with the starter totaling 79 pitches on the day.

DeMucci ended the inning by throwing out Schupmann’s steal attempt at second, for the second time of the game, showcasing his defensive capabilities behind the plate.

In an attempt to close the margin in the latter part of the sixth, Anderson followed up Mercado’s groundout with a hard-hit ground ball down the third base line for a double. While he was in scoring position, Anderson didn’t score and Penn State was still shut out come the seventh inning, despite the scoring opportunities.

Illinois added to its run tally after a Nelson double and a throwing error by Molinaro. Groves joined shortly after with a single of his own, sending Nelson home for the Fighting Illini’s fifth run.

Penn State sent in freshman Robert Brown III to take over the mound with one out. The blue and white escaped the inning, holding Illinois from there, as Brown shut the door. The Fighting Illini led 5-0.

In the bottom of the seventh, Molinaro’s base hit reached second after a Farina fielding error. Molinaro’s base running remained solo as Davis and DeMucci struck out and McDonald flew out, keeping the blue and white off the board.

At the top of the eighth, Penn State opted to switch its pitcher for the third time, sending sophomore Matthew VanOstenbridge in to take over the mound.

The left-handed pitcher walked both Johannes and Mezzacano. Nelson slapped a ball past second base to center field, sending Johannes home for Illinois’ sixth run. Following the energy spurt for the Fighting Illini, VanOstenbridge closed out the inning, striking out Groves, with the score 6-0.

“The runs that we’re giving up are linked to walks,” Gambino said. “It’s really hard to give up outs when you’re down six.”

Penn State failed to respond for the eighth and ninth innings, after a quick three outs from Illinois each time. The blue and white became Illinois’ second shutout of the year.

“We’re not executing at the level we want,” Gambino said.

Up Next

Penn State will stay home for a matchup against UCF at 5:30 p.m. on Wednesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘He plays confident’ | Penn State OL Malachi Goodman imposing his will early in spring ball
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
When offensive line coach Ryan Clanton lined up at practice opposite of his position room, he was met with a driving force.

That force, on Thursday morning inside Holuba Hall, would be offensive tackle Malachi Goodman, who “punched” Clanton in the chest. Clanton said the blow “didn’t feel good.”

“He put his hand through my heart today. I probably bruised," Clanton said after practice. “He can glide. He's long, he's aggressive and he's smart, and he does a good job of retaining information and then putting it out there on the field. He plays confident. You forget how young he is.”

Goodman entered 2025 as a highly touted recruit, joining Penn State for his freshman year as a 5-star prospect via 247Sports. A documentary on Paramount+ called “5-STAR” followed him during Year 1, but Goodman didn’t see the field in a crowded offensive line room.

Part of the learning curve for him was one that many players face in their first season — simply getting acclimated to playing college ball.

“The speed of the game, transitioning from high school to college,” Goodman said of what was a challenge. “I picked up the playbook pretty fine last year. I would say that the speed of the game really, how fast we were playing, how fast I can pick up the plays and go full speed while I'm playing.”

While the Bloomfield, New Jersey, native is finding his rhythm, he knows there are still ways to go.

“I'm still young. I'm still 18 years old, so I definitely still need to grow up more,” Goodman said. “When I first came in, I thought the same. And I still think that now. I still need to get better. Get better as a player, a person, everything.”

Having spent some time in the program now, opting to stick around following the mass turnover this offseason, Goodman is making his name known one week into his first spring practice after being a summer enrollee a year ago.

At the media viewing session of practice on Thursday, he was taking the first-team reps at left tackle. Clanton cited improvement from a year ago with the changes he’s made to his physique as an aspect that’s stood out so far.

“He's done a great job transforming his body,” Clanton said. “When you look at him, he's 330 pounds, and he lowered his body fat, so he gained solid muscle. He's done a great job.”

When Goodman arrived last summer, he said he was around the 315-320 pound range. He feels that he can certainly put more weight on, but the most important trait for him as he adjusts his frame is to maintain his athleticism.

“I could get bigger, but to be honest, (the weight increase) kind of snuck up on me,” Goodman said. “I saw it going up, and I'm cool with it, as long as I'm still able to move. I could gain weight easily and I could lose weight easily. My weight fluctuates, and especially when we start playing football, I'll probably be able to drop, cut all that in a game.”

There’s still a long way to go until the season starts, but if Goodman can live up to the pedigree he had coming out of Paramus Catholic High School, the Penn State offensive line can see a favorable return.

In the meantime, Goodman is focused on keeping his head down and grinding.

“I'm working every day. I'm trying to get better every day,” Goodman said.

title: ‘This is impacting all of us’ | Thousands in State College participate in third No Kings protest
author: Jacob Godshall I The Daily Collegian
description:
Thousands of community members and protesters took to Old Main Saturday afternoon to participate in the third wave of “No Kings” protests, many wearing costumes and waving elaborate signs.

The No Kings Day of Defiance is a national protest that aims to root out authoritarianism and unite people across the country against “chaos, corruption and authority,” according to the No Kings website. As protesters gathered on Old Main’s lawn, representatives from Keystone Indivisible gave short speeches and lead chants of “Hey hey, ho ho, Donald Trump has got to go,” and “Say it once, say it twice, we will not put up with ICE.”

Thomas Pelton, one of the protesters, said if people are able to get out to events like this, they should. He said people can’t be afraid to get out and protest for something they believe in.

“I just wish that everybody that feels patriotic would go out and make the right choice and help to get our country and the world back on track,” Pelton said. “We're not setting a good example like we've done for most of America's life. We've been someone (who is) there to help other countries, and now because of who's in office, he's making them and us enemies.”

A similar sentiment was shared by many of the attendees, as dozens of signs referenced the current war in Iran, displaying slogans like “Empathy not empire,” and “U.S. out of Iran now.”

Jane Pierzga, community member, came to protest the U.S.’s involvement in Iran, especially as Trump did not attain congressional approval for the war.

“We have people, tons of people out there, that are being killed and maimed for no apparent reason, no imminent threat,” Pierzga said. “We have our troops that have been killed and maimed for the same thing. I mean, it shows a startling lack of respect for human life worldwide, and also for our troops, to so callously play with their lives.”

Pierzga hopes the next steps include impeaching Trump and Democrats regaining political power in November’s midterm elections. Pierzga said the midterms are the first step in “chipping away” at the Trump administration.

Edison Pellumbi said like the protest itself, his reasons for attending are “very broad.” He also said he could see people with different views coming together to unite for a purpose.

“I think a lot of the time, political discourse between liberals and leftists can get complicated … so they don't cooperate at all when we're fighting the same cause,” Pellumbi, a first-year studying broadcast journalism, said. “At the end of the day, the authoritarianism in the government is what we're fighting, so I think it's good that people who might have different views on certain issues can still come together and find common ground here.”

On the outskirts of the protest, several tables were set up with representatives from different organizations, like the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and Prairie Fire Publishing, which publishes progressive and revolutionary literature. Activities like face paint and a photo booth were also offered at activity tables.

Terri Angeletti and Kristy Ganoe, two representatives from ACLU, spent their time teaching people about their rights.

“The ACLU defends the Constitution, and we're here to make sure people know," Angeletti said. "We want to make sure people know what their rights are, so we have some information about your rights. And we're here because we're protesting, because a lot of things that are happening are very unconstitutional.”

Angeletti said that if people in the U.S. don’t have the Constitution, “we don’t have anything.”

“We've built this democratic society over the past 250 years,” Ganoe said. “It was a lot of work, and it's not perfect, but it's kind of nice, and I'd like to continue to live in a democratic society. We're just living through one of those periods where we all have to stand up and affirm that that is what we want.”

The turnout and diversity of the protest speaks volumes, Ganoe said.

“The breadth of the types of people that you see here just shows the fact that different kinds of people are concerned about their civil rights and our society,” Ganoe said. “It's not like this is a problem only impacting people who are undocumented immigrants; this is impacting all of us.”

After about an hour of protesting, leaders from Keystone Indivisible led attendees up to Pollock Road, and the group marched through campus to North Atherton Street, before leading them down the sidewalks of College Avenue.

As they marched, the group was met almost constantly with honks of approval from passing cars, which spurred on even more chants and songs. One attendee brought a conch shell, and was blowing into it to make a loud horn sound.

The group eventually reached the Allen Street gates, where the protest officially ended. Despite the official end, a large group stayed at the gates to continue chanting, while others returned to Old Main to chant.

Mitchell Ballass attended with a sign displaying pictures of his father, a World War II veteran. He said he hoped to use history to draw connections to what is happening today, as democracies around the world are in danger of history repeating itself.

His sign included images of his father’s company and an image of his father’s “gunner window,” an opening in a military aircraft from which a soldier fired. Visible in the photo was a plane flying over a battle, and the black marks of smoke from gunfire and other planes.

“It's the same damn thing,” Ballass said. “The dictatorship is unbelievable. If you listen to history, I mean, Stalin, the Russians, helped create the Third Reich … and Vladimir Putin's using the same playbook that Stalin did, so we're facing the same evil.”

title: No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse stays perfect in Big Ten play, beats No. 6 Ohio State on the road
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 9 Penn State traveled to Columbus, Ohio, at 4 p.m. on Saturday to face No. 6 Ohio State in Week 2 of Big Ten action.

The Nittany Lions’ strong first half propelled them to a 13-6 victory over the Buckeyes. Penn State’s record improved to 6-3 overall and 2-0 in the Big Ten, currently 4-1 against ranked opponents this season.

First half

The Nittany Lions opened the game with early momentum, forcing a turnover on the first possession before Michael Faraone put Penn State on the scoreboard with his team’s first shot of the game. After goalie Preston Hawkins made a save, Liam Matthews scored a behind-the-back goal while falling in front of Caleb Fyock’s crease to take a 2-0 lead.

Hawkins made 10 saves on 16 shots for a .625 save percentage in the victory, carrying momentum from his breakout performance against Maryland.

Penn State continued its dominance on both sides of the ball, causing turnovers leading to a 4-0 lead before Ohio State took its first timeout. First, John Jude Considine fired a shot from close range with 7:39 remaining in the quarter before Jack Iannantuono struck the back of the net on a diving shot.

The Buckeyes cut Penn State’s lead to three, capitalizing as the Nittany Lions attempted a 10-man ride. With Hawkins out of the crease, defenseman Kyle Foster launched a shot from Ohio State’s end into the open goal.

Matthews answered immediately, assisted by Luke Walstrum. Walstrum’s pass found the Orangeville, Ontario, native at the top of the box and his side-arm shot didn’t miss.

At the two-minute mark, the Buckeyes scored their first offensive goal of the game. Alex Marinier caught Liam White on the left side, who beat Hawkins’ stick to set the score, 5-2, heading into the second quarter.

Marinier started the second quarter on the right foot, scoring less than a minute into play. His shot from mid-range trickled past Hawkins, who got a piece of the ball but wasn’t able to make the save.

However, Chase Robertson responded with a solo effort from the top of the box to reclaim the three-goal lead. He dodged past a pair of Buckeye defenders and beat Fyock just under the crossbar.

Lucca DiBartolomeo played a key role in Iannantuono’s second goal of the game, forcing a turnover with a heavy stick check in Ohio State territory after a failed transition attempt. On the following possession, Iannantuono ripped a shot from the left side past Fyock to take a 7-3 lead with 9:50 left in the half.

Ohio State’s defense settled in with Fyock making big saves, allowing for transition opportunities on Penn State’s end. However, the Buckeyes repeatedly made errors in clearing or going offside. Dominic Shaw took a cross-checking penalty, giving Kyle Lehman the space to score on the man-up for an 8-3 lead at halftime.

Second half

Garrett Haas scored the first goal of the second half, rounding the crease before bouncing the ball past Hawkins less than two minutes into the third quarter. Hawkins, however, responded with consecutive saves on dangerous chances on the doorstep as Penn State struggled to clear.

Then, the fireworks began. First, Mark Watters shot through heavy coverage to take a 9-4 lead at the eight-minute mark before Jake Cohen answered 21 seconds later.

On the following faceoff, Jon King cut to the net through traffic and was stripped of the ball. But Fyock was unable to scoop the rolling ball that dribbled past the goal line.

With 5:01 left in the third quarter, Faraone scored his second of the game on the right wing with a heavy side-arm shot that nearly clipped Fyock’s stick.

Robertson carried the momentum in the dying moments of the quarter, giving the Nittany Lions a 12-5 lead going into the final frame. Hawkins maintained that score, making a series of desperation saves with time winding down to stop the Buckeyes’ advance.

To start the fourth quarter, Ohio State elected to replace Fyock with junior Jack Allen after the starter’s seven saves on 19 shots. Allen made three saves on four shots in one quarter of action.

Walstrum struck first, scoring a wraparound with a defender’s stick lodged under his chinstrap. Immediately afterwards, Jack Oldman ran down the center of the box, beating Hawkins high to respond.

Scoring noticeably dropped off for the remainder of the game as both teams placed lock-down defense, forcing shot-clock violations despite multiple penalties. One last save from Hawkins iced the game, and Penn State moved to 2-0 in the Big Ten for the first time since 2019.

Injury Report

Attackman Hunter Aquino and midfielder Patrick Carragher weren’t dressed in the line-up.

Up Next

Penn State will travel to Baltimore, Maryland, to face Johns Hopkins at noon on Saturday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball outside hitters make big impact in NJIT sweep
author: Keira Albert | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 17 Penn State’s outside hitters, Sean Harvey and Matthew Luoma, played a massive game against No. 19 NJIT on Saturday, showing up big throughout the match to help take home another win for the blue and white.

The blue and white completed its sweep of the Highlanders after Saturday’s match. The 3-0 win advanced the Nittany Lions to 14-7 on the season and marked their third straight win.

As just a redshirt sophomore, Harvey has become a key piece to the Nittany Lions’ attack. And while the entire blue and white squad played a good game on Saturday, Harvey was a standout.

The first set was a battle for both sides, with the game staying tied for the majority of play. But Harvey was a big part of the 26-24 win.

With five kills and one service ace in the first, the Bartlett, Illinois, native brought high energy to the court, ultimately helping the Nittany Lions to get a tough-earned first set victory.

“Sean is a monster in the making,” coach Mark Pavlik said. “He’s going to be one of the best big hitters in the country in the next couple of years.”

Luoma was just as impactful that first set. With five kills of his own, and one block assist, the Nittany Lions would’ve seen a very different game had he not been on the court.

“Lou’s a guy who’s been there, done that for us, certainly last year,” Pavlik said. “He doesn’t look like he’s dropping off any.”

And redshirt junior definitely didn’t drop off in the second set. If anything, he brought more heat. With two service aces, which were back-to-back, he scored some big points to further the blue and white’s lead.

The Phoenix, Arizona, native also produced one kill in the second. Right there with him was Harvey, who also tallied two more kills.

The duo, along with the valiant effort from the rest of the Nittany Lions, gave them the dominant 25-12 win in the second set.

The third set saw similar action from the pair.

In the first two points for the blue and white, the dynamic duo, along with teammate Owen Rose, put up back-to-back block assists.

The pair got multiple kills each, as well as a few digs for Harvey and more blocks for Luoma in the third and final set of the match, yet again being major parts of the 25-18 win.

But beyond their point-scoring, the two were loud and demonstrated leadership and perseverance on Saturday.

By the end of the match, Luoma tallied a total of 13 points, with nine kills, four blocks and two service aces.

Harvey logged 12 points, with nine kills, four blocks, one service ace and four digs.

The two outside hitters finished the game as the first and second leaders in points for the Penn State squad, proving themselves to be key aspects of the blue and white’s victory over the Highlanders.

“Both had to battle through some recovery and some injury in the fall,” Pavlik said. “We were hoping they would be where they are now back in January, early February. I think we’re just starting to see them kind of get their legs, so to speak, and figure some things out.”

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball earns win over Rutgers behind late heroics from Britton, Gradishar
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State took to Piscataway for its 11th road game of the season, with a completely split record away from Happy Valley before first pitch.

Ultimately, in a back-and-forth offensive battle, Penn State seized the opportunity with a 7-6 win over Rutgers behind a masterful performance down the wire.

The blue and white started like a microwave in the batter’s box; it heated the basepaths in the first frame with a triad of hits, including a solo home run by second baseman Michela Barbanente.

Yet while the Nittany Lions plated a pair of runs, the Scarlet Knights were able to plate their own courtesy of a much less emphatic approach at the plate, as Rutgers scored courtesy of a pair of drawn walks and a sacrifice fly.

The blue and white's bombastic start to the game continued with a pair of leadoff hits in the second. Yet Rutgers opted for a change in the circle, and Brooke Shifflett ended the scare, replacing starter Kelsey Hoekstra.

Penn State’s own early arm, starter Abigail Britton, began to settle things in the home half, but the offense began to cool in the third.

On the contrary, Rutgers began to find its stride. A leadoff single, which culminated in a one-out steal, put Penn State into dicey waters.

The Scarlet Knights’ Hwang only further complicated things when she roped a ball to center field, which grazed the top of Natalie Lieto’s glove, and bounced down for an RBI double.

That tied things at two, and ended Britton’s day, yet relief arm McKenna Young couldn’t solve things. Rutgers followed with another double off the new righty, and then a blooper to the first baseman, Gabby Gradishar, resulted in an even bigger blooper for Penn State.

The freshman gunned it home on a ball that would have just been an easy tap on the base. Hwang was safe at the plate, and so was the runner on first.

Things only avalanched from there, the bases eventually loaded on a walk, and a sacrifice fly scored a pair after the throw from Lieto bounced off third baseman Brooke Klosowicz’s chest.

Penn State’s suffering slowed, and a 5-2 deficit seemed insurmountable for morale early. Rutgers opted to turn to Kassandra Gewecke inside the circle to make that goal difficult for the blue and white, but, luckily for Penn State, it only needed one swing.

Two runners got on with one out courtesy of a quick single and walk, then stepped up Klosowicz, who got all over Gewecke’s third pitch of the at-bat to tie things up with a bomb to left field.

Young quieted things, and after Penn State speedster right fielder Frankee Flesher got on base to start the fifth via a hit-by-pitch, the Nittany Lions opted for small ball measures to pull ahead.

Catcher Madison Seidel laid out a bunt, and Gradishar singled to place Penn State with a narrow 6-5 lead. Britton reentered the circle to finish out the fifth.

However, just as the blue and white tied things up just with a swing, so did the Scarlet Knights’ Baileigh Burtis with a high-arching dinger that stayed just inside the left field foul pole. Britton’s struggles continued with a pair of walks.

The LaVale, Maryland, native shook off the heat from Rutgers’ offense, silencing the inning with three straight punch outs.

The offense started flat with a pair of defensive outs, but the Nittany Lions found a late hero in the form of Gradishar, who earned the go-ahead run courtesy of a towering home run.

Britton got the call for Penn State to close out the game and earn the save entering the seventh frame.

The sophomore allowed a one-pitch single, but navigated out of the last inning with ease behind her defense and her sixth strikeout of the day.

With the result, the blue and white improve to 22-11 on the season, and 3-5 within Big Ten play.

Up Next

Penn State’s series finale with Rutgers takes place at 1 p.m. Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State earns first 2027 commit in CB Semajay Robinson
author: Lyle Alenstein & Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State finally has a player on board from the 2027 class.

Cornerback Semajay Robinson has committed to Matt Campbell and the Nittany Lions, per Hayes Fawcett of On3.

Robinson was on campus both Friday and Saturday, per his X account. He's ranked as a 3-star and the No. 46 cornerback nationally, per 247Sports Composite.

The 6-foot-2.5, 180-pounder held offers from other schools such as Texas Tech, Florida State, Auburn, Missouri and Kentucky.

The Fort Lauderdale, Florida, native will play for three different high schools in four seasons, spending two years at Boyd Anderson, one at Palm Beach Central and has transferred to Dillard for his senior year.

Robinson is coming off of a 2025 junior season where he appeared in all 11 games, recording 17 total tackles and a pass breakup, per MaxPreps.

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball pitching struggles early, falls in mercy-rule loss to Illinois
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State suffered a series sweep to Illinois the last time the two squads met. However, it was a different start this season as the Nittany Lions kicked off their third Big Ten series with a 9-8 win over the Fighting Illini.

Despite earning 13 hits in the game, the blue and white weren't able to spark a comeback for the second-straight game. Penn State dropped the second game of the series in mercy-rule fashion, 16-5.

“Situational (hitting) is always critical. Two-strike (hitting) is always critical, and as you can see we like to have some stuff where we can run the bases and do that stuff,” coach Mike Gambino said. “Part of the problem offensively is when you’re down eight, it just gets a little harder to do some of that stuff.”

Ben Hudson got the start in Game 2 against the Fighting Illini. The Medford, New Jersey, native last pitched against Purdue, in which he managed to strike out three batters and only allowed one earned run.

Signs of the first game between the two teams showed early after Illinois started the game with four runs. Both AJ Putty and Kyle Schupmann singled in a run. Will Johannes followed the pair of singles with a double that scored two additional runs. Hudson managed to get out of the inning with a strikeout.

Down once again in the bottom of the first, the Penn State offense needed to mount another early comeback. The Nittany Lions had to wait for any sort of fight back after a one-out single from Michael Anderson led to an inning-ending double play from Jack Porter.

The Fighting Illini used the top half of the second to add a run to their lead. Jack Zebig hit a sacrifice fly out to center field, scoring J.R. Nelson.

The bottom half of the lineup produced the first two runs of the game for Penn State in the second. Avery Smith singled in Spencer Barnett, followed by a Maddox McDonald RBI single.

Illinois scored a run in its third-straight inning. A fastball from Hudson got away from Smith and scored Collin Jennings from third.

After a scoreless bottom third inning from the Nittany Lions, Frankie Sanchez was brought into the game in relief for Hudson. The Kendall Park, New Jersey, native earned two quick outs before giving up back-to-back home runs. A grand slam from Jennings, followed by a Schupmann solo shot, gave Illinois a commanding 11-2 lead in the fourth.

Sanchez was pulled following a quiet inning from Penn State. Ethan Bauerschmidt took his place. The freshman righty began his eighth outing, retiring the Fighting Illini in order.

Molinaro woke the scoring up for the Nittany Lions after he hit a three-run home run with two outs in the fifth. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native has eight RBIs in his last five games.

Illinois poured on five more runs in the sixth. After giving up a ground-rule double with bases loaded, Bauerschmidt was pulled for Skip Shenosky. The Lancaster, Pennsylvania, native immediately let up a double into left field, scoring two runs. The Nittany Lions finally got out of the inning with a groundout.

Penn State remained quiet on offense after Illinois pulled starter Regan Hall for Oliver Martel. Being down 16-5 after the sixth inning, the Nittany Lions were three outs away from a mercy-rule loss.

The Nittany Lions kept the Fighting Illini off the scoreboard in the top half of the seventh, only needing two runs to stay in the game.

Following McDonald's walk, Justin Turcovski flew out to end the game.

“The message tomorrow is exactly what the message was today, we’re going full reset, full send each day,” Gambino said. “Regardless if we’re trying to win a series or whatever it is, we learn our lesson. Tomorrow we’re back with the same energy we had today.”

Up Next

Penn State concludes its conference series against Illinois tomorrow at 11 a.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball dominates, sweeps NJIT in back-to-back matches
author: Liana Tiamzon | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State entered the match with a chance to reaffirm its spot at the top of the conference, while NJIT arrived with revenge to prove that it can compete and win against the Nittany Lions.

Following their game Friday night, the Nittany Lions returned to Rec Hall on Saturday night for a second key EIVA matchup against NJIT, a team that proved last season it can challenge the Nittany Lions.

The blue and white began hot, with an ace from Michael Schwob, getting the first point on the board. The front line showed up strong for Penn State, with Matthew Luoma, Owen Rose and Bennett Wilson creating a wall to block an NJIT attack.

Rose’s block game showed up again, as he holds the top spot in the NCAA for blocks per set.

However, the Highlanders were bringing their A game, as the first set remained tight with a tough swing. Early on, Luoma led the way with three kills, setting the tone for the Nittany Lions, but NJIT showed how committed to staying competitive, as Wiktor Nowak led the team with four kills, continuing to challenge the Nittany Lions

Still in the first set, the Nittany Lions used their first timeout of the set, with a neck-and-neck 23-23 score. NJIT brought it again, making the set 24-24. Gustavo Chrcanovic stepped back with a chance to take the advantage, but sent it out of bounds. On the other end, Wilson dropped in an ace to end the set, 26-24.

The blue and white persisted with a chance at another sweep, following Friday’s 3-0 victory against the Highlanders. Saturday’s match showed a similar mentality, as the Nittany Lions brought the same energy.

Switching sides of the court, NJIT started off the match with a serve, but Penn State still resisted and earned the first point of the second set. Luoma served an ace as the Nittany Lions started to pull away in the second set, leading by five over the Highlanders.

NJIT libero Diego Lopez couldn’t keep up with the Nittany Lions and showed his lack of digs in the second set. Penn State was playing a completely different set than the first one, showing all its dominance by creating a larger gap in the score.

Wilson powered through with his bullet serves, resulting in the Nittany Lions winning their second set, 25-12.

While Penn State has historically controlled the court, last season was a different story. NJIT earned a statement win in the regular season, sweeping Penn State in straight sets, which marked one of the Highlanders’ most notable victories over the program.

The Highlanders and Nittany Lions met last season in the EIVA Tournament semifinal, where the Nittany Lions delivered their strongest performance of the series, sweeping NJIT 3-0 to advance. It’s looking the same today with this game against the Highlanders.

Penn State’s ability to maintain offensive efficiency and limit NJIT’s momentum likely determined the outcome. For NJIT, a slowed pace and capitalizing on crucial moments would be essential in attempting to pull off an upset and a reverse sweep.

The Nittany Lions started off the third set with an ace by Schwob and a block assist by Sean Harvey, Rose and Luoma. Penn State showed to keep the same energy from the last set, and created a three-point gap with NJIT.

The Highlanders showed that the last set did not deter them, though, as they tied up the set and closed that gap that Penn State was so eager to show behind dominant attacks from Wilson and Rose.

For the first time in the game, Penn State challenged a touch on the net and changed the score to 14-11.

Harvey and Luoma showed their dominance on the court and led with the highest amount of kills, with nine and eight, respectively. Wilson with five on the board.

NJIT used its last timeout of the set after a serve by Gabriel and a triple block from Schwob, Rose and Luoma rejected a swing once again. The Highlanders made one final attempt to creep back up and close the big gap made by the Nittany Lions. But, the Highlanders couldn’t succeed.

The last set ended with NJIT’s Andre Aleixo sending a serve into the net. The Nittany Lions closed off the last set 25-18, sweeping NJIT for the second straight day.

Up Next

Penn State will travel to Charleston, West Virginia, for a two-match battle with Charleston. The first match is set for 7 p.m. Friday with the teams returning to the court at 3 p.m. Saturday in Wehrle Innovation Center.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE
